title: Interesting but Looooooooooong
text: I love the story/plot & the look & feel of the movie but i really wish directors would cut scenes that do NOT move the plot & characters forward. Dump the leg in park scene & the german scene. No one but the director would miss them. Dang, he could have really tightened up the movie & made it GREAT!
author: maltesefalcon

title: Just not good
text: I have Brazilian connections and am fairly familiar with Brazilian film and television. . Wagner Moura's association with this film made me less of a fan, to be honest. Just poorly portrayed and trying too hard. Just because you can because you're a big name doesn't mean you should!The film was long, boring and didn't start getting even a little interesting until an hour in. Created so many questions that it didn't answer. What happened to his mother and why was he obsessed with it? How did the wife really die? They acted like Moura's character was the titled secret agent but he really wasn't? Seemed like the lady interviewing him in the cinema was really the secret agent but they hardly focused on her? So confusing. It also wasn't clear at all why the rich guy hated him so much.And then the time jumps smh. And the sudden anticlimactic revelation of the main character's death doh. Awful. I gave it 3 stars for Dona Sebastiana's character, she carried the entire film. I can't believe this won an award, truly.
author: Heiura

title: Realistic
text: If this has something to do with a secret agent, I am unaware what it is. That minor mystery aside, this is a fine film. A seemingly very authentic look at Brazil during it's violent and lawless 1970's era. The focal point of this is a man subtly and very well acted by Wagner Moura. He spends much of the movie on the run and in hiding from the unlawful law, among others, to save his life simply due to getting on the wrong side of wrong people. The film is really less about this man and more about the condition of this country at that time which is brutally interesting with a harsh and cold ending. The film is too long as the early part is slow, but the acting by everyone is as authentic as it could be making me wonder how many of these people are professional actors. Tânia Maria is a special delight. Tense stuff.

author: LucasP-891

title: It feels so real.
text: There's a certain thrill involved with the uncovering of history. You see it in a few non-fiction books, ones that contain narratives of their own, interlaced with descriptions of the author finding this material - making connections, peeling back the layers of dirt, debris and forgotten pain. David Grann's works are exceptional at this.The thrill is then, of course, subsumed by a deep heartache for the horrors of the past, for the people that were more than just stories, the people that lived through it all.True Detective channeled all this through fiction in its somber first season, and now The Secret Agent follows suit, its short interspersed modern-day framework keeping the past story grounded, and impactful.It's all so real - flawlessly presented as the 70s. Not just the era-accurate clothing, cars, and sensibilities, the actual presentation is reminiscent of older craftsmanship: slower paced editing, a love for screen wipes and dual-screens, a focus on minutiae, nothing feels fake.It can feel aimless at times, but it uses bouts of exposition to lock what we've just seen into place. It's as if we're piecing together scraps of the past ourselves. Things that matter, the things around it; exhume anything you can, interpret how you will, make do with what you've got.This is fragmented memories manifested, brought to vivid life and presented for examination. Generational trauma and the environment that spawned it, in non-linear high definition.I finished watching it days ago and it still echoes. It still echoes.
author: H4wke

title: Jaws: The Revenge?
text: The real secret is...what was this all about?I just finished this nearly 3-hour film and I barely know more than when I started. Some movies are subtle; this one won't tell you anything. I think homework is needed following.Just trying to be a Best Picture completist here, though I have ZERO plans to see F1 - how was that even freaking nominated for Best Pic? I mean, I guess I cannot comment since I haven't seen it, but along with this, Hamnet, Sinners and Bugonia, what was the Academy thinking?Sure, this is wildly original. Unique, and well-acted and shot. But Best Picture? I could name 10 movies to take this spot.Okay, here goes. I guess a man who hates another man wants to kill him with a hammer also wants to take his son from the in-laws. Meanwhile, there's a hit out on him for reasons.Some movies dive WAY TOO MUCH into exposition to explain everything to a weaker audience. If you hate that, good news! There is absolutely zero exposition here. Your homework, as mentioned, is to figure out everything, and I mean EVERYTHING, including the entire plot/motivations, for yourself. Honestly, I hope you did because I'm still wondering.While this was an exotic deep-dive into 1970s Brazil, which I really did enjoy, I'm still puzzled what this is all about. I keep expecting a Cinema Snob to spend 15-minutes mansplaining the entire outline to me. And I would welcome it. I'd love to understand what I just spent nearly 3-hours on and $9.99 to rent since it was nominated for Best Pic.Recommended for those deep into Brazil's 1970s culture.***Final Thoughts: Despite the above, I never hated ANY of this experience. I just got Lost in Translation and maybe the history. I still don't even know if this was a true story or not since I don't do any research prior to writing my reviews. Heck, I don't even know how he's a "Secret Agent" other than his Alternative Name. But I loved the movie-references.
author: thesar-2

title: Amazing Characters, Photography and Pace!
text: This film portrays a specific place and moment in Brazil with a beautiful mix of humor and melancholy. The characters are remarkable and the cinematography is stunning. Although some of the actors feel a bit inexperienced, the director somehow manages to make them unique and enjoyable in their own way.The pacing is also brilliant. It starts very slowly, gradually builds up, and reaches its peak at just the right moment, finishing calmly but still in a very engaging way.Loved the movie!
author: guibiro

title: A backwards return to form for Brazilian cinema. A rug-pulling experience.
text: Brazilian filmmakers hate the military dictatorship period (1964-1985) so much that, for decades, they could not stop making films about it. The reality is that Brazilians starved for decades for actually entertaining local productions. These political films reflect a tiny, self-serving artistic class that fought the dictatorship, while ironically having their own movies subsidized by the defunct government movie agency EMBRAFILME.Then comes Kleber, the director of "O Agente Secreto". He was only 11 years old when the 1979 Amnesty Law effectively ended the dictatorship; he never truly lived through it. It is not his story, nor is it Wagner Moura's (the lead actor and producer). Unlike the older political movies which, however flawed, often told real stories of real people, here we have a modern progressive fairytale. It injects contemporary themes and types of victimhood into an anachronistic setting. It is a virtue-signaling movie for a paranoid artistic upper class that still cultivates the fear of a new military coup. It's been 40 years, come on.Regarding its artistic merits, the movie looks gorgeous, and the reconstruction of the 1970s Northeast is CGI-accurate. Some of the acting is flawed because they chose to cast non-actors for minor parts. Though well-coached, they remain a distraction for native viewers. The film is paced too slowly, and many segments are irrelevant to a story that only maintains interest by revealing too little (if any) of itself over long periods. The first hour is entirely wasted. Moura's acting is merely serviceable.The overall feeling of the film is that of a polished 2020s production executed by a bunch of pretentious and overconfident amateurs, much like the old Brazilian filmmakers were.Watch out for the "tape girls" duo. The true stars of this movie.
author: BIASED4215

title: A film masterpiece
text: I was grabbed by the acting, pacing and exquisite period detail. No way is it too long, as some reviewers suggest, the film sustains beautifully. I'd love to see it win in the international films category of the Oscars. Every character comes to life, and little things - like the cast on the young football player's arm - give the film an almost documentary feel. From the first scene, which is an exercise in the bizarre, to the last, which leaves the action somewhat unresolved, this is a film with integrity.
author: julianjoyce

title: It's not what you think, the awards hyped it up
text: The Secret Agent is one of those films that arrives in theaters surrounded by awards buzz, which is exactly why I decided to check it out. With all the talk about it being in the Best Picture conversation, curiosity alone made it worth the trip to see what the hype was about. On the surface, the film sets itself up as a serious foreign drama with a layered story slowly unfolding.Like many international films, the language barrier can make the viewing experience a little harder to navigate. Much of the story requires reading subtitles, which can take attention away from the cinematography and performances. Still, for the first stretch of the film, you can piece together what's happening and begin to understand the direction of the narrative. The structure starts to form a story that feels like it's building toward something meaningful.For a while, the film seems to be heading in a serious and dramatic direction. The tone becomes increasingly heavy, and the story begins sliding into darker territory as events unravel. Characters face setbacks, and the overall arc leans into a bleak atmosphere that suggests the story is about to deliver a powerful emotional payoff.Then the film suddenly takes a bizarre turn that feels completely disconnected from everything that came before. The last portion introduces an absurd element involving a random animated foot jumping around and attacking people in the most ridiculous ways imaginable. Instead of deepening the story, the moment pulls the entire film into something that feels almost unintentionally comedic.This shift is what ultimately sinks the experience. The final hour becomes difficult to take seriously because the narrative loses the tone it spent so long trying to establish. What started as a slow-building drama suddenly feels chaotic and confusing, leaving the audience wondering how the film reached such an odd destination.By the end, The Secret Agent feels like a film that had potential but lost control of its own identity. While some viewers may appreciate the experimental storytelling, the final stretch turns the movie into something frustrating rather than meaningful. Considering the awards attention surrounding it, the result feels far more puzzling than impressive.
author: TheMovieSearch

title: Could have been a masterpiece
text: Good movie with flaws. Wagner Moura and most the bad guys on the movie are great. But other actors look and act like they got the part thanks to some relationship with the production team and not because of their acting skills. As the movie goes on there are some side stories that completely failed in my opinion. And one of them was ridiculous even though i got what they were trying to show. A big positive is the excellent recreation of that specific era. The clothing, the haircuts, everything was spot on. The last two bad things about it, i think it lacked suspense, and they also messed the end of the movie. author: Greek_77

title: Discombobulated
text: Put aside the terrific acting and the stereotypes you may hold about Brazil (partying at Carnivale, and corrupt rural police), this near-3 hour movie challenges you to make sense of why you are spending so much time watching it. The main character is a university researcher who basically angers an industrialist who then places a "hit" on him, something he finds out later, all of which is in the preview. But guess what, nobody is a secret agent. In truth, the researcher has no idea why he is on a hit list. And after so many hours, you have no idea either. Is upsetting some petro-guy all it takes to get killed in this country? The "action," what little there is, is pretty basic, sort of indie/art house stuff, even as the gritty life of living in a humid South American country comes through. There are VW Beetles galore, along with sweat and old-fashioned human labor...but little else. And by the end, you are scratching your head as to how this film won so many awards, as shown in the trailer. Don't be fooled. There are many much better films out there, despite what every capitulating critic is saying. Look at it this way, it's three hours of your life...how do you want to spend it?
author: MichaelM-0475

title: A time of "pirraça"
text: A portrait of a time of "pirraça" (mischief), a provocative Pernambucan expression used to describe the horrors of Brazil's military regime in 1977. More than mere stubbornness, "pirraça" came to embody a form of popular defiance against the paranoia, fear, and violence that haunted Brazilian society.The film unfolds as an intense chase thriller, revealing how many people at the time were forced to live double lives simply to survive under the dictatorship. Anchored by powerful, absorbing performances, it moves through an atmosphere that balances tension and humor, while vividly evoking the Recife of that era. There is also a faintly "supernatural" undertone that pushes the story beyond straightforward realism-most notably in the urban legend of the "Hairy Leg," which journalists reportedly used as a coded device to expose cases of police violence during the regime.A necessary work that honors and revisits Brazil's identity and history.A film about memory, or the lack of it...
author: joana-niza

title: Reminded me at times of Mulholland Drive - and that's a problem
text: Mulholland Drive I hear you scream? Yes. I confused myself on that one.But I can only attribute that to the disjointed and at times dreamlike nature of this movie. It jumps about from time and scene and is quite baffling a lot of the time. At one point it goes completely off the rails.Much like I wanted to decipher Mulholland Drive, I was chatting to AI after this to try and piece it together.There's so much I didn't get. So much that is deliberately vague. And that's problematic for me in really wanting to rave about this movie.Now there's LOT to love about it. Firstly the scene setting and direction. I don't know what Brazil was like in this era but I feel like I was there. Every scene crackles with colour and atmosphere, it's truly beautiful.The actors also magnificent. At times I felt like I was watching a documentary, and I mean that in the best ways. It just felt so real.But then it comes back to the baffling story, from its non-sequitur title to the end credits. End credits that show you photos then an actor and you understand as a viewer that you are typically seeing the the real person and then who portrayed them. Even this was a confusion... they're not real at all.It's as if the director is playing with us.There's lots to love. I wonder if this movie will benefit from a second watch. If I watch it again one day I may revise my rating up from 6/10. I'm intrigued... this one will sit with me for a while.But right now... the story is just too long and too unfocused and that prevents this from being better than it should be.
author: antony-1

title: Shadows of Power in Carnival Colors
text: The Secret Agent takes you to Brazil in the late 1970s with such detail that you can feel the heat, the music, and the dust of Recife. It doesn't have the big Hollywood effects; instead, it tells a slower, more realistic story about how everyday life is affected by the harsh legacy of a military dictatorship. Corruption runs through the whole movie, from small-time officials to powerful men who use violence and threats as normal business and political tools.I was impressed by how the story kept me interested without using cheap action cliches. The movie has a lot of funny, tense, and violent parts that come out of nowhere. It does this in a way that feels unsettling but believable for a society that is warped by fear and lack of punishment. Some people may think the movie is too long and the plot is too loose, but the payoff is a deep look at a country where dictatorship has become a part of everyday life and personal relationships.The Secret Agent is not a liberal sermon; it is a strong reminder that when the government makes corruption and violence normal, everyone suffers, even after the generals leave office.
author: DickVG

title: 6.9/10. Recommended.
text: I find this film somewhat overrated. I'm sure I wouldn't feel the same if I were Brazilian. From what I understand, the movie touches upon sensitive-and dark-aspects of Brazil over the last 50 years; it would have resonated with me much more if I had lived through that historical context or had more knowledge of it. In any case, although the film is elliptical, vague, and minimalistic, you don't need to be Brazilian to appreciate it.It is a good political drama, rather than a thriller. There aren't many moments of tension and suspense, but when they do appear, the film becomes very intense. It would have been even better if it were shorter; 161 minutes was excessive. Nevertheless, it was interesting, both as a time capsule and in terms of direction, cinematography, and acting. Wagner Moura was indeed very good, as was the rest of the cast.Overall, it is an uneven but quality film that manages to immerse you in the spirit of that era and make your skin crawl-and fill you with rage-at certain villainous characters and their actions. The ending was realistic, though not satisfying.Unfortunately, when dealing with real historical events rather than fiction, there aren't often satisfying conclusions. With that in mind, the ending was exactly what suited this film.

author: jobaybe

title: Another pretentious nothing
text: Horribly misleading title, at least an hour too long, insanely bad editing supposedly used deliberately to convey the meaninglessness of dictatorships and random human outcomes - it all makes for really miserable viewing. If you think this film is about secret agents, real or metaphorical, it isn't. If you think it's about dictatorship, Brazilian or any other, it most definitely isn't. If you think you will glean some deeper truth about social injustice, you won't. It's a disjointed, confusing, pretentious slog trying to be about the human condition in Brazil in the 1970s, but it just fails to convey anything meaningful or even interesting.I will try to paint you a picture that I think people are trying to use to explain away this simply bad film: it's as if Jaws was trying to depict the randomness and meaninglessness of a shark attack by having the film be two hours of watching a guy suntanning on a beach with little or no dialogue, then in the last minute of the film getting into the sea, killed by a shark, and the credits roll.
author: NixSar

title: What's the point?
text: I kept hoping the movie was going somewhere but in the end it went absolutely nowhere. I would have been better off cleaning my bathroom. How is this movie nominated for best picture? The acting was good and the characters were interesting but there were a lot of unanswered questions and unnecessary stories within the story.
author: mattkay-62692

title: Great idea. Messy storytelling.
text: I really wanted to love The Secret Agent, because the core concept is strong: political paranoia, erased truths, and how a system can destroy someone and then bury the story under noise and "official" narratives. On paper, that's the kind of cinema I respect.But the execution didn't work for me.The main issue is storytelling clarity. The film throws a lot of information at you through scenes that feel like exposition dumps, and instead of building tension, it often made me feel lost. I don't mind challenging movies-actually I like them-but here the confusion felt less like intentional mystery and more like the movie wasn't guiding the viewer through its own structure.The biggest example is the recurring focus on the shark and the severed leg. The film insists on it so much, but without giving enough internal context for why it matters. Maybe it's a metaphor, maybe it's cultural symbolism, maybe it's political commentary-but the movie didn't make that readable for me while watching. So instead of deepening the narrative, it became distracting and frustrating.That's why my final feeling is mixed: I respect what it's trying to say, and I can see the film's ambition, but I wish it communicated its message with more clarity and less "information fog."
author: haabouzid

title: A storage of cultural memory and a perspective on collective remembrance and societal change through and after the military dictatorship. All in an amazing thriller.
text: This movie is one of the most vibrant depictions of 1970's Brazilian culture and the violence during the military dictatorship. Every single character and interaction feels rich in ways sometimes hidden from those outside Brazilian culture.The way the story is slowly revealed after almost half the movie spent presenting the characters the environment and the looming threat is a great indicator of what this movie is about whole after the mid point the movie suddenly feels more real and grounded. You are slowly hit in the stomach with a story so raw it feels real. The way the ending is presented is equally unusual and unsettling in a way that will leave you thinking. It makes the story feel real and raw and is essential to one of the movies main themes: memory, how time slowly erases most of it, how things change, how Brazil got past the military dictatorship, but the scars remained.I've read many reviews saying the movie dragged on for too long, saying there were unnecessary scenes, complaining about the ending not being a fitting one for a thriller. I can understand how someone disconnected from Brazil and the Portuguese language, loosing much of the dialogues richness to translation and used to american movies that usually go straight to the point would say that. However: If you go into this movie knowing that it isn't just a thriller meant to have you glued to the screen waiting for the action (even though it will do that at times), knowing that it is a storage of collective cultural memory while also providing a perspective on how we dealt and deal with those memories and realities, you will love the movie.You will have the best experience if you go into the movie like a sponge, absorbing every small detail, from the way each character speaks, to how all the secondary characters interact with eachother and how each one seems to have a rich backstory, even if those stories are not fully revealed. Remember that the scenery for the movie os not a world crafted for the story, its 1970s Brazil, mostly 1970's Recife, its the real world, and it is not to be ignored. Its not an aesthetic backdrop, its the center of the story, one of the movies main missions is its characterization. If you feel like many scenes are unnecessary, that may be because you may only be interested in half of the movies premise.Let me just end this review by saying I found the movies perspective on a story that could very well be a real one to be extremely sensitive. The ending could have been extremely exciting, intense, graphic and shocking but instead it was a slap of reality and an unsettling perspective on the story, and how society healed and changed after it. The plot and the action are compelling without ever feeling embellished for the sake of the movie. The story is good enough to not need exaggeration and culturally the movie is deeply vibrant wothout feeling artificially oversaturated.
author: manelmagueijo

title: The film's plot is too slow
text: This concludes my list of all the films nominated for an Oscar last year, and I have to say right from the start that I think this film is worse than the others. It's good, but not like the others. The story is simply too slow and the film drags on because of some unnecessary scenes, such as the very beginning of the film. At certain moments, it was interesting to me, and at others, a bit boring. The characters are okay, nothing too special. Wagner Moura was good to me, but with all the nominees for best actor, I don't know how he ended up here. I would have preferred Joel Edgerton from Train Dreams to be here. What I liked most was the step-by-step construction of the story.
author: Smirke

title: Two Faces, Many Secrets
text: This is one of those films that leaves you scratching your head a little after its 2 hours and 41 minutes runtime. Directed by Brazilian director, Kleber Mendonca Filho, and starring Wagner Moura, the film takes us back to 1970s Brazil during "a time of great mischief." What stood out to me most wasn't the political aspect, but the Brazilian atmosphere and the people. Beneath the tension and paranoia, the film quietly celebrates the Brazilian family spirit, friends and relatives gathering, sharing food, joking, and looking out for one another even in a country plagued by corruption, surveillance, and abuse of power. It's a small but touching reminder that ordinary warmth can survive even in oppressive times, and it reminds me very much of my country, Malta, as well.One of the film's more interesting ideas is its obsession with facades. Almost everyone seems to be hiding something, living a double life of sorts, and the strange image of the two-headed cat works as a neat little symbol for that theme. Two faces, two stories, two truths. In a way, the title starts to feel less literal: the "secret agent" could be anyone, because everyone is protecting some private version of themselves. Mendonca Filho also throws in some odd, almost folkloric moments that blur the line between reality and myth, an unusual stylistic choice that gives the film a distinctive flavour, even if I personally do not think it is incorporated well into the story.That said, the film isn't flawless. Some stretches are undeniably slow, occasionally drifting into monotony, and the modern day ending feels a bit anti-climactic after spending so much time immersed in the wonderfully recreated 1970s setting. Still, there's a lot to admire here: the careful period detail, the thoughtful themes, and Moura's performance. It may not be a film I loved every minute of, but it's certainly one I respected, and in today's cinema landscape, that already counts for quite a lot.
author: carmelolia

title: Failed movie
text: The story had potential and it could have been a good movie...if the director hadn't made bad decisions when deciding how to tell the story. It's like after slowly building the characters, the plot and the tension (if you can call it that), the director suddenly decided to quit. The result is 2.5+ hours for nothing. Don't waste your time watching it.
author: gprytoluk

title: Utterly Boring
text: What an utterly boring movie. The story is a complete mess. There are fragmented storylines that go nowhere and add little to nothing to the story. It's so convoluted that the crux of the movie isn't clear until around an hour and forty-five minutes into the movie. By the two hour mark the movie picks up but by that time I just wanted it to end. There's no reveal or twist that justify how relentlessly boring this movie is. Editing is not this movie's strong point.
author: williamnash-25132

title: A Stylish but Uneven Spy Story
text: 77/100The film works best as a slow-burn political thriller built around tension and atmosphere rather than action. The story gradually reveals a world of paranoia, secret agendas, and ideological manipulation, letting the mystery unfold through quiet conversations and suspicious characters. The script keeps the intrigue alive and the pacing allows the suspense to build naturally, making the narrative feel thoughtful and controlled.The performances are solid, even if none of them become truly unforgettable, while the direction gives the film a restrained and elegant visual style. Muted colors, careful framing, and subtle music create a consistent mood that fits the espionage theme well. At times the storytelling feels a bit distant and could use stronger dramatic peaks, but the atmosphere and steady tone keep it engaging.
author: Niklyor

title: There was nothing secret about it.
text: The Secret Agent arrives with an impressive cast. The actors are clearly talented, delivering performances that remind us why they're so respected. Every scene they inhabit crackles with authenticity, their craft elevating even the most questionable material. It's almost enough to make you forget that the film around them is a mess.Almost.The trouble begins when the movie decides it isn't content being one thing. For a while, it plays as a taut espionage thriller-tense, grounded, focused. But then it veers into something else entirely: a folkloric detour about a "hairy-legged legend" that feels like it wandered in from a completely different film. This urban legend subplot isn't just jarring; it's baffling. What is this doing here? Why should we care? The film never bothers to answer, treating its own mythology as self-explanatory when it's anything but.As if one identity crisis weren't enough, The Secret Agent further sabotages itself with its structure. The constant shifting between past and present feels less like narrative ambition and more like indecision. Flashbacks don't illuminate-they interrupt. Timelines blur not for artistic effect, but because the editing seems unsure which story it actually wants to tell. By the midpoint, the tension has evaporated, replaced by confusion. We're left piecing together fragments that never quite cohere.And yet, the cast persists. They act their hearts out, finding nuance in underwritten roles and selling emotions the screenplay hasn't earned. Their commitment is admirable, almost heartbreaking-like watching talented performers forced to play a broken instrument. You can see the better movie hiding behind their eyes, the one that might have existed with a tighter script and a clearer vision.What a waste. What a frustrating, bewildering waste of talent. The Secret Agent had the pieces: a stellar ensemble, an intriguing premise, the bones of something compelling. But it drowned them in folklore no one asked for and a timeline no one can follow. By the end, you're left not with the thrill of a spy thriller, but with the melancholy of watching great actors stranded in a film that lost its way and never found it back.
author: Sewer-eel

title: Could've been a classic
text: I genuinely believe the first two-thirds of the film are absolutely perfect. The characters are so unique, and they're really presented in a good way, making them memorable and engaging. However, unfortunately, the film really goes off the rails after that point. The pacing becomes inconsistent, it speeds up and slows down erratically, and the plot becomes incredibly unclear, leaving so many loose ends and unanswered questions. I truly believe that the film had the potential to become a classic, but in the end, that opportunity was ultimately wasted.7/10.
author: alejandrocorreab

title: Powefull essay on Memory
text: "The Secret Agent," Kleber Mendonça Filho's marvelous new film, is a complex piece of craftsmanship that defies viewer expectations, evoking 1970s Brazil as it zigzags between personal drama, political reality, corruption and violence, cinematic memory, and the identity of a city, Recife. Patiently unfolding a mosaic of stories and characters that converge on Marcelo (a monumental performance by Wagner Moura), the film illuminates the present, with a melancholic anticlimax that confronts us with the question of what we remember and what we forget, and why."It was while writing the script that I realized that conservatism in Brazil in the 21st century was a nostalgic and sugarcoated version of the civil-military dictatorship of the 20th century. The dictatorship not only set back the development of Brazilian society by at least 30 years, but also left millions of Brazilians confused about what it means to live in a moral and democratic country. With the worsening of the moral crisis in 2018, marked by the election of Jair Bolsonaro, Brazil also dusted off ideas that had been shelved during the 40 years of redemocratization that preceded this setback."In Argentina, this month (50 years since the start of the bloody civil-military-clerical dictatorship), the director's words resonate powerfully. It is possibly the most important Brazilian film in decades, and should ideally be seen on the big screen to appreciate it in all its magnificence.
author: Bocio

title: A Story That Needed More Context
text: It left me with mixed feelings. Throughout the film, I had the sense that it was building toward something meaningful, but I struggled to fully understand the story because I didn't have enough context.It was never clear to me that the film takes place during Brazil's fascist military regime. Because of that, I didn't grasp that Marcelo (or Armando) was essentially a refugee trying to escape problems tied to someone within the regime. Likewise, I never understood who Ghirotti really was. To me, he seemed more like a mafia figure than someone connected to political power.There were also elements, like the strange imagery involving legs in sharks and parks, that felt symbolic but never fully explained. I know think they are linked to disappearances. Without a clearer framework, these moments only added to the confusion rather than enriching the story.By the end, the film felt like it was heading in an interesting direction but stopped before the narrative and its themes became clear. It's not a bad movie; in fact, it might even be a good one. But I felt the film didn't provide enough tools for viewers unfamiliar with the historical context to fully understand what was happening.
author: ricarwuf

title: There's A Good Movie In Here Somewhere
text: One of the joys of making a point out of seeing ALL of the films nominated for the Major Oscars every year is that there is a gem of film (usually in a foreign language) that the BankofMarquis would never have gotten around to viewing, but is rewarded with a rich, filmgoing experience.Films like I'M STILL HERE, DRIVE MY CAR, ANATOMY OF A FALL and the ZONE OF INTEREST were powerful character dramas that deserved to be seen by as large of audience as it can get.The Brazilian film THE SECRET AGENT does NOT fall into this category.Overly long at 2 hours and 45 minutes, THE SECRET AGENT tells the story of a man trying to escape a dictatorship government in Brazil in 1977. As Written and Directed by Kleber Mendonca Filho, this film has scenes that go on too long, characters that are interesting but go no where and a myriad of plots and subplots that abruptly end.All of this would be forgiven if they would all coalesce in the end, but most of those threads are just abandoned.Fortunately, Filho has Wagner Moura (last year's underrated CIVIL WAR) as the central character who is in about 90% of the scenes. His character, Marcelo is grounded, real, steadfast, earnest and focused on his goal of laying low until he can get out of Brazil. Moura is Oscar Nominated for Best Actor and while it is a very fine performance, Oscar-caliber it is not.And the same goes for the rest of this leisurely paced film. It is up for a total of 4 Oscars (including Best Picture) and it won't win any of these. What this film could have used is a good, strong editor, trimming this down to a taught 2 hours, driving out unnecessary characters and subplots.What Filho and team will have to be happy with is that with these nominations, folks like me are putting eyeballs on a film that, ordinarily, would not be on the radar.Letter Grade: B7 stars (out of 10) and you can take that to the Bank (ofMarquis)
author: bankofmarquis

title: A slow burner with room to breathe
text: It is interesting to me that there are a lot of negative reviews that discuss the length of this movie, the 'slow' pace and that it has a lot of scenes that they believe add nothing. It is interesting because I do feel that for a long time now, films seem to have to be well over two hours and that it is often unnecessary. However, I feel that this movie actually benefited from the long running time and the opportunity to build many scenes that seem to have no purpose to the story.So, let me put down my feelings on the positives. It looks fantastic with great sense of time and place and it has a vibrancy that really helps. The acting is very strong and the actors are given the space to really build. This is where the long running time comes in. This gives the film the opportunity to slowly build and slowly get to know our characters and it made me really care for them and what happens to them. It also means that they are real and even the 'baddies' have great characterisation and avoid the 2d characters often seen in big budget american movies.What i particularly liked is that we often don't know what is happening and that the film takes it's time to unravel the background rather than the Hollywood trope of characters discussing the background within a two minute conversation so we can get to the action scenes.It was actually a relief to see a movie that uses it's long running time to take it's time rather than say Marty Supreme or One battle after another where the pace was chaotic and the narrative repetitive.It is not perfect, I felt the modern day narrative was under developed and felt like a bolt on and it could have had a bit more style as at times it felt a bit too workman like.Overall though, I thought this was an assured piece of filmmaking, seemingly not for everyone.
author: Denno1972

title: Overrated
text: A boring film, lacking rhythm, and just when it starts to make some sense, it ends. The question is, what was the purpose of the young women listening to the recordings? Why is it called The Secret Agent - just to attract an audience? Wagner Moura's performance is overrated, nothing extraordinary. Overrated by some critics, and many seem to feel compelled to say it's very good. Two hours and forty minutes wasted.
author: AmadoB-8

title: A Thriller About Erased Identities
text: What great cinema Brazil has been making lately! A powerful film filled with suspense and a clear love for 70s cinema, building a portrait about the importance of historical memory and topics that are not unfamiliar across Latin America.Last year I'm Still Here, by Walter Salles, captivated and moved audiences with its portrayal of Brazil's military dictatorship; and this year that same topic is once again taken up by Kleber Mendonça Filho to tell a crime thriller about a professor who must take on a new identity after being persecuted, exposing the corruption of a system in which opposing the government meant being forced to hide who you were.At the same time, Recife in the late 70s becomes a character of its own: a city full of life and color, yet also marked by crime and impunity.Handled in a style very similar to 1970s and 80s thrillers and crime films - both in its narrative structure and in the cinematography by Evgenia Alexandrova, with period color palettes and the use of split diopter shots often associated with filmmakers like Brian De Palma - Mendonça delivers an intelligent and gripping film that also works as a tribute to the cinema of that era. The references are clear, such as The Omen and King Kong, which are advertised and screened in a movie theater that plays a key role in the story, the use of music by Ennio Morricone, and especially Jaws, which is used as an analogy for the feeling of a population trapped in social uncertainty.Wagner Moura has delivered strong performances in Brazil, such as in Elite Squad, in series like Narcos, and in Hollywood productions such as Civil War, but in this film he confirms himself as one of the most important actors of his generation. His performance as Armando (or Marcelo, depending on the identity he assumes) allows us to connect with his fears and insecurities as he tries to reclaim who he is and escape with his family.Also worth mentioning is Tania María's performance as Doña Sebastiana, stealing every scene with her spontaneity and warmth, as well as the actors playing the hitmen and police officers chasing him.After being one of the most awarded films of its Cannes edition (with four awards), Mendonça once again makes Brazilian cinema shine with an unsettling and political work that positions it as a strong contender for the Academy Award for Best International Feature Film.
author: GerardoMenmar

title: A finely detailed story about Brazil's difficult past
text: THE SECRET AGENT (2025) Armando (a fine Wagner Moura) drives his bright yellow VW bug into a gas station. It all seems normal enough even with a dead body attracting flies and wild dogs off in the distance. Cops arrive, but not to investigate the corpse. They demand to see Armando's papers (and his required fire extinguisher). It's a cash shakedown. Brazil. 1977. The government is run by a military dictatorship.In this simple seemingly unassuming scene, Writer-Director Kleber Mendonca Filho (AQUARIUS, BACURAU) subtly sets up the atmosphere of the time. American tunes and movies are allowed to be played, but Brazil isn't fully the free and easy place as seen in their Carnival celebration which is the backdrop of the story here.Armando is a former teacher who returns to the city of Recife where his son Fernando (Enzo Nunes) is being brought up by his deceased wife's grandfather, Sr. Alexander (Carlos Francisco), who is a film projectionist. Armando is given shelter by Dona Sebastiana (a spririted Tania Maria), who's a den mother to a small group of political outcasts and refugees. The large supporting cast is strong with Maria Fernanda Candido, Alice Carvalho and Udo Kier (in his final role).Filho's story is a slow burn, revealed without any rush to action, unfolding at its own pace. Nothing is as it appears at first, with Filho adding in bits of imagination and magic realism along the way ("the hairy leg" is sinisterly amusing). Spielberg's classic JAWS is to play at Alexander's cinema which also sets the backdrop for further offbeat metaphors (Cinefiles will particularly enjoy a scene where THE OMEN is showing and a woman in the lobby is overwhelmed by it). A light as some of these references may be, they also echo how government misinformation can be propagated on the masses and ingrained in their minds.Revealing too many of the literal plot details would spoil the finely detailed screenplay here. In many ways, Filho's movie is a companion piece to last year's excellent Walter Salles film I'M STILL HERE, each exploring different aspects of the web of corruption within the Brazilian government of the era. A corruption so deeply ingrained that it poisons everything from the law to academia to business.The greatest crime against the people wasn't simply the repression and violence against the citizens, but the theft of the population's collective memory. A society full of sharks, hairy legs and dishonor among politicians and their complicit conspirators. THE SECRET AGENT continues the effort to unveil more and more those secrets.The Secret Agent was nominated for 4 Academy Awards (Best Picture, Best International Picture, Best Casting and Wagner Moura, Best Actor)
author: gortx

title: An incredibly moving film that stays with you days after watching it.
text: This film was just exceptional. From the story, to the performances, to the cinematography, this film didn't disappoint in any aspect whatsoever.Wagner Moura was absolutely phenomenal. What a performance. He definitely deserved The Golden Globe. And if he won The Oscar it would 100% be deserved.The emotional impact of this film builds gradually. It lingers for the whole film once you understand what's going on. Even after the film when I walked out of the cinema I could still feel it deep down, like a weight on my chest. Once your film does that it's an immediate piece of art and the definition of cinema.Overall, this film was truly incredible. Seeing it in the cinema is how it should be seen."I'm not a violent person. But this guy... I would kill him with a hammer. I'd smash his whole head with a hammer."Marcelo Alves.
author: Kevin_Eleven

title: A Slow-Burning Spy Drama Elevated by a Great Performance
text: It feels less like a flashy spy show and more like the story of someone slowly losing trust in everyone around them. It's tense, thoughtful, and quietly intense - not packed with explosions, but full of emotion, doubt, and difficult choices. A good film, even if the story feels slightly unfocused at times, and yet another powerful performance from Wagner Moura. His Golden Globe Award win was well deserved, and this could finally be the role that earns him his first Academy Award.
author: pranabchaudhury

title: Not enough content for such a long runtime
text: Like what the title says... way too long movie with not enough content: the story is there, it feels like it is knit point by point but it never gets there. By the time it gets there, we are at the end of the movie and the end doesn't reveal in a way to compensate for what we are promised.I understand that these lately movies from Brazil are sort of accounts for a past that yearns for mourning and reconsideration and I love the nostalgic elements and story telling but if I am given an almost-three-hour long story, I think there should be more coherence in at least a combination of story telling elements: editing, cinematography, story itself etc. Unfortunately none of them made enough of a whole for getting through this viewing.
author: isefmert29

title: A waste of time and money ..
text: Honestly, I don't understand how this movie won so many awards and received so much recognition. The script is a disaster , the film is extremely boring, and on top of that it lasts more than two hours.And the problem is not that the film is slow. Slowness, in itself, does not necessarily have to be a flaw. The problem arises when slowness is merely postponement. When a movie runs for two hours and forty minutes-which is excessive-and most of its scenes consist of characters talking, verbally explaining what the film does not know or cannot construct through images, what becomes evident is a lack of an aesthetic proposal capable of sustaining such a duration.
author: augustushood-31885

title: It's a film that only really makes sense if you know the context of the South American dictatorships of the 70s and 80s.
text: What a disappointment this film was.I really wanted to love it more than any of this year's Oscar nominees.But the truth is, I just couldn't connect with it.First of all, I'm Uruguayan, and I understand the social and political complexities of the civic-military dictatorships during the 70s and 80s, so I can grasp certain things that audiences in other parts of the world might not.What happened with this film? I think it has many interesting and impactful points, but also many others that feel disconnected.Let's talk about the good: the cinematography is a major strength; it portrays Brazil in 1977 authentically, with all that entails. Moving on to the acting, I completely believe Wagner Moura's performance, which I think is incredible. The film is full of symbolism, some more obvious, others subtle. The most eloquent aspect is Armando's son's fears about the film "Jaws," which is a clear allegory for the American specter lurking behind dictatorships-a fear that is felt but not tangible. It's a real, social fear that anything happening during that era would be masked and covered up; hence the secret agent, the state. That ending, where we see the cinema that represented the people transformed into a clinic where the blood of all Brazilians is drained, is very powerful.But there are things I really don't like about the film. I think the editing is bad, really, I don't like it; it's disjointed. There are plot threads that you don't know where they're going, what they mean, what's happening. Armando's death is completely anticlimactic, off-screen, anonymous. Why does this happen when we saw so much death in the last half hour of the film? Another thing I don't like is the pacing. We spend over an hour trying to figure out what the movie is about and why hitmen are after him. It's implied that his wife was murdered by the Brazilian government and that he had to go into hiding because of it. Again, this is very difficult to understand if you don't know the history, and while I'm a firm believer that reading is essential, not everyone will connect with this.Another thing that bothers me is the allegory of the hairy paw. How many of us who saw the movie know that it refers to and acts as a representation of the irrational horror and state repression that terrorized the population during the dictatorship and that was used by the press to communicate this?In short, I feel that this movie isn't the masterpiece it's being touted as during awards season. I don't think it's better than "Sentimental Value," for example. I feel that last year's "I'm Still Here" was more representative of the turbulent times South American countries went through during those decades.Of course, this is just my opinion, and I could be wrong...
author: LeonardoOliva69

title: I loved this movie. Flat out.
text: From the first reel, The Secret Agent announces itself with confidence-smart, dangerous, sensual, and deeply assured of its own voice. The acting is uniformly excellent, the direction is precise and playful, and the film hums with a lived-in intelligence that never feels showy or self-congratulatory.But what truly floored me is the feel of it. Set in the 1970s, this doesn't merely recreate the era-it inhabits it. Whether that credit belongs to the cinematographer, the director of photography, or a perfect storm of design choices, the result is uncanny: the film genuinely looks and moves like something made in the '70s. Not a modern pastiche. Not a glossy homage. An actual 1970s movie energy-grain, pacing, framing, color, restraint. For those of us who grew up in that decade, the internal references land with quiet delight rather than winking nostalgia.At the center of it all is Wagner Moura, delivering a performance that is overwhelming in the best way. He's magnetic-sexy, funny, dangerous, wounded, and effortlessly watchable. This is star work without ego, charisma without strain. You don't just follow him-you lean in.The film balances tones with remarkable ease. It's funny without defanging itself, violent without glorification, and genuinely tense when it needs to be. A couple of sequences are edge-of-your-seat tight, and the music-perfectly chosen-doesn't underline emotion so much as breathe alongside it.Most impressive of all is how natural it all feels. Nothing is forced. Nothing begs for attention. The craft is so confident it almost disappears, leaving you with the rare pleasure of simply being inside a movie-one that knows exactly what it is.Smart. Sexy. Dangerous. Funny. And utterly authentic.A terrific film.9 out of 10.
author: nydjames

title: Ambitious, but narratively muddled and poorly edited
text: The Secret Agent benefits from a richly realised world, excellent cinematography and consistently excellent performances from a fairly sizeable cast. However, it is undermined by a bloated runtime, poor pacing and a muddled narrative that is difficult to follow at times, which make for a somewhat exhausting and emotionally flat viewing experience.
author: edredwhittingham

title: Intimacy in the classroom.
text: With the depth and symbolic richness projected by the vibrant life of one of the most multicultural, unequal and complex countries in the world, Brazil, portrayed in cinema with so much depth and charm.It is a dazzle-filled 2-hour dance of Wagner in a battle of personas that is extremely demanding when one is as socially and politically active as the actor. How to distance yourself from the story that was being told, from your connection with the previous character and from the nation's painful past, which serves as the diegetic backdrop for the narrative? And he achieves this.In addition to the admirable range of histrionic nuances that he achieves, his brilliant characterization is perceptible to the public thanks to an imposed testimonial point of view, which becomes one of the strokes of genius in Mendonça Filho's direction. This creates a distorted vision and a feeling of somewhat intrusive presence, as if coming from behind an invisible barrier that we have crossed and we don't know how, but our curiosity makes us continue listening and observing.Evgenia Alexandrova's work in photography is the second largest celestial body that occupies this solar system that revolves around Wagner Moura's effervescent work as a Sun.Another beautiful costume design work by Rita Azevedo in its quality of setting and transporting the audience to a time through specific styles of the northeastern work environment between eras in Brazil that seem like hundreds of different countries glued together to form a single story that is connected by its heritage in the way of dressing in each region of the country.
author: vitinhaoriginal

title: A interesting watch
text: The Secret Agent ended up being a slightly frustrating affair.For me it circled around a lot of interesting ideas and content and moments, but never executed them to the level they could have been. Perhaps this was due to the length, which was a little unforgiving. Perhaps due to the sprawling story that maybe needed a little more focus. It is still a good story and interesting film, but maybe missed a few opportunities.Still, there is plenty of vibrancy and energy to the whole thing, and a host of really solid performances.I was expecting a bit more from this film given some of the hype, and I wouldn't say I was disappointed considering I still enjoyed it, but it could have been better!
author: ethanbresnett

title: So Secret You Don't Understand Anything
text: Aside from the joke in the title, there is some truth in it.The movie offers an interesting setting. Brazil in the 70s. The dictatorship. The paranoia. The daily tension of living in a country where silence could be survival. That context alone is powerful. Latin America has enough history of repression to build a thriller without inventing anything.The problem is not the material. It is how it is delivered.The film is painfully slow. Even for a political thriller. It drops hints about who the characters are, what they did, how they are connected. But the hints are so subtle that following the story becomes an exercise rather than an experience. I was fully paying attention in the cinema and still felt like I was missing something. Not because it is deep, but because it seems engineered to be obscure.Throughout the film you wait for a payoff. You assume the climax will justify the restraint, the bland dialogues, the focus on ordinary daily routines. But when the climax finally arrives, yes, there are deaths, but they do not land. You were never allowed to truly connect with anyone except the main character, and even his story is never fully told.Some critics might say that is the point. That the emotional distance reflects the cold machinery of dictatorship. Maybe. But intention does not automatically create impact. If the audience leaves without feeling the weight of what happened, something is missing.Then there is the leg that comes back to life. A clear nod to magical realism, to Latin American literature. On paper, interesting. In practice, completely out of place. It feels like a different film inserted into this one. Instead of enriching the narrative, it fragments it even more.At moments the movie feels like a series of recordings stitched together. Detached. Observational. Just like the women listening to tapes inside the story. You keep waiting for cohesion.I respect the decision to tell the story in that fragmented way. It is bold. It is controlled. It is deliberate. But it is also distant. There is too much hype around the craftsmanship and not enough emotional payoff.Kudos to Wagner Moura. His presence anchors the film.But for me, not kudos to the director.
author: alexandergotzp

title: The Secret Agent
text: It is fair to say that I went to see this Brazilian film purely because it received various accolades during Awards Season, but many foreign language films have gained my admiration, and I was hoping this would be another one, directed by Kleber Mendonça Filho (Bacurau). Basically, in 1977, during the political turmoil of the Brazilian military dictatorship, former professor and widower Armando Solimões (Golden Globe winning, and Oscar nominated Wagner Moura) travels to Recife, Pernambuco, during the carnival holiday, where his young son Fernando (Enzo Nunes) has been living with his in-laws since his wife, Fátima Nascimento (Alice Carvalho), passed away. He arrives at a refuge operated by former far-left communist Dona Sebastiana (Tânia Maria), where he adopts the name Marcelo and befriends other political protestors, including Claudia (Hermila Guedes), Haroldo (João Vitor Silva) and Angolan Civil War refugees, Thereza Vitória (Isabél Zuaa) and Antonio (Licínio Januário). Meanwhile, corrupt Civil Police chief Euclides Cavalcanti (Robério Diógenes) and his sons Sergio (Igor de Araújo) and Arlindo (Italo Martins) are called in to investigate a severed human leg that has been found inside the stomach of a tiger shark. Armando is placed in a job by his protestor network at the city's identity card office under the alias of Marcelo, where he has a chance encounter with Euclides, who offers him his friendship and protection. Armando is visibly irritated by Euclides's arrogant behaviour, which includes harassing Jewish Holocaust survivor Hans (Udo Kier, in his last posthumous role), who is mistakenly assumed to be a Nazi fugitive. Armando's job allows him to search for files on his late mother, whom he has some memories of. Meanwhile, in São Paulo, hitmen Bobbi (Gabriel Leone) and Augusto (Roney Villela) are hired by former Eletrobras executive director Henrique Ghirotti (Luciano Chirolli) to kill Armando, whom he has a political and personal vendetta against. Armando meets political resistance movement leader Elza (Maria Fernanda Cândido) at the Cinema São Luiz, where his father-in-law Sr. Alexandre (Carlos Francisco) works as a projectionist. Recording a testimony about Ghirotti's corrupt activities, Armando recounts a dinner he went to with Fátima that ended in a physical argument with Ghirotti and his son, following remarks on the couple's lower socioeconomic class and supposed communist sympathies. Elza informs Armando that a contract killing has been ordered and instructs him to flee the country. Sergio and Arlindo dump the human leg in the Capibaribe River, where it suddenly comes to life and attacks gay men cruising in a public park at night. The story of the leg attacking, presumably a newspaper cover-up for the political corruption and violence during the carnival week, creates a public frenzy. Augusto and Bobbi hire penniless gunman Vilmar (Kaiony Venâncio) to find Armando. Anticipating a departure, Armando bids farewell to Dona Sebastiana and the other refugees. The following day, Armando's cover is blown after Bobbi closely monitors Sr. Alexandre. Vilmar identifies Armando but fails to kill him, shooting Arlindo and a fellow police officer. Bobbi, watching from a distance, chases a bleeding Vilmar through Recife's city centre and eventually finds and kills Vilmar in a barbershop. In the present day, history student Flavia (Laura Lufési) is researching Elza and the resistance network through audio recordings and newspaper archives. She learns that Armando was murdered and framed as a corrupt professor. Travelling to Recife, Flavia meets with Fernando, who has become a middle-aged doctor. He agrees to be interviewed in the hospital where he works after Flavia donates blood. The pair discuss Armando's political past and subsequent assassination, and the identity of Armando's mother, "India". Fernando states he has no memory whatsoever of his father, but fondly recalls watching Jaws with his grandfather. The cinema he went to see the movie has since become the hospital where he works. Also starring Enzo Nunes as young Fernando Solimões, Isadora Ruppert as Daniela, and Aline Marta Maia as Lenira Nascimento. The scenes and moments that stand out are the opening scenes at the petrol station with the dead body, the boy obsessed with Jaws including drawing his own posters, the severed leg reanimating and kicking people to death, the chase through ending in the barbershop, and obviously the present day ending with the former cinema reveal, but I must admit I struggled to understand everything going on. The main plot involves a mysterious bloke wanting to connect with his son while there is a complex story dealing with corruption, chaos, and carnival; it has eye-catching period detail, memorably engaging and bizarre parts (a two-faced cat and the leg kicking people are random and perhaps too weird), and it is unclassifiable, but I can see why it has gained attention, it is an unusual but equally fascinating neo-noir historical political thriller. It is nominated the Oscars for Best International Feature Film, Best Picture, and Best Casting, it was nominated the BAFTAs for Best Film Not in the English Language, and Best Original Screenplay, and it won the Golden Globe for Best Motion Picture - Non-English Language, and it was nominated for Best Motion Picture - Drama. Good!
author: jboothmillard

title: dumber than its trailer
text: There is no explanation of why the protagonist and the villain hated each other so much, apart from a single incident, which most normal people would have tried pretending never happened after a week of quiet seething. Non-contextual insertion of a different suffering individual in a single scene ... pointless virtue signalling. It has that modern "streaming service" feel of fine acting, great filmography and perfect period décor, but the script is in black and white.
author: scrofula_and_vanity

title: Film making of the highest order
text: The Secret Agent takes its time to weave a narrative and reveal its story, occasionally falling into flashbacks too often, but it never loses its focus and honesty. There are wonderful performances, superb period detail, outstanding cinematography, and clever direction, I thought it was simply brilliant. The story is at times brutal and honest, and essentially is all about (as we are initially told) the 'time of mischief' when crime and corruption ate up Brazil at the time if the generals in the 70s, and is about individuals caught up in it. The violence when shown is never over done, or glamorised. Great cinema is rewarding and absorbing, and this is it.
author: brockfal

title: Features some solid acting and immersive atmosphere but is brought down by its unnecessarily complex plot
text: "The Secret Agent" is a political thriller film written and directed by Kleber Mendonça Filho ("Aquarius", "Bacurau", "Pictures of Ghosts"). Starring Wagner Moura in the lead role, it features some solid acting and immersive atmosphere but is brought down by its unnecessarily complex plot.In 1977 in Brazil, former professor Armando Solimoes (Wagner Moura) returns to his hometown of Recife where he intends to reunite with his young son Fernando years after he has been living with his in-laws in the aftermath of his wife's passing. Upon arriving, Armando takes refuge among a community of far-left activists where he takes on the name "Marcelo" and befriends other members of the group living there. Amidst the ongoing political turmoil taking place within the country, Armando soon finds himself meeting with Elza (Maria Fernanda Cândido), who leads a resistance movement at a cinema that employs his father-in-law as its projectionist. After recounting his past experiences to her, Armando is informed by Elza that a hit has been placed on him and is advised to flee Brazil as soon as possible.During the 1970s, the entire country of Brazil experienced much unrest due to numerous circumstances related to serious political matters ranging from changes in leadership to economic instability. As someone who wasn't even born when any of this happened, let alone lived in the country at the time, I can't exactly say I am fully versed in everything related to any of these major events that took place in Brazil's recent history so I'm not going to pretend I know everything about it outside of a quick Google search. On that note, it seems that this era is filled with many stories and anecdotes of those who lived through this chaotic time, some of which have been adapted into media for the general public. The 2025 film "The Secret Agent" takes place during this tumultuous era, but if it weren't for its great performances and nicely atmospheric direction, I would say I took away absolutely nothing of value about what even happened here over the course of this decade.What I take the most issue with in this film is the way in which it sets its story up, only to become sidetracked later on. Beginning in 1977 in the Brazilian town of Recife, we are introduced to our protagonist Armando, who drives his yellow Volkswagen beetle into a service station to fill up on gas. After arriving, Armando cannot help but notice a dead body lying on the ground near the gas pumps, something the attendant tells him not to worry about since it has nothing to do with him. A short time later, two policemen pull up to question Armando about what he is doing in Recife, but surprisingly have no interest in dealing with a corpse lying there right in front of them. As a way to get himself out of trouble, Armando bribes one of the officers, not with money but with a packet of cigarettes. Though I did enjoy the way this immediately establishes that the film will be addressing corruption within Brazil's law enforcement during this particular era, it quickly becomes evident that this will be only one of the many issues this story tries but flounders at bringing up to the audience, and as a result it becomes rather muddled in its execution.Once Armando arrives at his predetermined destination, we watch as he attempts to become friends with a group of political dissidents, where he is given the new name of Marcelo and given the job of working at their identity card office. It is at this point the film starts to introduce multiple plot elements that either go nowhere or aren't communicated all that well to uninformed viewers. For instance, we are shown that random people are being killed and their bodies are dumped in the ocean, only for their severed limbs to turn up inside a shark's stomach being cut open at a research lab. As well as this, we also see that certain refugees are being smuggled into the country, including a German Holocaust survivor incorrectly assumed to be a Nazi war criminal by others. While these story elements seemed intriguing at first, I was disappointed to see that the film never truly follows up on anything substantial outside of some strangely placed comic relief. Worst of all, the film's entire second act is filled with bizarre moments like this that had me scratching my head over what I was supposed to consider important or insignificant as a whole.It's hard to say whether or not it's because I'm not too familiar with Brazilian culture in general or if it is because director Kleber Mendonça Filho is just blindly assuming his audience knows everything already, but I frequently struggled to figure out almost everything this film was trying to convey to me. Several references to political upheavals that took place years prior to the film's story feel glanced over and the allusions to folk tales that the general population of Racife are all aware of seem tacked on as an afterthought. Normally I wouldn't be too bothered if the film was simply trying to show its audience something that is meant as an inside joke for only Brazilians to understand, but when it is featured so often throughout the story, it causes casual viewers like myself to scratch my head in confusion trying to make sense of what I am watching. Mind you, for a film that clocks in at 160 minutes (2 hours and 40 minutes), the persistently confused state I was in helped prevent me from ever being bored throughout its lengthy runtime.To his credit, Kleber Mendonça Filho at least does a great job at immersing the viewer in late 1970s Brazil, which is one of the main reasons the film is so nice to look at. Filho accomplishes this by featuring some creative cinematography to help capture the sketchiness of this era that characters like Armando must navigate through if they want to survive. There were some nicely shot scenes of the town of Recife that give the location a cosy yet seedy vibe of a place that looks pleasant enough on the surface but is hiding genuinely sinister secrets out of the public eye. Additionally, since sharks are mentioned frequently throughout the story in relation to the aforementioned human body parts found inside their stomachs, the movie "Jaws" is featured prominently at times, either with it playing at the cinema Armando's father-in-law works at or with his son's obsession with the film's famous poster of the shark rising up to eat a swimmer. It's just a shame that most of these parts feel more like cosmetic features of the story rather than anything of real substance.Carrying the film's convoluted story entirely by himself, Wagner Moura gives one of his best performances as Armando, a man living through all of these turbulent times. Though the rest of the movie's plot was confusing all round, Moura has plenty of screen presence to help the audience deal with even the most complex of story elements. Without giving away spoilers, in the film's final act, I was on edge as to how Armando's character was going to turn out during one particularly climactic scene. Had the rest of the plot been able to keep up this same momentum the whole way through, I would be more inclined to say that the film is worth sitting through solely for that scene alone, but there is little else worth mentioning that even comes close.Though the film is certainly not without its fair share of highlights, "The Secret Agent" is far too reliant on the audience's prior knowledge of modern Brazilian history to be easily accessible for casual viewers. That's not to say that the film is bad in a conventional sense, in fact, stylistically it is very competently made, although people who know next to nothing about Brazil's culture will likely have great difficulty in trying to figure out what is happening most of the time. Granted, I'm sure there are plenty of those out there who could make sense of the film's quirkier, more distinct attributes without any problems, but for someone like me who only wants to be entertained, it doesn't quite hit the mark. With that in mind, I think there are much better Brazilian films out there that are worth your time, like "I'm Still Here" and "Central Station", so you'll always have those to enjoy instead.I rate it 6.5/10.
author: MrDHWong

title: Slow motion Brazileiro
text:
Beautiful,colourful depiction of a dark era of Brazil, a story that takes forever to start and once it starts flowing, it abruptly ends leaving nothing but loose ends
Two hours forty minutes long, thank God I didn't pay to watch it on a Theater and waste a full evening/night. Really disappointed even if I enjoyed the characters, but even their stories are left hanging even if there was sooo much time!
author: bob_katsionis-75670

title: Thin Sctipt Fleshed Out with Small Talk
text: You know you're going to have to work to enjoy a film when small talk makes up half the script. I want to escape small talk I can't avoid all day, not listen to 'authentic' characterization. There was one scene where people were meeting for the first time and the editors left in the hellos, hi, how are you, fine, great, just ... whatever, oh that's nice. Really?No Really?Yes there is mystery, and clues that all this slow burn, maudlin 'authenticity' is leading somewhere, but I just didn't care. Maybe because there was too much mystery! Or maybe I'm a bit too much of a dopamine addict and need reqular shifts, you know, character beats or plot twists or some action.This film drags for an hour to reveal something, then drags again. It felt like a lot of foreplay by a man with a small you know what.The reviews, I'm guessing, are not a true reflection of what the average viewer will take away from this film. With a good 35 minute edit, it would justify a higher score for me. But nah. Boring.
author: dardunn-45060

title: directors skills amazed me
text: This is the kind of secret-agent film that doesn't rely on constant action or twists, but instead pulls you in through atmosphere and control. From the beginning, you can feel the director's confidence in the structure - it's patient, deliberate, and carefully built. The first half is undeniably slow, and at times it tests your patience, but it feels intentional rather than empty.The story itself isn't the strongest part of the film. It doesn't reinvent the genre, and narratively it's not the most gripping spy plot I've seen. However, what truly stands out is the direction. The visuals are elegant and immersive, the camera work is precise, and the editing gives even quiet scenes a strong presence. You can feel a clear understanding of mood and rhythm behind every frame.The characters are well-written and convincingly performed, which adds emotional weight to the film. The third act becomes slightly confusing, almost abstract, but it's beautifully executed and emotionally impactful. In the end, I wasn't immersed by the plot - I was immersed by the filmmaking itself. Not perfect as a story, but undeniably impressive as a piece of cinematic craft.
author: MorfenZ

title: A Heavy & Intense Political Thriller To Watch But It's Damn Slow
text:
The Secret Agent (2025)
Movie Review -The Secret Agent is one of those films that instantly tells you it is not here for quick entertainment. It aims to immerse the viewer in a tense political atmosphere, and to its credit, it succeeds on the higher part. I genuinely liked the core idea and the historical nuances woven into the narrative. The film feels serious, layered, and ambitious in its intent. However, it also tests your patience frequently. While the storytelling is rich and thought-provoking, the pacing makes the experience uneven. It's a film that demands attention and time, and whether that investment pays off depends on how much slow-burn political drama you can tolerate.Set during Brazil's military dictatorship era, the story follows a mysterious man who gets pulled into a dangerous web of surveillance, suspicion, and political intrigue. As events unfold, secrets from the past begin to surface, placing the protagonist in increasingly risky situations. The narrative carefully builds a picture of a nation under watch and the paranoia that comes with it. Without relying on loud dramatics, the film steadily explores themes of identity, resistance, and state control. The plot is intentionally measured, focusing more on atmosphere and character psychology than conventional thrills, which makes it feel authentic but occasionally overstretched. The final moments introduce exposure, escape and survival and that, my friends, you have to experience in the movie only.One of the film's biggest strengths is its intensity. The grounded and realistic treatment gives the story a raw, almost documentary-like feel that works very well. The political backdrop is handled with maturity, and many scenes carry genuine tension. However, the screenplay is where the film struggles. The pacing is undeniably slow, and at 160 minutes, it begins to feel exhausting. Another small but noticeable issue is with the subtitles - the white text on bright backgrounds becomes irritating at times and affects readability. These flaws don't ruin the film, but they do make the viewing experience more demanding than necessary. I would have loved to see the entire film in English and skip reading subtitles to focus more on the screen.Performance-wise, the film is solid across the board. Wagner Moura delivers a restrained and compelling lead performance, perfectly capturing the internal conflict of his character. His screen presence keeps many of the slower stretches engaging. Maria Fernanda Cândido offers strong support and brings emotional depth to her role. Gabriel Leone and the rest of the supporting cast fit naturally into the film's realistic tone. Overall, the acting department is one of the film's reliable pillars and helps maintain viewer interest even when the narrative pace dips. Carlos Francisco, Tânia Maria, Robério Diógenes, Alice Carvalho, Hermila Guedes, Isabél Zuaa, Udo Kier-everyone had something to add to.Technically, the film is quite impressive in parts. The cinematography is fine and effectively captures the tense mood of the period setting. The dialogues are fairly okay and serviceable, though not particularly memorable. Editing is where the film needed sharper work - a tighter cut would have significantly improved the flow. On the positive side, the production design is superb and convincingly recreates the era. The background score, however, doesn't add much to the impact. Director Kleber Mendonça Filho keeps the storytelling steady and convincing. For Indian viewers, the Brazilian political backdrop may feel alien, yet the film manages to hold attention. Still, the long runtime makes it exhausting. Overall, it's a good film that rewards patience, but it's your choice afterall.RATING - 6/10*
author: SAMTHEBESTEST

title: Not worth the time, unfortunately
text: As a Brazilian I was eager to watch this movie and the acting from Wagner Moura, but I ended up so disappointed and confused. Why is this movie being nominated for any awards? Honestly. The story is so boring and weird scenes and no ending. Wagner Moura has done various brilliant performances, but this movie didn't require anything special from him. I truly don't know what the hype is about this movie, unfortunately.
author: danielfelicissimo

title: One of the slowest and hardest-to-follow thrillers I've seen in a long time
text: The Secret Agent, directed by Kleber Mendonça Filho, is a visually striking but divisive political thriller set during Brazil's military dictatorship in the 1970s. The story follows Armando, a political dissident played by Wagner Moura, who returns to Recife under a false name while being hunted by killers.The basic idea is simple, but the way the story is told is complicated and often confusing. Important information is held back, and the film expects the audience to connect the dots much later. While this approach is ambitious, it often feels overly complex and emotionally distant.
