I frequently felt lost and had to read about the plot afterward to fully understand several twists and character motivations.Technically, the film is strong. The cinematography beautifully captures Recife with warm, rich colours that bring the 1970s setting to life. The opening gas station scene during Carnival is intense and engaging, and the final act is thoughtful and satisfying. However, the tension suffers because the music is used too sparingly, and much of the film feels tonally flat until the last thirty minutes.Moura gives a solid and restrained performance, though it doesn't feel truly awards-worthy. Tânia Maria stands out with a strong screen presence.Overall, The Secret Agent is well made but somewhat overhyped. It is colourful, ambitious, and occasionally moving, but its long runtime (over two and a half hours) and unclear storytelling weaken its impact.6/10 - I rated it slightly higher for its exotic setting; a similar U. S. or European film might have scored lower.
author: BeneCumb

title: Solid decent film
text: I finally watched The Secret Agent, and overall, it's a solid film, though it does feel a bit too long.The story is well executed, and the writing is strong. However, the pacing could have been tighter. There are a few scenes that feel unnecessary and could have been removed to better emphasize the main plot and keep the momentum steady.As for the other aspects of the film, including the music, visual effects, and acting, everything is well done. Nothing stands out as exceptional, but nothing falls short either. It is consistently good across the board.Overall, the film is enjoyable and worth watching, even if it does not leave a major impact. With some trimming and slightly sharper pacing, it could have been even better. As it stands, it is a solid and satisfying watch.
author: Talfar_the_goat

title: Overrated
text: This South American thriller had been getting rave reviews from the usual wet pants critics (Bradshaw 5*, Kermode).It starts well with a strong opening ; a wide screen shot of a garage with a dead body laying on the ground, a sweaty,claustrophobia aided with great photography and sound design throughout, a great cast of supporting actors who all look of the late 70s period.Sadly, this is a meandering mess at 2hrs,40mins- the plot makes little sense, what levels of tension and suspense(remember, its marketed as a ' crime thriller') are diluted with choppy editing and acres of talk.Only the lead actor, a brooding presence by Wagner Moura, gives depth.Sadly, this isn't the superior 'The Secrets In Their Eyes'.
author: dweston-38669

title: What a treat!
text: Absolutely loved it. Superb acting, great characters and an unconventional (and perhaps slightly erratic story). Visually very pleasing.My only possible negative observation would be that it would pay to have just a little bit of background knowledge of the Brazilian political scene in the 70's - everything then falls into place).
author: fralbig

title: Abhorrently long and dull
text: Oscar season 2026 is really testing me. It continues to let me down. How 'The Secret Agent' is supposed to be one of the ten best films of the year, I have no idea.There is absolutely zero reason this movie needed to be as long as it is. Clearly nothing was left on the cutting room floor. There were endless scenes that could've easily been left out and the film would've been better for it.How Wagner Moura received a Best Acting nomination for this film I will never know. The entire film I kept thinking, man he must have an incredible scene coming up that is going to blow me away. Nope, it never came. Completely flat the entire movie.There is one interesting scene in the entire movie that comes later on, and even that they didn't know how to execute properly. This was a tough watch. 4/10.
author: jtindahouse

title: A good movie, but too long again.
text: Brazil's Oscar entry. Good or bad? In my case, a good film, but for my taste again, far too long. Gets a little boring at times. The actors are good in their roles, really good ensemble. Absolutely worth seeing as a film. It's just that I think these days the movies can be too long for no good. That bores me a lot.
author: kentrahmn

title: Beautifully made and acted.
text: I've just watched this and loved it, though it's not a short film. A standout performance from Wagner Moura who plays the main character, Marcelo/Armando. I could have sworn I've seen him in something before but a quick look on the Internet says this is his first role. I very much doubt it will be his last. A considered and nuanced performance.The film is very much in the style of Ken Loach in that it's consistently naturalistic. There is some violence in the final reel which has a documentary-like feel to it. The ending is especially tragic because the main character isn't a communist or politically motivated at all, but when a minister shuts down his university department in order to personally cash in on its research, Armando speaks truth to power and ultimately pays for this with his life.The film is beautifully made with great attention to detail and some fine performances which are honoured at the end in a quietly moving montage. Try and catch it in the cinemas if you can.
author: seannaylor

title: A compelling, harrowing portrait of a dark time
text: There are times when this is stunning, and packs a very powerful punch, or successfully brews simmering tension, and I do appreciate the piecemeal manner in which we see assembled the story of a man fleeing persecution and violence in a Brazil plagued by official corruption and malfeasance, by the wealthy and powerful operating with impunity, and by a press which through either collusion or incompetence effectively covers up these crimes. There are also times when this feels unfocused, and the direction too casual as some scenes sail by without striking a nerve, and the piecemeal construction may at points work against the whole as stitches feel too few and far between. At its best it's shocking, maybe even horrific, or otherwise brilliant, and I may go so far as to say that this is the case more than not - making it more unfortunate that some fragments are relatively weak.Yet 'The secret agent' does find more strength as the length draws on, and one might argue that even as a discrete narrative is imparted, this is as much a broad portraiture of this moment in Brazil as it is anything else, if not more so. And if that's the worst one has to say about a flick, and it is, then I'd say it's done very well for itself. I'm reminded to some degree of Walter Salles' 'I'm still here' of 2024, not just because of the historical setting of the active narrative, but also because this very much comes across as the continuing effort of a country to reckon with its living history of political horrors and unpredictable violence, moreover with critical, infuriating parallels to be drawn to the world of fifty years later. In addition to the matter of the plot structure it must be said that this is rife with disturbing violence, imagery and themes, even if those themes present more subtly, so it won't appeal to all comers. Yet the value the picture boasts handily outweighs any perceived shortcomings, and it's easy to see how it's earned such acclaim.Filmmaker Kleber Mendonca Filho weaves a difficult tale in a relatively difficult fashion, but it's readily compelling and absorbing all the same, with strong scene writing helping to carry the day alongside firm direction. I also very much admire the music, both the original score and the popular songs that dot the soundtrack, as they lend to not just the flavor but at points very distinctly to the tension and low-key thrills to be had. With excellent stunts and effects (less, perhaps, the computer-generated imagery), robust sound design, and steadfast acting, among more, further rounding out everything that makes this so good, ultimately there's not much going wrong with 'The secret agent,' and any subjective faults turn out to be fairly minor. I wouldn't necessarily say that this is a piece which demands viewership, but all told it's pretty fantastic, ably demonstrating what cinema is capable of as a storytelling medium that other forms are not. If you have the chance to watch then this is well worth checking out.
author: I_Ailurophile

title: A Masterpiece of Brazilian Cinema
text: Kleber Mendonça Filho creates a synthesis of his filmography in this thriller, blending several genres - comedy, thriller, and a touch of fiction. The film takes place in Recife during the 1970s, in the midst of Brazil's military dictatorship, a time marked by intense political censorship.For me, the highlight of Secret Agent is the characters, who perfectly represent the people of Recife. Kleber's direction is remarkable: he sustains a three-hour film that fully captures the audience's attention. Wagner Moura delivers an outstanding performance, adding even more strength to the story.I truly believe this is the greatest Brazilian film of all time.
author: luizfhmaia

title: Colorful, Vibrant, at times Confusing Political Thriller
text: I'll admit, I almost gave up on the movie a third of the way in. It dragged, didn't seem to explain a lot and some scenes appeared a bit pointless. However, I am glad I stuck with it. I'm not from Brazil and so, I don't have the prerequisite historical knowledge of this era, but as a cultural and historical voyeur, I came to really enjoy this window into another time, along with the way the story was told. Could it have been told another way? Sure but then it would lose its flavor. The movie rewards, but not immediately, quietly but surely making its point. Corruption, crime and the way it gets its tentacles around institutions is a familiar story no mater the setting, but this vivid account is great addition to the genre.
author: adaire-1

title: Great Neo-Noir Political Thriller
text: The Secret Agent: 1977, Armando (Wagner Moura), a former research professor is caught up in the political turmoil and corruption in the midst of the Brazilian military dictatorship. Travels to Recife where his young son Fernando has been living with his in-laws since the death of his wife. He is literally on the run, his attempts to expose corruption has resulted in his personal and professional destruction. He arrives at a refuge run by Dona Sebastiana (Tania Maria), where he adopts the name Marcelo. Dona Sebastiana reminds me of a more political version of Fräulein Schroeder, the landlady in Christopher Isherwood's Berlin novels who cared for and presided over a crew of misfits. The whole operation is funded by a political resistance movement led by Elza (Maria Fernanda Cândido), she tapes his account of the corruption he wants to expose and promises him passports for himself and his son. Life is cheap in Junta era Brazil. The corrupt company executive Armando is trying to expose hires hitmen to kill him. Not directly part of the State death squads but theu contacts in the police. The corruption of the police is shown at all levels from the petty actions of a cop who shakes down Armando to the Recife chief of police. Nepotism is also at play, incompetent sons, accompany the corript executive, the police chief, even the senior hitman. Armando's tale is clarified in flashbacks, how his fall came abiut, his relationship with his son and the boy's maternal grandparents is touchingly portrayed. A story about repression, resistance, a neo-noir thriller. In the background sharks abound, Jaws is showing at the cinema where .Armando's father in law works, a leg is found in a shark's stomach. This is likely a murder victim of the local police who steal it. A newspaper publishes a story about the leg coming back to life and attacking people. The absurd is used to cover the reality of mass killings going on in plain sight. Throughout the film we see present day researchers listening to Elza's tapes and checking newspaper files, trying to piece together the story. Great performances from an ensemble cast. Written and Directed by Kleber Mendonça Filho. 9/10.
author: Pairic

title: Pretensious
text: The film has both strong points and many weaknesses. In terms of the story, it is fairly cohesive and addresses important themes in a subtle way. Another very positive aspect is the portrayal of national culture: the settings are carefully constructed, with furniture, clothing, utensils, and other details that feel authentic and well researched.However, there are elements that undermine the film. What bothered me the most were the sex scenes, many of which feel unnecessary. This is not a matter of moralism; rather, some of these scenes simply add nothing to the narrative. Another issue lies with parts of the cast. While some performances are solid, others are quite weak-especially the actress who listens to the tapes and speaks with Fernando at the end.There is also a clear attempt to be "cool" by leaving things implied, but not in an effective or engaging way. Several plot points would have benefited from clearer development and resolution. Characters who initially seem important end up losing their relevance because of these choices.Finally, the title deserves mention. I am not even sure whether this title could be used, given that other films share the same name. More importantly, it sets up expectations that the film does not fulfill. It feels like another attempt to appear clever or stylish, but instead it comes across as tiring and somewhat pretentious.
author: rodrigo-nepomuceno

title: I walked out after 2 hours
text: I will not give a rating, as I have not finished this movie and after reading all the other people´s reviews there seems to be some kind of Tarantino style violence coming up in the final act which might redeem the whole thing. I don´t know. All I can say is that the first scene is intriguing setup, but then there is literally no plot for the next 100 minutes. In our screening there was a break in the middle and I tried to describe to my friend on Whatsapp what the movie had been about so far, but I could not come up with a single actual plot point. It is a guy in Brazil who is talking to various people in the village that he seems to be hiding in. And some other guys following the trail of a human leg that had been found inside a shark´s stomach. They give an explanation for the main character´s history at the beginning of the second half but then the movie just keeps on dragging on with unnecessarily long scenes that seem to go nowhere. After 2 hours I made the decision to leave and go to bed early. Maybe I missed out on a masterpiece in the final act, but no movie should have me watch nonsense for 2 hours just to blow me away at the very end. I don´t know, I am not from Brazil so maybe I also just missed the massive cultural significance that this movie must have...
author: julius-maerz

title: Perfectly OK but over hyped
text: I couldn't shake the feeling with The Secret Agent, Kleber Mendonça Filho's Brazilian set epic that takes us back to the political upheaval in the country in the late 1970's, that what I was witnessing was a decent film that has been made out to be a great one, one that has managed to receive amongst many other plaudits 4 Oscar nominations that should have arguably been taken up by other films of the 2025 class.Clocking in at a bum-numbing two hours and 40 minutes, Agent see's Filho deliver an on paper simplistic set up in a complicated way, creating an at times downright messy and convoluted plot line that is brilliantly filmed and staged but devoid of much heart and soul as Wagner Moura's critical darling performance as Armando Solimões walks us through this pretty mess.Opening with a fantastic segment at a rural gas station during Brazil's Carnival holiday period in a time in the country where corruption, civil unrest and political upheaval was running rife through the nation, Agent kicks off with a bang but at multiple time falls of the cliff with unnecessary scenes and an unwarranted storytelling delivery that makes Agent at times hard to really connect with and follow, despite its repeated attempts to show you how smart it all is.Receiving Oscar nominations in categories that includes coveted spots for Best Feature, Best Casting and Best Actor, it's particularly sad that Agent's prominence in these spaces took spots from the likes of Jesse Plemons or Joel Edgerton in the acting space or Train Dreams in the Best Casting category and while Moura certainly gives it his all, his much lauded performance here doesn't exactly demand we stand to attention.Having long been a prominent player in the likes of hit shows such as Narcos or Dope Thief and films such as Civil War and Elite Squad, Moura is as good as his ever been but it doesn't feel like a performance or role that will be talked about in the years yet to come, with the talented performer overshadowed here by the likes of Tânia Maria as Dona Sebastiana, a character you almost wish would become the central focus of the film.Everything is all very unconventional here in a lot of ways, the way in which the film is edited, its much talked about final act and zany score from composers Tomaz Alves Souza Mateus Alves all working to create the odd vibe Filho was going for but you can't help but escape the feeling that Agent was one of those films, one of a few that come around every year, where critics didn't want to speak up against it and general public didn't want to buck the trend and suggest it just wasn't as good as many have made it out to be.Final Say -The Secret Agent is a perfectly decent film but one that outstays its welcome on more than one occasion and benefits little from its odd storytelling approach. Pretty to look at and unquestionably unique, when you consider the other films contending for this year's Best Picture Oscar, this one stands out for all the wrong reasons.3 filing cabinets out of 5.
author: eddie_baggins

title: Tension, silence, and intelligence in every movement.
text: It's a tense, elegant, and very well-directed film. The atmosphere is its strongest point, with long, silent scenes that heighten the feeling of constant danger. It's not explosive action all the time; it's psychological tension. The performances are extremely solid, especially from the protagonist, who carries the film with a restrained yet intense presence. The cinematography is cold and calculated, matching the atmosphere of espionage and paranoia. Perhaps the slower pace will bother those expecting something in the Hollywood style full of chases and shooting, but this very choice makes the film more sophisticated. If you enjoy more "adult," political, and subtext-filled cinema, it's high-level. If you want pure adrenaline, you might find it slow.
author: Th10_Lmz

title: Dona Sebastiana and her six refugees.
text: Perhaps the enigmatically titled 'The Secret Agent (2025)' leaves you wondering what its ultimate point is, but who cares when it's as textured and tangible and compelling as this? There's no denying the vague sense of disappointment I was left with as the credits started to roll, and yet there's also no denying the growing fondness I have for the overall affair. The further I get from it, the more I like it. The third act's refusal to provide the catharsis of certainty is somehow both purposefully unfulfilling and retrospectively satisfying, both the flick's most immediate weakness and its most invigorating strength. It's the sort of ending that I'm almost sure is actually perfect, even though it initially seems to be quite underwhelming. Besides, despite being arguably a little too long, most of the movie is close to enthralling, an immersive and expansive experience that feels much bigger than it actually is. With a lived-in period setting, an unmistakable eye for incredibly specific detail, a surprising penchant for comedy, warm cinematography that almost emanates the dusty heat of its small-town setting, performances so subtly staggering that the characters almost feel real, writing mostly so well-observed that the feature feels like it must be based on a true story, and a complete disregard for the formula these Oscar-nominated affair's so often fall into, the picture constantly keeps you on your toes and effortlessly sucks you into its tactile setting within seconds. It's never quite what you expect it to be, but it isn't subversive simply for the sake of it. While I'm still not quite sure what theme - or, I suppose, message - it's specifically reaching for, it feels like the sort of thing that will solidify itself as completely cohesive on a rewatch and I have no doubt its writer-director knows exactly what he made and why he made it. Not all of it works as well as most of it does, with one sequence in particular standing out as something almost from an entirely different movie (and not in a particularly enjoyable way) even though I now know that it's referencing a specific piece of regional folklore. Furthermore, some of its purposeful messiness - which is generally a net positive - is still vaguely frustrating because the subplots that don't go anywhere in particular are so interesting that I'd like to see them through to their end (or not have been teased with them at all). Still, this complaint essentially means I wouldn't have minded if the movie lasted even longer than it already does, which kind of tells you how entertaining and engaging it is. In the end, this is a very strong effort. It's imperfect, but fun. Its delightful specificity is perhaps its best aspect, followed by its divisive yet bracing untidiness. It's the sort of thing I can see slowly starting to love, and a rewatch - when I know, and can appreciate, what it is - may prompt me to readjust my rating. For now, though, I'll simply say this is one of the best films I've seen so far this year. It isn't what I expected, but it's all the better for it.
author: Pjtaylor-96-138044

title: A haunting, atmospheric detour into the ghosts of 1977
text: If you come to The Secret Agent (2025) expecting a high-octane Hollywood spy thriller, the title might be your first "misdirection." Instead of explosive action, director Kleber Mendonça Filho delivers a deeply immersive, "slow-burn" neo-noir that feels more like a fever dream of 1970s Brazil. Set in 1977 Recife, the film is a masterclass in world-building, using the vibrant colors of Carnival and the gritty reality of the military dictatorship to create a refuge that feels increasingly like a trap.The story follows Marcelo, a technology expert fleeing a troubled past, who returns to his hometown seeking peace. However, the film cleverly shows that in a time of political oppression, there is no such thing as a quiet life. While the pacing might feel deliberate-even a bit "slow" for some-the film is layered with "mischievous" details, from urban myths like the "Hairy Leg" to the constant, subtle tension of being watched. These elements act as a breadcrumb trail that keeps you hooked, curious to see how Marcelo's personal search for identity connects to the larger corruption around him.The ending is bound to be a conversation starter. It avoids easy closure and instead leaves the audience with haunting questions about memory and history. By jumping between the past and a modern-day investigation, the film suggests that some secrets are never truly buried. Wagner Moura delivers a stellar, weary performance that grounds the more surreal moments of the plot.It's a unique cinematic experience-part political study, part atmospheric mystery. While it demands patience, the rewards are found in its rich textures and the lingering sense of unease it leaves behind.Verdict: Not your typical spy movie, but a brilliant, novelistic exploration of a haunted city. Highly recommended for those who appreciate history told through a lens of mystery.
author: ffadityawahyudi

title: Both too long and not long enough...
text: I really enjoyed this movie, especially once the momentum picked up, my only issue is they spend so much time building up to what seems like is going to be a great final act just to seemingly cut it short. All of the arcs coming together just for it to conclude off screen and get a time skip resolution.That is not to say that it is a bad film, I really enjoyed it and left wishing they had carried on instead of leaving the ending quite abruptly, but then again, leaving it to my imagination might not be all bad.
author: Funkycreeper200

title: Beyond boring and incredibly sluggish
text: I honestly don't understand how this movie has such high ratings. The pacing is incredibly sluggish, making it a chore to watch from the very first scene. I usually enjoy all types of cinema, but I couldn't even make it past the first 30 minutes of this one. Imagining sitting through the full 2h40m runtime feels like a total nightmare. It lacks momentum, the scenes drag on forever, and it simply failed to grab my interest or provide any reason to keep watching. Not for me at all, definitely a waste of time if you prefer movies that actually move at a reasonable pace.
author: lui-z

title: Never forget
text: In times like the ones we live right now, it's important we don't forget our darker times in recent history.Specially for Brazil's political climate not many years ago, this movie serves as a reminder and a cautionary tale at the same time, it shows how we as a society suffer from short term memory when it comes to politics, and how we should always value the freedom we have today regardless of the improvements that many countries still need.The movie itself is a slow burner, however the way the narrative is built and the fact that the director decided to go with a subtle realistic approach, makes it more engaging.There are some details you need to infer since they are not explicitly told, and it helps if you are aware of some of the historical events that took place in Brazil during the 70s and 80s otherwise you might not enjoy it as much.The performances are great and Wagner Moura is an amazing actor, one of the best, and while his performance is great I didn't quite feel it was Oscar worthy, not this time, but I've seen him depict outstanding character roles that were never recognized so I am rooting for him!It's a solid political movie but most of all a really important one to keep us thankful and watchful.
author: thePopcornExplorer

title: A Gripping and Powerful Thriller

Tania Maria is equally compelling, adding emotional strength and complexity that elevate the narrative even further. Together, they create a dynamic that makes the film both thrilling and deeply human.
author: ErikV-25

title: A waste of time.

The journalist at the end of the film, or whoever she was, for some reason fawned over the hero's son. What was the point of this whole story? It is not clear why the film received nominations. author: imdbfan-932457

title: Total Rubbish!
text: The Secret AgentThe title presupposes a thriller with espionage, a strong story, well written characters and some thrilling action, instead we got this glacial tripe!I am not suggesting that it was all bad, there were some redeeming factors for this movie. Firstly the styling and cinematography was really top quality. We really felt we were in 1977, what with the Volkswagen beetles the dodgy haircuts and cheese cloth shirts but quite frankly that was about it.There was just not sufficient here to sustain a movie of two hours and 41 minutes, the story was tissue thin. It amounted to a man that had upset his boss and gone into hiding over research money that was somehow being stolen. It seemed to me the movie displayed endemic corruption in Brazil in the 1970s linked to organised crime that just made any person that stood in their way disappear.The movie was glacially slow as we just saw characters going about their daily lives. It had very much a documentary feel about it with no narrative impetus and no character development.Again, I cannot see why this movie has been nominated in the Oscars, we do not nominate movies because they are worthy. They are nominated because of excellence, any other elevation of a movie brings the Oscars into disrepute. However, it is often the case that it is just sufficient to nominate and not to award to make the point.I'm going to give this a 2 outta 10 it really was a two hour and 41 minute waste of my life.
author: martimusross

title: Brilliant Intentions, Exhausting Experience
text: I've now watched two Best Picture nominees back to back that seem determined to test the audience's patience. At least with Sinners, a second viewing helped me grasp what it was trying to say. With this one, I'm still wrestling with it.The opening is strong, tense, promising, charged with purpose. It gives you the sense that something sharp and urgent is coming. But halfway through, the film begins to fragment. The narrative jumps, characters feel isolated within their own arcs, and the emotional throughline becomes increasingly obscure. Ambiguity can be powerful; here, it often drifts into disengagement. At times, it's not just confusing, it's exhausting.There's an especially grotesque image, a severed leg inside a shark (or whale) that initially feels like it should anchor the mystery. It doesn't. Only later, after researching, did I understand the "hairy leg" as coded satire used during Brazil's 1970s military dictatorship, a metaphor journalists employed when direct criticism meant disappearance or death. That context unlocked the film for me. It reframed the opening scene too: the ignored corpse, the indifferent police, the obsession with petty enforcement over real justice. The critique is sharp. Brutal, even.But here lies the issue, the film relies heavily on historical and cultural literacy. Without awareness of Brazil's dictatorship era, much of its allegory risks alienating international audiences. If your storytelling depends on decoding external history, you either guide the viewer or accept that many will be left behind.I respect the ambition. I see the intelligence. I recognize the political anger beneath the surface. But admiration is not the same as engagement. For me, the experience was distant, more intellectually decoded than emotionally felt.Best Picture? Not yet.
author: moondalbit

title: My country, Pernambuco
text: I am Brazilian, from Recife, so beyond all the historical sentimentality represented in the film, the regional cinematography has become impeccable in my eyes, in my experience.A sensitive theme is addressed in this film, talking about the dictatorship, the losses that came from that time, and how the lower class will always be a social victim. The plot lays bare the dilemma in which the rich get richer every day, while the poor, well, the poor die.
author: draude_m

title: Had a great time with this
text: The Secret Agent is a film brimming with quiet confidence, embracing a neo-noir lens to explore its political subject matter with restraint and precision. That confidence pays off, vividly capturing late 70s Brazil in all its color and corruption without ever softening the tension simmering beneath the surface. With the ever-charismatic Wagner Moura at its center, it all comes together into a damn good movie.
author: isaacsundaralingam

title: A Beautiful Film About a Brutal Time
text: The film really resonated with me. It's beautifully shot and powerfully conveys the era - you feel as if you're fully immersed in that time, even though you've never lived in Brazil. It also evokes very strong emotions and a deep, lingering sadness that you feel in your whole body, stemming from the way entire generations had their lives and their right to happiness destroyed by the sick, inexplicable whims of truly despicable people.
author: borbekk

title: A sprawling snoozefest.
text: Never has organised crime, espionage, and political subterfuge been such a bore. The Secret Agent is every bit as original as its title: at its heart, it's an incredibly basic neo-noir crime story dressed up as a crime family epic with some weak gesturing towards political commentary, and a horrible, lately-introduced framing device to scotch tape it all together.The story is that the protagonist, Armando, was a researcher working for some publicly-funded university lab in North Brazil, contributing to various little projects like a new leather tanning machine and an early lithium battery in the early-to-mid 1970s. This quaint existence is disrupted when, in a classic neo-noir fashion, a new corrupt political appointee, Ghirotti, is given charge of this lab and immediately begins to deliberately scuttle it in order to divert the taxpayer money away from the lab to a utility company, Electrobras, which he has a financial stake in; a classic set up: a private-sector crony comes in to dismantle public services to prop up their own private interests. This realistic, but rather mundane, event is the catalyst for what little plot there is; worse, we aren't even given this backstory until over an hour and half into the two-hour-and-thirty-minute movie. Ghirotti, despite only appearing in two brief scenes in the whole movie (true of ninety-nine percent of the characters in this movie), is inexplicably the most consequential character in the story as, for no particular reason, he has a hobby of hiring hitmen to wantonly assassinate the university researchers he's already put out of work and blacklisted from their respective fields. This brings us to where the movie actually starts, 1977, Armando is looking for documents verifying the identity of his mother (this personal goal as no relation to anything else in the movie: a common theme we'll see) in Recife, where he's assumed a pseudonym and taken lodging with a group of "refugees" (the quotes are even used when they refer to themselves), a ragtag bunch of people each only given a single sentence vaguely suggesting they're hiding from political prosecution from "somewhere" in the world (whether it be from France, Italy, Angola or whereever) - all the while Armando is oblivious to the fact that Ghirotti has put a price on his head and hired two professional contract killers (of erratic competence) to make a gruesome example of him ... for some reason.And, well, that's it! That's the entire plot. I swear I'm not being reductive: that's all the consequential plot details in the whole movie. Without spoiling things, even the assumed climactic confrontation from this setup happens off-screen between scenes. The rest of the movie's verbose rambling is dedicated to introducing hundreds of cardboard-cutout characters, each usually getting at most two scenes, and two sentences of lines across the whole movie, just so they can be immediately vanished from the story, effecting nothing. It's worth stressing, The Secret Agent is in no way a comedy: it takes itself completely seriously, the characters aren't even meant to be quirky one-off joke characters; they're all presented like they'll matter eventually but none of them ever do. There's a dirty police chief Euclides, who ends of having nothing to do with the story; there's a weird Jewish Nazi Soldier, who is present in one scene and never even thought of again; there's a long daisy chain of career criminals sought by the hired killers, each introduced solely for the purpose of introducing the next in the line; there's a disfigured cat with two faces, just there; Armando briefly has a dentist girlfriend, they're together for one scene; there's a woman at Armando's work that really wants to jump his bones, there's no follow up; and on, and on.... And I haven't even mentioned the completely pointless framing device of the modern-day, podcasting, university students, which isn't even introduced until you're two-thirds into the movie. The script is just pure whiteboard-scribble and noise for two and a half hours.The Academy really beef'd it making this their obligatory "one foreign-language best picture nom" (Sentimental Value doesn't count because it's still mostly English and stars an American actress) this year instead of a much more deserving film like It Was Just an Accident. The Secret Agent is an absolute embarrassment to the whole industry.

author: Stardst1

title: great Brazilian film
text: It's 1977 Brazil under military dictatorship. Strange things are afoot. A dead body is left at a gas station and the cops are not in a rush to pick it up. A leg is found inside a dead shark. Armando Solimões moves into an apartment block full of 'refugees'. He uses a false identity to work in the police records department. There are two assassins and many secrets. In the modern day, researchers are reviewing Armando's story.Right off the bat, this movie paints a great picture of this dysfunctional society. It's a little bit confusing at first. It's not showing all the cards right away. One has to stick with this story and it rewards the audience. I was a little shaken by the jumping dismembered leg, but it's a relief to turn it into a joke. The final takeaway from the modern day story is devastating. I love that it doesn't try to force a happy ending. This is great.
author: SnoopyStyle

title: If you like The Sympathizer, watch this film
text:
From its first frame to its haunting final beat, The Secret Agent is nothing short of a cinematic masterclass - a political thriller that transcends genre and becomes living cinema. Director Kleber Mendonça Filho delivers a work of art as bold as it is beautiful. At the same time, Wagner Moura gives a performance that can only be described as career-defining - magnetic, nuanced, and unforgettable.Set against the pulsating backdrop of 1977 Brazil under military rule, the story follows a man trying to find peace, only to discover that escaping the past isn't nearly as simple as returning home. The film immerses you in Recife with an atmosphere so visceral you can almost hear the samba rhythms, feel the humidity in the air, and taste the tension as night closes in
Where many thrillers offer formula, The Secret Agent offers depth. What could have been a straightforward chase becomes a meditation on memory, fear, and resistance. The storytelling constantly surprises - absurd moments sit alongside moments of intense human emotion, cinematic images stay with you long after the credits roll, and the city itself feels like a fully realized character.Visually, this film is an absolute triumph. Every shot - from the riotous colors of Carnival to the shadows of a hunted man - is composed with painterly care. The score, set design, and soundscape work together to create something that feels both timeless and uniquely anchored in history. And let's talk about Wagner Moura. His portrayal is mesmerizing, anchoring a sprawling story with emotional clarity, charm, and aching humanity. He is the film, commanding every scene with quiet power.In a world of predictable narratives, The Secret Agent is a rare beast - ambitious, thought-provoking, visually striking, and deeply felt. It doesn't just entertain-it transforms the viewer. An instant classic.
author: ongoam

title: Best of the 2020's
text: The best scene in any movie from 2025 is in this film. The scene in question is the chase while "A Briga do Cachorro com a Onça" plays. Impressive script, real and surprising suspense, and a perfect soundtrack. But what makes this film spectacular is that it's not ashamed of being a film. Unlike "I'm Still Here" or "Central Station," which seem more like a staged book, The Secret Agent isn't ashamed of being a film and uses everything a film can offer. Music, camera work, parallel stories (like the telegram journey), split screen-all of this elevates the film to a higher level, a level that only great names can reach, like Scorsese and Tarantino (which is why people might find similarities in the filmographies of these three).The first time I saw the film I didn't like the ending, but after days of thinking about it I changed my mind. The ending is perfect, in dialogue with everything the film has been dealing with up to that point.The only little thing that bothered me was the fact that one of the villains got away with it. However, this morbid desire doesn't align with what the film discusses, so it's okay, especially since Kleber Mendonça already satisfied this thirst for historical revenge in "Bacurau".The scene that proved to me that this is the best film of the last 5 years was the shootout and its subsequent chase. That's art. What an amazing scene. I couldn't stay in my seat. I had spasms and fits of laughter because I knew that was the pinnacle that the seventh art can reach. Few films manage to do that.Best film of 2025 without a doubt. And so far the best film of the 2020s.
author: RafayellowMelo

title: Colourful,engaging,humourous,poignant
text: In 1977 during Brasil's capitalist military dictatorship a dissident flees to Recife with hired killers on his tail. Writer/director Kleber Mendoca Filho's 2025 feature film is a Dutch/French/German financed Brasilian political thriller shot on location in my mother's hometown (the city of Recife), and featuring Wagner Moura as political dissident Armando, who returns to Recife to see his young son, search for records of his mother in the city archives, hide from his pursuers (under the name Marcelo) and arrange to escape abroad (like those Angolans planning to go to Sweden). Long (over two and a half hours), colourful, and engaging in places, there's a running theme of police corruption and brutality, as well as many knowing jokes and references about Recife and it's history from the director (a Recife native), including hairy legs attacking people (so that's what they call p.....), and shark attacks (which are apparently not unknown in the coastal waters of Recife), which must have made the film 'Jaws' an especially terrifying viewing experience for people in Recife. The poignancy of the story is brought home through the character of Flavia (Laura Lufesi), a young history student studying Brasil's murky past in the present day. This film also marked the last film role of German actor Udo Kier, who plays a political dissident from a different age - a German Jew called Hans. And of course there's that damn leg which just won't go away. Confounded sharks.

author: gutolm

title: Too Slow...
