author: patricio-henderson

title: I've fallen asleep twice.
text: I had a really hard time getting through 'The Secret Agent'. I fell asleep twice, and came dangerously close to a third time. Not even a decent dose of caffeine managed to keep me awake. It wasn't physical tiredness - I was actually feeling fine the day I watched it - it was that specific kind of drowsiness that appears when a film simply isn't engaging.From the very beginning, something didn't quite click with me. The production makes it clear right away that patience will be required, because everything suggests the story is going to simmer on a very, very low flame. That doesn't put me off; I like slow cinema. I don't mind when films take their time. The problem is that here, it just didn't work for me.The opening scenes aren't bad. They promise a strong direction, a deep, dense, high-quality script. But in the end, neither of those things really landed. There are a few isolated moments - very few - that managed to wake me up, especially when there's some movement on screen. But halfway through, the desperation sets in. That's when I completely disconnect. Everything starts to matter less and less.And I don't consider myself someone with a short attention span. I don't usually lose patience with slow-paced films. But here, my patience runs out early, mainly because the story doesn't grab me from the start.That's when I had to reread the synopsis. Not because the plot is complicated - it isn't - but because I was already outside the context and had no idea what was going on. I do manage to re-engage slightly during the last half hour, but it hardly matters when I've spent a third of the runtime mentally elsewhere, thinking about other things - like how much time is left.I assume this film is meant to carry weight. But honestly - and this is one of the main issues between me and 'The Secret Agent' - I can't figure out what it's trying to achieve. I have no idea what its purpose is: whether it wants to deliver a social or political statement, unsettle the viewer, open perspectives, stir consciences, point fingers, transform something, or simply expose reality.Maybe it feels revelatory to someone. Maybe it changes someone's life - which I highly doubt. But I can't find its practical point. I also don't think it's commercial in nature. My theory is that it aims to be pure "serious" cinema. Still, I can't find its emotional or intellectual core. And at some point, I stop caring. I don't even want to know anymore - I just want it to end. In fact, some scenes feel so unbearable to me that I actually start getting irritated.Quick story: I fell asleep for a few minutes because I literally preferred giving priority to sleep over this movie. My dad called to ask where something was. Already annoyed by how little I was enjoying the film, I answered him rudely: "I don't know, just look for it, it has to be somewhere."Obviously, speaking to my father like that was on me. But the point is: this movie put me in a bad mood. That night I even told my partner, "I was grumpy today because I watched an unbearable movie and it pissed me off."I admit it - and I accept it - maybe the problem is me. As a viewer, I didn't connect with it, despite finding it promising when I first saw it mentioned during awards season. I probably should give it a second chance, go in better prepared, more aware of what I'm seeing and hearing. But I don't want to revisit it for at least a couple of years. If I even remember it.I believe the first experience matters. Boredom - and the disconnection that follows - is also a valid experience. Wanting to check how much time is left because you're fighting sleep says a lot about the relationship between a film and its viewer.I can't say much about the plot, for obvious reasons, but from what I gathered, it's about a man who returns to a place that once meant a lot to him, hoping to start over and find happiness - only to realize it's no longer what it was, and he has to escape again. Beyond that, I can't draw many conclusions. And I might even be explaining it wrong, but that's all I managed to take in.I finished it. That much is true. With effort, but I made it to the end credits. I wanted to quit, but my desire to feel victorious for having completed it was stronger.And honestly, I don't think it's a bad film in terms of production. Technically, it's competent. I completely understand that some people like it - I just don't understand why.The music is another element that didn't convince me, even though it initially did. The performances are correct, functional, practical - but Wagner Moura, in my opinion, shouldn't be nominated. He doesn't excite me, doesn't pull me in, doesn't transmit much beyond a kind of superficial, performed fear. Maybe over time I'll come to accept him, but that would be in a very, very distant future.For now, I'm left with the feeling that it's an overrated work. And also with the idea that maybe I'm the problem.Or simply that it's not for me.Which is also possible.
author: chriss17eu

title: the secret agent
text: This very disturbing yet perversely entertaining film (very Hitchcockian that, no?) grabs you by the throat with the very first image of a rotting corpse covered by flimsy cardboard in the parking area of a Brazilian gas station in 1977 during Carnival and does not release you until almost three hours later, which in the skilled, suspenseful hands of director Kleber Mendonca Filho feels more like ninety minutes. Along the way you are treated to one of the most searing visions of Fascistic hell that I have ever seen depicted onscreen, replete with corrupt cops and business tycoons and their equally morally fallen sons, body parts in sharks' belllies (with "Jaws" playing in movie theatres, along with "The Omen", to pacify the masses), right wing, surreal visions of gays being terrorized by severed limbs in city parks at night, a Jew who escaped The Holocaust being humiliated by the corrupt cops, and three of the scummiest, scariest political assassins since the guy in "Z", a work to which this great film bears comparison, in my opinion. It would asll be insufferably hopeless and bleak were it not for The Resisters who gather in a safe house run by septugenarian Dona Sebastiana, a brave, wry, weary, wary bunch, chief among them the ironically named title character, a man whose wife has been murdered and who is now on the run with a contract out on him for the heinous sin of standing up to corporate authoritarianism. Brilliantly playing this character is Wagner Moura who brings new meaning to the phrase "quiet intensity". Without once raising his voice or engaging in camera mugging he manages to convey the fear, anger, and sheer indomitability of all those opposed to being pushed around by the powers that be, from Recife '77 to Minneapolis '26. He deserved the Oscar nomination he received and I hope he wins. Ably supporting Moura are a gallery of fine performances my five faves being, in no particular order, Tania Maria as the spry, seventy something safe house owner, Gabriel Leone, Roney Villela and Kaiony Venancio as the creepy killers and Udo Kier as the hounded Holocaust survivor.In short, a stunning work that I'm sure I will revisit several times. My only criticism and the reason I give it a 9 rather than a 10 is that the flashback structure, with present day Brazilians delving into the past, is un-necessary and serves to slow an otherwise perfectly paced film. Give it an A minus.
author: mossgrymk

title: Confounding, inscrutable, talky...but a handsome piece of work
text: Co-production from Brazil-France-Germany-Netherlands (in Portuguese and German, with subtitles) is a great-looking but confounding film, its scattershot narrative made to seem even more inscrutable by the picture's length, an astounding 161mns. Wagner Moura stars as a widower-professor and the absentee father of a little boy in 1977 Brazil who's on the run; seems his past as an opponent to the Brazilian military dictatorship got he and his late wife in hot water, and now he's warned that he may be marked for assassination. Writer-director-co-producer Kleber Mendonça Filho is in love with narrative, and he gives us an onslaught of characters, character detail and minutiae to dress his story. Moura, under an assumed name, gets a job in the Documents department at what appears to be the local police station in Recife, a city in Pernambuco state; there, he's determined to find the identity card for his late mother, whose name is either too common or too complicated for him to locate it in the files. We meet his little boy, who is living with his maternal grandparents and is fascinated by sharks and the movie ads for "Jaws"--there's also been a tiger shark attack on a swimmer in which a severed leg has been pulled from the bowels of the shark and has become something of a source of amusement due to write-ups in the newspaper (Mendonça Filho imagines "the hairy leg" coming back to life and attacking gay men cruising at night in the park!). "The Secret Agent" (its title apparently inspired by a British TV show starring Patrick McGoohan that the little boy is watching) is a talky but handsome piece of work: Evgenia Alexandrova's cinematography is gorgeous, the ambiance is narcotizing, and the filmmaker's love of music, local color and cinema is enjoyable. The pacing does pick up in Part 3 of the story (with bursts of violent action), while the epilogue set in the present day makes an attempt to bring everything together. However, personal taste will have to decide if the film is really worth all the accolades it's been given. For cinephiles in love with neo-noirs and political thrillers, Mendonça Filho may just be their new Antonioni. ** from ****
author: moonspinner55

title: Meh
text: The Secret Agent (O Agente Secreto) review:This is the 8th Best Picture nominee I've seen this year and the first I really didn't enjoyI'm usually good reading closed captions but to begin with I barely followed the plot so if there was tension and intrigue I missed it. Plus the cinematography is so dark at times I could barely see what was happening or who was even in the scene. Plus there were the a few time jumps that weren't obvious (are we in the present now or back in 1977?).I gave this 4 out of 10 stars. Honestly, I only finished it because I'd paid $15 to rent it and timed my workouts these past 2 mornings to cover the 2 1/2 hour film.
author: mchl88

title: Disappointing
text: As a Brazilian, I had high expectations for this one, but now I'm sitting here trying to understand how the hell this movie got several nominations. Not to mention Wagner Moura, who delivers a mediocre, flat performance. The only truly positive aspect is the visuals - the art direction is very nice.
author: Jana2046

title: For now the best film of the year
text: For now, I consider it-perhaps tied with No Other Choice-the best film of the year. I didn't find it boring at all, contrary to what I'd heard; on the contrary, every part has its purpose, and the storytelling is engaging, making you want to know more and more until it reaches what is, in my opinion, a wonderful ending. I don't think it should have been shorter, but some parts could have been trimmed to make it even more seamless. Also, I would have liked to delve deeper into the stories of certain characters like Dona Sebastiana, played by Tânia Maria, or Hans, a character I absolutely loved. Obviously, it's impossible not to mention the protagonist Wagner Moura, who plays Armando and another character I can't talk about to avoid a huge spoiler. From a technical standpoint, it is undeniable: the directorial choices are truly interesting and the cinematography is beautiful, with tones leaning towards yellow. The staging and production design are extremely accurate. In certain scenes, the film is raw and vividly portrays the reality of Brazil in those years; even without a deep knowledge of this historical period, the movie conveys how common situations like murders were and effectively describes the context of the Brazilian military dictatorship. Also noteworthy is Luciano Chirolli's performance as a hateful and selfish character who acts only in his own interest. I'd like to talk more about this film, but I don't have time to write a more extensive review; this is my general take. I wouldn't call it a masterpiece just yet: it's one of those movies you need to see at least five times to define it precisely. That said, go see it in theaters while you can, because it is an important and beautiful piece of work.
author: tascopaolo

title: A boring film where nothing happens
text: With a title like The Secret Agent - you're expecting a dramatic spy film, or at least some kind of action. With 2 hours and 40 minutes, "the action"- if you can even call it that - happens toward the end of the film after sitting through a LOT of talking heads. The film has some neat little motifs, sharks being a huge motif. I found the main character flat and uninteresting, and I found his relationship with the villain of the film to be kind of boring. There's so many parts of this film that don't make sense, or seem to be lacking in purpose, and they are, there isn't a purpose to a lot of parts of this film. Setups but no payoffs. A disaster of a film. Expect to be disappointed.
author: ultrevor

title: 3 boring hours I'll never get back
text: Possibly one of the most boring films I've ever seen. Totally useless scenes that add nothing to character or plot development, not shot in a particularly interesting way. This film tries to be mysterious and intriguing but fails because there's too much fluff and no one part of the story is truly developed well. Basically a university student's ego piece that got funding. Best part was the vintage cars.
author: rattay-83205

title: If this is Brazilian cinema in its best, I am not a fan
text: The movie felt like every movie dealt with the oppressed with such a weak storytelling, I see the corrupt and understand it as I live in a third world country, but come on, I saw many movies that adapted that idea way way, better.And sorry but Wagner Moura is no best actor winner, a nominee of course, but not a winner at all, would be upsetting to me, and the score, come on, what is the mosquito sound or whatever, it is not interesting.I understand that people may like it as they liked ABBA as it is like the resistance and everything, but come on, there is not even a comparison between both.I was going to watch I am still here for Fernanda Torres but after watching this, no way.Sorry, but Brazilians overhype their movies like it is a treasure while they are still starting their path to great movies.Sorry but no way that is the best international movie in a year full of many very well and better international movies with better stories and plots.
author: sherifsaleh-55206

title: I got the idea. I just expected more
text: Really well made. Technically sharp, great visuals, music, camera work, strong acting. That deep dive into 1970s Brazil is impressive - everything feels authentic, lived-in, convincing. Not a single false note. I was there with the characters, inside the moment, breathing the same air.But... that pacing. Slow is fine. Slow can be great. Here it's slow and opaque. For most of the film something is happening, but you have no idea what - no clues, no orientation, just mystery stacked on mystery. The suspense is insanely thick, you could cut it with a knife.And then, when you finally get closer to the "solution" (not even fully), you realize the story itself isn't that interesting. Yes, there's personal drama, but not on the scale the movie spent two hours preparing you for. Maybe it's intentional, but I still felt disappointed. At some point the slowness plus confusion turns into boredom - you stop caring. And when the reveal comes and it's not a big deal... you feel tricked.I got the idea. I just expected more.
author: Jelisei

title: Good political historical thriller
text: This is no where near my favorite movie from Kleber Mendonça Filho, but Filho still remains as one of the best Brazilian filmmakers in our times, and The Secret Agent is a good, neo-noir and slow-burn thriller with fantastic atmospheric direction choices, writing choices, and strong performances.Throughout, Filho establishes the time period with some fantastic colorful presentation, atmosphere and camerawork. As it helps bring the time era of the 1970s, and the warm and gritty nature that the characters are experiencing and witnessing. What Filho successfully does with his movie is offer some interesting characters and developments. Where his characters are both conflicting and engaging, as he is able to create these characters to feel real, diverse, and real sense of emotions and perspectives. Alongside some good costume designs, practical effects, and sound designs, all the performances from the cast members are fantastic. Wagner Moura absolutely gives one of his best performances in his entire career, as Moura has so much range with his character and role.Although the writing does have some cliche neo-noir thriller tropes with characters and some plot devices, Filho's writing still remains engaging because there are some new concepts that he focuses on within the setting, and it does feel refreshing and interesting. Although, some of the characters didn't really work very well as they did feel somewhat thin or not as emotionally connectable as it could have been. With the same for the runtime, the runtime, at times, doesn't feel justified as some writing moments and pacing did feel somewhat repetitive.Overall, it's a good Brazilian thriller.
author: peter0969

title: Poignant and gripping drama
text: This incredible award nominated drama is definitely very much deserving of all the praise it has gotten. Very thrilling, poignant and indeed very important.The actors all do an incredible job, conveying a complex, yet focused and nerve tickling tale that keeps you on the edge of your seat. Beautifully written and paced indeed, and all accompined by a stellar script, that is very true indeed.The cinematography, cutting and editing is great, featuring a very 70s look the the best of ways, very beautifully put together, to say the least.Overall, an incredible Oscars nominee, very intriguing, well acted and all around stellar, a must-see for any lover of film!
author: martinpersson97

title: Gorgeous looking twisty story that tries your patience a little
text: This was not what I expected it to be and it misled me for quite a while as to what kind of story it was. Let me just say it had a really strong beginning with that scene at the gas station and the police and really set the mood for these very mad and corrupt times in Brazil's history. I also thought Marcelo was a taxi driver cause of the yellow car so imagine my surprise when he reveals his past in the middle of the movie. Cause I didn't pay attention to the synopsis. I also love the look of the movie and the gorgeous yellow tints and the vintage cars (for us).I would say its only flaw is the pacing and the length. It does stall sometime in the first hour and takes a while to pick up the pace or pique your interest again. But when it does it's really gripping. And the last half hour turns into a full-on action movie, followed by a quiet but even more impactful ending. Funny thing memory, Fernando doesn't remember and the girl cares more than he does. Also a terrible thing for us to find out Armando's fate from that picture but I think it would've hurt more to see it enacted so it's better that way.What is it with these long South-American movies? They're good but... Anyway, can't watch much else on an evening besides this. That is it.
author: lilianaoana

title: The death of identity can be more devastating than physical death (SPOILER)
text: The Secret Agent is a film that trusts its audience. It trusts silence, time, atmosphere and, above all, the viewer's ability to read what is not explicitly stated. This review contains spoilers, because discussing this film honestly requires engaging with its ambiguities.Set during Brazil's dictatorship, the film is not interested in reconstructing historical events in a direct or didactic way. Instead, it focuses on their collateral effects. Not the great political gestures, but the quiet consequences that infiltrate ordinary lives, families and intimate decisions. Authoritarianism here does not shout. It organizes, registers, archives and erases.From its very first scene, the film establishes its moral logic. In a gas station, ordinary citizens are subjected to routine control. Someone is erased in plain sight, and no one reacts. The priority is not the violence itself, but compliance. Even when everything is right, something still must be surrendered. This opening functions as a precise analogy for everything that follows.Wagner Moura delivers a restrained, deeply human performance. His character is not a conventional hero, but a man who carries fear in his body, his gaze and his breathing. The careful construction of characters, production design and the deliberate use of silence create an oppressive world without excess or spectacle.One of the film's most powerful ideas is that the loss of identity is, in itself, an extreme form of violence. The ambiguity surrounding Armando's fate is central. The altered photograph shown at his supposed death, the role of the identity agency, the erasure of memory and the film's title all suggest a possibility more disturbing than physical death. Survival through complete erasure.This reading gains even more strength when we consider the fate of the child. The son does not remember his father. He states calmly that the only father he ever had was his grandfather. There is no visible trauma, no indignation, only adaptation. This absence of mourning points not to a clear death, but to a conscious disappearance. A continuity achieved at an absolute moral cost.The same ambiguity surrounds the death of the mother. To the child, Armando speaks of illness. Later, in a private conversation with the grandfather, the line she was so healthy quietly dismantles that explanation. Her disappearance feels linked to the same machinery of persecution and silence. Another identity erased, another story that cannot be told.Even the accusation from the grandfather, whether Armando had been running around, is not about infidelity, but about displaced guilt. When structural violence cannot be named, suspicion turns inward, toward the private and the moral. The system vanishes. Relationships absorb the damage.The most devastating moment comes in the child's letter, where he writes that he has already forgotten his mother. There is no rage, only relief and functional calm. Love in this film exists beyond memory, beyond recognition, beyond record. Protection does not require being remembered.In this light, The Secret Agent is not merely a film about dictatorship. It is a film about the ethics of protection in a world where existing can be dangerous. About a father who accepts not to die, but to stop existing symbolically, so that his child may live in safety.The film refuses closure. It refuses comfort. Its genius lies in allowing multiple readings without invalidating any of them. When a film explains everything, it ends before the credits roll. When it leaves space, it continues to grow inside the viewer.This is not just a well-told story.It is an experience that remains unfinished by design.
author: rodrigoperrini-70524

title: "It's better not to ask too many questions."
text: The Secret Agent maintains a constant sense of tension despite its long runtime and restrained pacing. Not because something is always happening, but because you remain alert throughout. Conversations feel casual, situations seem ordinary, yet nothing feels accidental. There is a constant sense that a wrong moment could have consequences.The title is deliberately misleading at first. It suggests a classic spy film, clear roles, familiar patterns. That is not what the film delivers. Instead of agent work, it focuses on observation, caution, and adaptation. Expectations are not fulfilled but shifted and that is exactly where the tension comes from.A central aspect is the use of language. The film shows very concretely how communication worked under the military dictatorship. It was not possible to speak openly about police violence or repression. Newspapers relied on codes, circumlocutions, images. Words stood for something else. Messages were disguised. This is exactly how the film operates. Meanings are not placed openly on the surface but hidden within the everyday. Knowledge is dangerous. Silence becomes a survival strategy.This also explains the title. The Secret Agent does not refer to a classic agent, but to people who are forced to move within a system of codes and control. Anyone can become part of it, consciously or not.Wagner Moura plays in a restrained and controlled manner, without pushing himself to the foreground. The film's quiet standout, however, is Dona Sebastiana, played by Tânia Maria. She brings lightness into the film without breaking its tension. No exaggeration, no comic relief. Just a character who works and gives the film room to breathe.The supporting roles are also precisely placed. Characters appear, disappear again, leave traces without being explained. Everything aligns with a feeling of control and latent threat. The historical context of the military dictatorship is present at all times, but never didactic.The Secret Agent is a political thriller that does not rely on action, but on mechanisms. On language, restraint and what cannot be said. The film is more complex than its title initially suggests and that is exactly where its strength lies.
author: FilmNotesByDen

title: It kept me hooked and curious right until the very end
text:
It's 1977 in Brazil, and times are grim. They've managed to capture such a raw, natural vibe that it feels completely unscripted -even with all the gritty absurdities: a thief's body left where he fell for days, a leg swallowed whole by a shark, a two-headed cat, and a mob-style execution by a reservoir.Safe to say, this is a world away from the Brazil you see at the Rio Carnival :)) I couldn't help but notice how many people looked strikingly Turkish :)
It kept me hooked and curious right until the very end. (77/100)
author: nikromcelik

title: Decent Movie
text:
Rating - 6.7:
Overall, 'The Secret Agent' is a decent movie that has a noticeable performance from Wagner Moura and a strong ending, but the movie is tonally flat and requires you to understand the Brazilian regime of the 1970s because, without it, you will be incredibly lost for the first half of the movie; the pacing also is a major issue issue for this movie because it is just too long and does not do a good job filling the space, so 'The Secret Agent' would be a stronger movie if they cut the first half in half.Direction - Decent:
The direction on a macroscale is tonally not the best because it feels like the movie requires you to know a lot of context ahead of time, and if you do not, you are kind of just lost for the first half of the movie; In the second half, the macroscale is better when they explain it more, but it still does not make up for the confusion for the most part; The direction on a microscale is not really the best for the first half of the movie, but it does end well with the final 30 minutes when you start to grasp these relationships and their significance; Storytelling is a major issue for this movie because the first half is just very vague about what is going on and only starts to explain in the second half; The storytelling style only works if you have context for the situation beforehand because it just makes the experience very confusing and convoluted as you try to put pieces together; Tension is built very poorly as the lack of score for the most part fails to build tension, though it does fix this in the final 30 minutesStory - Pretty Bad:
The concept only works if you have context for the Brazilian regime of the 1970s as they only start explaining the situation by the second half, so if you are not invested by this point, it would be hard to get back into the movie; The plot structure is pretty unique, with the first half being exposition that does not give anything and the second half providing the context to make the first half make more sense, with constant jumps to the present and an ending that puts a bow on the whole thing; Character writing is not good because of how intentionally vague they are with the storytelling, as it requires the audience to piece things togetherScreenplay - Decent:
The dialogue is pretty good; The humor is pretty good; The symbolism is pretty good as it shows the impact that fascism and corruption played in Brazil at the time, but it just takes a little too long to get out the message as you have to piece things together; The foreshadowing is set up to be good but is not well executed because the first half is vague exposition that only makes sense in the second half, and this setup did not work well because it did a poor job of alluding to the actual conflict; This foreshadowing style only works if you have some grasp of what this movie is about or know about the Brazilian regime of the 1970sActing - Pretty Good:
Wagner Moura - Pretty Good to Good (Plays the lead role well and does show some range, but nothing really wowed me with this performance (which is a shame because I really like him as an actor)), Tânia Maria - Pretty Good (Plays her role well and has good on screen presence), Rest of the cast - Pretty Good (Everyone plays their role well and the cast as a whole has good chemistry)Score - Bad to Pretty Bad:
A major issue as to why this movie feels tonally off is that it is scarcely used to help build tension when it needs to, creating dead space and making this movie feel bland when it is supposed to excite; The score does a pretty good job in the final 30 minutes of setting the tone, but at this point, it is just too lateCinematography - Pretty Good:
The film is well shot and does track and follow the characters in a way that keeps you somewhat engagedEditing - Pretty Good:
The editing is seamlessly put together and feels polishedSound - Pretty Bad to Decent:
The sound does not do the best job of building tension as the movie feels tonally off, but it does a decent job in the final 30 minutesVisual Effects - Pretty Bad:
The whole leg scene just felt completely out of place, even if there is symbolism behind it for what the leg representsPacing - Pacing is a major issue for this movie because it is just too long and does not do a good job of filling the space, as the first half is just void of anything to keep you interested; If the first half were cut in half, this movie would be much strongerClimax - The climax is executed well and does make you feel like the journey was somewhat worth it as it provides closure to the story, and I really liked how they ended it the way they didTone - Tonally, this movie is just off as a whole as it does not do a good job of building up tension in the thriller moments or making you care for the protagonist or the investigation.
author: cinemapersonified

title: TWIST DE POBRE É MACUMBA
text: One of the best cinematic experiences I've ever had.When I read the synopsis, I truly believed it would be a story of suffering, a context that already made me anxious before the screening - repression and corruption during Brazil's military dictatorship. However, I faced this personal fear and received a gift. From the beginning of the story, the characterization, the attitude of each character, the narrative, the mystery, the strangeness, the action, the soundtrack, and the editing were, for me, impeccable.If the direction of "The Secret Agent" hadn't been so sublime, perhaps I wouldn't have been curious enough to see the film. Regarding the acting, Wagner Moura gave way to Marcelo/Armando - perhaps this type of actor is one of those very rare chameleons. Honestly, he was totally human, courageous, respectful for keeping the premise. Mourning permeated the entire film, but despite that, I laughed and became so immersed that. So for the first time, the pain and suffering were presented in such a way that I could evaluate it as a spectator, understand the context, and not be haunted.However, the story of the leg that came to life, in my opinion, could have been presented differently - I have trypophobia, and when I realized what could happen, I managed to avoid a serious panic attack in the movie theater. On the other hand, the description of the fantasy, the analogy of it being a leg that shouldn't be found and being the left one, is a precise touch of fantasy in a bang and suspense film.As for the reconstructed Recife setting, I was dazzled, knowing that everything was meticulously planned and what a perfect view from that window. Finally, I really hope the film wins at the BAFTAs, the Oscars, the César Awards, and any other awards it's nominated for, because it has the caliber for it, and the recognition, in my view, would be more important for the academies than for the film itself.Furthermore, I want to congratulate Kleber Mendonça Filho for his sensitivity in including elements like the phrases on the truck mudguards, for example. This is because my grandfather and father were always truck drivers, and it's a very undervalued profession worldwide."For poor's twist is macumba"AK.
author: also109

title: Secrets out
text: No pun intended - the movie is different than I expected. And my expectation purely and solely were based on the title of the movie. So I had no idea about the real persons and or had seen a trailer about it.So the title was suggesting something more ... well mysterious, with ... you know "Agents". But lo and behold that is not the case. We have a regular Joe (Jose? What would be the Brazilian counterpart - well no pun intended and you catch my drift) - who kind of is witness to certain things.You'd think nothing too spectacular ... but what do we know? So many great characters in this other than our main one. The Esso gas station employee ... funny yet dramatic in a sense. Also an older woman later on - she is a scene stealer! Considering the weight of the movie (there is a sense of dread and danger always looming above it all, be it smudge/stain of blood on a shirt or other not so obvious "clues") ... it is quite light most of the time. Many jokes - and we are always with our "hero" - except from a few times when we jump forward in time - there is a connection to be made. We'll understand at the end ... and we'll be sweating up to that point too - will it have a happy end? If you already know the story, that question is not one you ask yourself I reckon ... but even if, I think it is told well enough so you can just immerse yourself in it.
author: kosmasp

title: A colorful and powerful brazilian film
text: Kleber Mendonça Filho, the director, is at the top of his game in this film, which I think is his masterpiece. I've always had a hard time watching his movies, which are more about the visuals than the talking. There's always too much hidden meaning and not enough in the actual dialogue. Maybe I'm just not good at grasping the full depth of his beautiful, very Brazilian works. But this film, which I saw in the cinema, hit me differently.The movie title might throw you off, but trust me, there's no secret agent. It's about people fighting for survival during a military dictatorship, trying to hide their pasts. You see corruption everywhere, police who don't care about human life, and a lot of bureaucracy. It's not that different from today, but amplified by the political regime. But despite all that, you still see the vibrant colors, beauty, and joy of Brazilians, even in tough times. Like someone else said, few films capture Brazil as well as this one.The cinematography is gorgeous, full of color. The historical accuracy is spot on. The script is sharp. The directing is confident.And the performances from the carefully chosen cast are probably the best part of the film.Even the plot twists, some scattered stories, and the bloody, Tarantino-esque violence at times, really surprised and impressed me, since / wasn't expecting that kind of movie.So, about the kinda sad ending, Wagner Moura was very clear in his Golden Globe speech: the movie's all about holding onto memories. So many stories and lives got lost during that time in Brazil. Swept under the rug. No win here, just a realization.Honestly, I can't find anything wrong with the movie to knock off a star, which is rare for me. The mix of how much fun I had and the perfect filmmaking I saw just screams for a top grade.I think the only reason someone would be bummed out by this movie is if they took the title too literally, thinking it was a thriller or something. Don't fall for that.Long live Brazil. Long live the Brazilian people. And let's always remember our history.
author: gcatharino

title: Secret lives
text: The new film from one of today's most intriguing South American directors is a collection of memories, experiences and emotions from the dark periods of Brazilian history. I should mention that I am not Brazilian.Starting from the opening sequence, which sets the tone of the film, we are introduced to the atmosphere of Brazilian society in the 1970s. A human body rotting in the sun at a gas station, torn apart by stray dogs and ignored by police who are only interested in bribes. Even a pack with a few cigarettes is enough...This is not a thriller, as the film's title might suggest, but rather a depiction of the human condition in a paranoid and corrupt world, where a human leg is found in the belly of a shark, a police station is adjusted just to make a privileged person comfortable, and where a hairy leg attacks and kills people. Yes, hairy leg !!!The story is shown without filters, sometimes is unpleasant, but with a lot of love for the ordinary Brazilians, whose spirit radiates from the screen and credit for that goes to the extraordinary cast led by magnetic Wagner Moura.One might get the impression that the characters lack depth, but on the other hand, this is not an introspective film, but rather an observational film, where many things are said between the lines, with silence.Overall, this great film is a valuable chronicle, with many textures, and like a music album that you listen to occasionally, it can be watched multiple times, enriching your experience with each viewing.
author: martinodjak

title: What Didn't Work for Me
text: The Secret Agent was disappointing overall. The storytelling is weak, and the movie takes too long to become interesting. Many scenes feel unnecessary, especially the sex scenes, which don't add anything to the story. The ending is also frustrating because it doesn't clearly show what happens to the main character, leaving the film feeling unfinished. It had potential, but it never really delivers.
author: kemilfer

text: A film that understands espionage not as action, but as a permanent state of distrust. Here, danger doesn't flee, but observes you.The script transforms the "agent" into an almost abstract figure, closer to a social symptom than a hero. The true suspense lies not in what will explode, but in what is never said, in what remains off-screen, in what everyone pretends not to know.The direction operates within the everyday discomfort amidst corridors, listening devices, long silences, and a constant feeling of diffuse surveillance. Nothing is urgent, but everything is threatening, like the country's own recent history.In the end, Secret Agent doesn't seek adrenaline. It seeks to remind us that, in certain places, the greatest secret is surviving without drawing attention.
author: gustabene

title: Weird Movie
text: This disjointed, overly long film doesn't get straight to the point of what it sets out to do. As a result, it drags at the beginning. There are political critiques within this film, but only a generic characterization of the villain representing the right wing of capitalism ("the Eletrobras Company"), without delving into their motives, and, on the other side, the "purity" of the left-wing political spectrum that simply wants to improve the world through its scientific research.
author: RafaelM-644

title: Spy Title, Political Pain
text: This became one of only two or three Brazilian films I have ever watched. The title is a bit misleading. Honestly, I expected a spy movie about a real secret agent, but it is not really related to that. I do not know exactly what Brazil was like during that period, but based on one or two articles I read, it seems somewhat close to reality. I cannot say how accurate the portrayal is, but it was still very impactful. The cast and acting were excellent. It was interesting to see performances from actors of different nationalities. The story itself was engaging, but the pacing and execution felt a bit slow and sometimes weak. Even though I was affected by the film, the way the story was told was a little too slow for my taste. Still, I appreciated it. Wagner Moura may seem like he gives a simple performance, but he was actually great. His Oscar nomination feels well deserved. The subtle discomfort in his face while smiling, or the expression in his eyes, was very impressive. The other actors were also excellent. I did not like the ending. It was not satisfying to me, honestly. But even though the film was slow and occasionally boring, aside from the ending, I liked it overall.
author: jack_o_hasanov

title: A Love and Hate Letter to 1970s Brazil
text: Anyone going in to see The Secret Agent expecting thrills and high-octane action will be sorely disappointed. By design, the film is everything a spy movie is not. It is, instead, a love and hate letter to 1970s Brazil.Across a 2-hour-and-40-minute journey, director-writer-producer Kleber Mendonça Filho painstakingly captures vivid memories of a time when living under political oppression felt like breathing in air and letting it out.Unfolding at a leisurely pace, the several narrative strands and non-linear structure make it feel like we are peeking into old photo albums of total strangers. Interjected with unexpected bursts of dark humor, the film owes its alluring charm to magnetic performances by the entire cast and a meticulous attention to the smallest details of a bygone era.By deliberately avoiding the tropes of a political thriller, The Secret Agent ends up delivering a more piercing and unflinching political commentary. It is a profound testament to how love manages to survive amidst hate, turmoil, and oppression.
author: AsianLensPlus

title: Disappointing & Boring
text: Only decided to watch the movie because the main actor used to have a pretty good role in the TV series Narcos not that long ago. But the storyline and directing of this movie is very disappointing. I watched the first 30 minutes and just couldn't continue anymore because I'm confused on what the story is about and where the movie is going. Lots of random pieces without any cohesiveness or attraction especially to someone who's not familiar with that era or that area of the world. Pretty boring to say the least.
author: HighlanderABC

title: A Film of Exceptional Form and Unresolved Meaning
text: The Secret Agent is a film that immediately commands attention through its formal mastery. From the very first frames it convincingly recreates the atmosphere of 1970s Brazil with remarkable precision. The production design and costume work feel historically authentic rather than decorative and the cinematography reinforces this illusion with disciplined framing and a classical visual language that rarely draws attention to itself yet remains consistently striking.Several sequences stand out as pure cinema. The chase sequence is tightly constructed and rhythmically precise creating real tension without relying on excessive editing. The framing and mise en scène throughout the film show a director fully in control of the frame and confident in visual storytelling. In purely formal terms the film is often excellent.Almost every actor delivers a believable portrayal, responding to the tense political and social environment with subtlety and restraint. The interactions feel lived-in and authentic, creating a tangible sense of reality within the stylized historical setting. That said, Wagner Moura, while consistently present and controlled in his performance, does not reach the same level of nuance as some of best performances we witnessed this year as an actor in leading role . His portrayal is solid and reliable but lacks the subtle shifts and emotional depth that might have elevated the film's most intimate moments.The character of Dona Sebastiana is one of the film's strongest achievements. She feels alive layered and grounded in social reality. Unlike many other figures in the film she carries a clear sense of personal history and presence and every scene involving her feels purposeful and emotionally anchored.However where the film begins to struggle is in its narrative structure. The story repeatedly withholds crucial information without providing a clear dramatic or thematic justification. Key power dynamics remain vague. Motivations are implied rather than established and major events unfold without sufficient causal clarity. This does not create productive ambiguity but instead results in narrative gaps that weaken emotional engagement.The film's political dimension is visually persuasive but dramatically underdeveloped. Power is shown as omnipresent yet rarely explained. Characters with immense influence appear and act without the film clearly establishing who they are or how their authority functions. As a result political tension exists more as atmosphere than as narrative force.The time jumps and fast forward sections further complicate the experience. While they suggest a thematic contrast between past repression and a calmer present the execution is confusing. The decision to cast the same actor in multiple generational roles without meaningful physical or psychological differentiation undermines any intended statement about historical change. Rather than clarity the result is interpretive noise.The ending gesture in which Marcelo turns rival groups against each other is conceptually intriguing and visually effective. It suggests an understanding of how power fragments responsibility and manufactures internal conflict. Yet even this idea is left underexplored and lacks sufficient narrative groundwork to fully resonate.Ultimately The Secret Agent feels like a film that prioritizes form mood and historical texture over narrative resolution. It succeeds powerfully as an aesthetic and atmospheric experience but falls short as a coherent political drama. Its ambiguities feel less like deliberate artistic choices and more like unresolved writing decisions.This is a film to admire and to question often at the same time.
author: mehdimellatdoust

title: A Listening Film in an Age of Noise
text: O Agente Secreto, directed by Kleber Mendonça Filho, arrives less as a thriller than as a patient act of excavation. A work that resists spectacle, preferring instead the slow pressure of atmosphere, memory, and political residue. This is a cinema of vigilance-one that watches as much as it withholds.The screenplay's apparent minimalism is deceptive. What is left unsaid carries the weight of censorship, fear, and complicity. Dialogue becomes an echo chamber where language is constantly surveilled, never fully free.Accordingly, the narrative does not advance through twists or revelations, but through accretion. Information gathers like dust, like layers of sediment, until the viewer senses the weight of what has accumulated. The screenplay's apparent minimalism is therefore deceptive. What initially seems sparse or restrained gradually reveals itself as dense with implication. Dialogue often skirts around its own subject, pauses linger uncomfortably, and silence assumes a semantic weight equal to speech. Meaning is never handed over; it must be inferred, assembled, and sometimes suspected rather than known.At the center of this carefully modulated world stands Wagner Moura, whose performance has been widely singled out for its internal gravitas. A man under continuous negotiation with his surroundings. He embodies a subject shaped less by decisive action than by the necessity of caution, a man whose interior life has been disciplined by fear and history.The film refuses to treat the past as something concluded or safely archived. Instead, it seeps into the present, contaminating gestures, spaces, and relationships. History is not recalled; it persists. In this sense, the film becomes a meditation on how systems of control colonize subjectivity, shaping not only behavior but perception itself.The strength of this meditation is amplified by an ensemble cast that operates with remarkable cohesion. This is a clear example of how a well-structured and attuned group of actors can transform a film, creating a social texture in which no character feels incidental. Each presence contributes to a collective atmosphere of unease, reinforcing the idea that control is systemic rather than isolated.Embedded within this larger structure are the film's micro-narratives - small, seemingly peripheral stories that open sudden, abyssal depths: the war hero, the bizarre attack of the "hairy leg", the abandoned corpse, the divorced dentist, the disappeared former resident... These microcontos function as narrative fractures, brief windows into lives marked by absurdity, violence, or quiet despair. They do not advance the plot, yet they deepen the world, suggesting that behind every surface lies another story, equally unresolved and equally haunted.Equally striking is the film's metanarrative dimension, in which cinema itself becomes a living component of the story. A significant portion of the narrative unfolds inside a movie theater, literally situating the characters - and by extension, the audience - within a space of representation. In the midst of chaos, cinema appears as a refuge, but also as an ironic mirror: the horror film on screen is a caricature of the far more insidious terror of reality. The title makes this relationship explicit. The protagonist emerges as a kaleidoscopic variation of John Drake (Danger Man), a reminder that art distorts, refracts, and parodies life.In the end, O Agente Secreto leaves the viewer suspended in a state of unresolved awareness. It is not a film that seeks to explain or absolve, but one that insists on attention, on patience, and on the uncomfortable recognition that the past is never truly past. It watches us as we watch it, and in that mutual vigilance, it reveals the quiet, enduring violence of systems that never fully disappear.
author: luis-boaventura22

title: My review got deleted! I'm reposting it.
text: I'm Brazilian, politically on the left and a fan of Wagner Moura, but this movie is not good. Normally, I'm extremely tolerant when it comes to movies. I even like to watch bad movies, such as "Manos: The Hands of Fate", because they are so bad that they are good. "O Agente Secreto", however, is just plain bad. It's so bad that I even left the cinema in the middle of the film (something I almost never do). This movie is not only slow and waaaaay overlong, it also doesn't know what kind of movie it wants to be. The story is all over the place, with loads of blablabla, characters and situations that cutting them out would change nothing in the story, which lacks suspense anyway. Movies like this win prizes not because they are well-made, but because they are "exotic" (Europeans love that) and because of their political message. I wasted my time, but at least I didn't waste my money. It was a free ticket.PS: The movie's title is total nonsense.
author: Freethinker_Atheist

title: Leggless and Pointless for me
text:
My Review- The Secret Agent
In Cinemas now
My Rating 5/10It always puzzles me as to why foreign language movies are eligible for a double dip at the Oscar's to possibly win Best Movie in a Foreign Language as well as Best Movie of the year? So I did some research and the short answer was that the Academy's top prize has never had a language restriction. If a film meets the general eligibility it can compete for Best Picture regardless of language or country of origin.For decades, international films were effectively siloed or ignored so the Academy decided that allowing TV crossover of categories prevented the ghettoisation of non-English cinema signalling that world cinema is not "separate but equal" plus acknowledging that a masterpiece doesn't become "lesser" because it has subtitles.My question to the Academy is by including two foreign language movies as they have this year in the Best Film as well as Best foreign language creates a disadvantage to two of the best English language movies eg -Nuremberg or The Choral .Oh well , let's talk about The Secret Agent the highly praised Brazilian movie that's nominated for 4 Oscars this year including Best Picture, Best International Feature Movie , Best Actor for its star Wagner Mourer and Best Casting.If I was voting I wouldn't vote for The Secret Agent in any of those categories because I found it to long at 161 minutes, to disjointed and while realising the script may be better than the subtitles suggest not very interesting.Set in 1977 Brazil Wagner Mourer is a good choice for the role of Marcelo a technology expert who becomes a fugitive on the run.Hoping to reunite with his son, he travels to Recife during Carnival but soon realizes that the city is not the safe haven he was expecting.It may well have got lost in translation however it's completion from Norway Sentimental Value for me is a far superior movie in every way because I could identify with every character and every scene interested me .If anyone can explain the bizarre scene in this movie of a severed leg that was found in a sharks belly suddenly coming to life while kicking gay men in a park I'd appreciate finding an answer?The continual toing and froing back and forth made the timeline of the movie confusing for me and the conclusion after so many unnecessary scenes seemed rushed and unexplained.I've seen some wonderful Brazilian movies like the 1998 Central Station and the stunning I'm Still Here 2025 but I can't add The Secret Agent to my list.
author: tm-sheehan

title: An exceptional movie lost in translation
text: Even though I am Portuguese and familiar with Brazilian Portuguese, I watched *Agente Secreto* with English subtitles. The translation is very poor. For viewers who do not know Brazilian Portuguese idiomatic expressions, it will be very difficult to understand many of the dialogues, how rich they are, and to grasp the 1970s Brazilian atmosphere, which is essential to understanding the plot and the characters. Wagner Moura is an exceptional actor, and his performance in this film is absolutely memorable. I fear that many viewers will never be able to appreciate the enormous cinematic work that this film represents and Kleber Mendonça Filho's talent because of the difficulty in relating to the events and Brazilian reality of the 1970s, in addition to the language barrier.
author: jjntavares

title: A Misleading Title, Powered by Misleading Hype
text: If I could give this movie one star, I would (I can, but out of respect for Moura, I won't).I really wanted to like this film. I was extremely hyped before watching it, and I watched it attentively, yet it kept disappointing me minute after minute. The movie's potentially amazing story failed to deliver its quality for several reasons.First, the pacing is highly problematic. During the first half of the film, you're barely given any hints about what the story is actually about, and you're introduced to a variety of characters who add nothing to the plot or the main character's development. Then the film finally gets to the core of what happened, but even there you're given only incoherent fragments, with some parts making no sense whatsoever.And the finale completely ruined it for me. After so much back-and-forth, the main character's ending is one of the most underwhelming I've ever seen. I honestly felt betrayed and angry.The film is also far too long. It should have been at least an hour shorter and features about five characters more than necessary.All in all, one of the biggest disappointments of last/this year.
author: andreadjokickv

title: underwhelming and overrated
text: Heard great things about this movie but it's just a big mess. A visual spectacle that falls short in its story telling. Act 1 starts off with a compelling story but after two more acts you will still have the same questions you started with. Don't recommend putting almost 3 hours of your life onto this movie.
author: p-28597

title: Not for everyone - complicated, cagy, and dense. But worth it
text: I saw "The Secret Agent" on 01/28/2026 at the theaters. I went because I was curious about this movie's Oscar recognition: it was nominated for Best Picture and Best Actor ... altogether, it's a complicated, cagy, and dense work. But after making it to the end and reflecting on what I saw - I feel it was worth it.I'll begin with what I like -- the movie, on a technical level, is nice to look at. The color scheme is welcoming; I felt as if I could feel the 1970s Brazilian sunset through the screen. Wagner Moura's character drives a beautiful yellow car in the movie.I have no knowledge about Brazilian history, but the movie captures the rampant crime, corruption, and seediness of this country's era. The opening scene in the gas station might be the best scene in the movie. The end of the movie is not what I expected, but I found it moving and thoughtful; the message of the movie is worth understanding.All that said -the movie is like a labyrinth. There are all sorts of narrative shifts, digressions, and vagueness. The following often happened: the movie would show a sequence, I would get confused about something, a scene much later would occur to offer new details to clarify, and then I would have to recollect on the earlier scene to apply the new information to make that confusing earlier scene make sense ... does it sound hard to understand? It kinda is .... In short, the movie seemingly expects the viewer to unpack a lot of scenes long after those scenes have occurred in the 2 hr + 40 minute runtime. Some details go totally unexplained in the end.The movie is too long. I could've done without some scenes, like a bizarre leg subplot as well as a sequence involving a World War 2 veteran ... Some character development was necessary. The movie should've better established the father/son connection as well as the main character's trauma. About the latter, all the movie gives is one brief scene where he has a nightmare at night. The effect is that I felt as if I couldn't grasp the character's predicament ... on a minor note, there are some bad prosthetics in a certain shooting sequenceWagner Moura is serviceable as the lead, but I did not see Oscar-worthy. Most of the time, he merely conveys stoicism or blankness. He's not mind-blowingly good as some people say, and there are multiple actors whom I would've nominated for the Oscar instead6.5/10.
author: redban02

title: A compelling and masterfully crafted cinematic achievement.
text: Kleber Mendonça Filho's "O Agente Segreto" is a brilliant, genre-defying tour de force. It masterfully weaves sharp political critique with deeply human drama, creating a film that is both intellectually provocative and profoundly moving. The direction is confident and visionary, the screenplay is razor-sharp, and the performances are uniformly outstanding. It is a powerful, essential work that showcases the vitality and world-class artistry of Brazilian cinema. Fully deserving of its place among the Oscar contenders. He deserves to win.
author: Gleydson-3

title: Generic Drama but Great world.
text: The Secret Agent keeps throwing elements into a mix of emotional and political drama, but they don't stick together. The generic character arcs are left hanging as they are without a satisfactory closure. However, the world it's trying to build and sell works great, it is so bold that they don't even show us the years and just make the statement with its world's context for us to pick up.
author: yothisisyo

title: A Beautiful Nothingness
text: Impressive acting for the protagonist in his first film, apparently, beautiful colors, great locations, great production, and reconstruction of the 70's vibes, every single scene was made expertly.After a beautiful introduction to his life, what's up with this story? There are too many holes and mistakes; why lose half of the movie's time with the recording of the tape? I lost all connection with the movie at the halfway mark. I was almost shocked by how the movie started to go downhill. Such a waste of acting skills ruined by a story that still makes no sense to me. Was this taken from a book? If so, why did nobody think that was a boring story, and do something more original, even if the story is true, is a big pile of nothing?So much hard work for something that could be made for an episode of some thriller Series done in 25 minutes. People giving 10/10 are delusional. If this had a great story could have been a Brazilian masterpiece of cinematography. Let's hope the next film the director gets some compelling story to work with.

author: babydu-85006

title: Hyped movie
text: The film stands out primarily for its impeccable setting and carefully composed cinematography. The re-creation of 1977 Recife offers a convincing immersion into the dense, oppressive, and politically tense atmosphere of the dictatorship, with richly detailed environments and visual work that reinforces the constant sense of surveillance and paranoia. It's a film that immediately captivates through its aesthetic precision and striking imagery.The supporting cast is another strong point: secondary actors and actresses deliver intense performances, full of nuance and presence. However, the strength of these interpretations contrasts with the limited development the script grants them. There are visibly promising characters who end up underexplored, serving more as fragments of a larger universe than as fully realized figures within the narrative.The film's biggest stumble lies in its uneven script, which manages to build an initial sense of danger but gradually dismantles it as the story progresses and loses focus. This is compounded by a lead performance that, while competent, feels predictable: Wagner Moura, despite his undeniable charisma, repeats mannerisms already familiar from his previous roles. The final result is a technically brilliant film with powerful moments, but one that leaves the impression of not fully achieving the potential it seemed to promise.
author: thalisson-ovelar-243-406515

title: A great thematically rich timepiece
text: Brazil is in the spotlight right now with two movies. First, it was I'M STILL HERE. Now, it's the Golden Globe-winning historical neo-noir political thriller THE SECRET AGENT. While I'M STILL HERE took place in the South-East region of Rio, this one takes place in the North-East region of Recife. Both movies feature the nightmarish military dictatorship of the 1970's, but in different ways. Corruption, disregard for truth, and indifference to suffering are common in this time and place. It shows a dark piece of Brazilian history. The opening scene is a masterclass of suspense, setting the tone for what we're about to watch. It's a story about Marcelo (Wagner Moura), a man who's trying to lay low. I can't really say too much more about him, but he ends up living with people like him. I really enjoyed Dona Sebastiana, played by Tânia Maria. She takes him in and deserves her own movie. I enjoyed this experience, but not as much as a lot of movie critics. I just think maybe some things went above my head a bit. It has intentional ambiguity, perhaps too much for me, especially because I was busy reading subtitles. There's a subplot involving a human leg that's found inside a shark's mouth. There's a theme of how a government can steer the narrative in whatever way that most benefits them. The characters are fully fleshed out. Moura is a movie star, already winning a Golden Globe for this performance. This film is multifaceted. It's a love letter to cinema and the preservation of it. The preservation of culture itself. There was one storytelling choice I didn't really get, but I believe I'm getting close to. Overall, THE SECRET AGENT is an unconventional slow burn thriller that takes its time getting the audience used to its dangerous environment and time period. It's thematically rich and has amazing performances. It has been nominated for four Academy Awards: Best International Film, Best Actor for Moura, Best Casting, and Best Picture. This film is very important to Brazil and its people, as it tries to heal the country's fractured memory of a horrible dictatorship. We don't want history repeating itself, right?

title: PUTS CERTAIN OSCAR/BAFTA NOMINATED MOVIES TO SHAME
text: Just finished watching this extraordinary multi faceted Brazilan / Portuguese language movie which explores similar themes as the outrageously over-rated " ONE BATTLE AFTER ANOTHER " which was neither Feast or Famine and devoid of ... basically anything other than extreme over acting and little idea what its ambiguous story was meant to be about . This , however is a deeply considered movie set in the past in Brazil.which was emerging from the type of violation of democracy that the USA is sinking into . A better movie all round.
author: alanlamont-34154

title: Admire its novel approach, interesting pacing, and colourful characters, but it lacks tension and purpose.
text: If you are the type of person who sees a title like "The Secret Agent," and need that film to be a high intensity, tense spy thriller that has you at the edge of your seat...you probably will not like this film, I'll be honest with you.The Secret Agent is a surprisingly laissez-faire film; breezy and almost documentarian in pace, more of a slice of life film than anything else. On some level, I found that very refreshing. It's not the isolated, cold, "competence porn" audiences typically expect. Perhaps appropriately (being Brazilian), it's a lot more laid back and feels like a social drama, involving many characters of varying importance, who come in and out of our protagonist's life.The eponymous "secret agent," in this film isn't a reference to an actual secret agent, rather the sort of secretive, spy-like lives people are forced to live in an authoritarian system. When opposition becomes a crime, in effect, everyone becomes a secret agent.I really enjoyed the committment to the time period, the sets and the costumes, the performances in isolation are really great. The film plays around with the editing in ways that are both fun and fresh, and at other times, makes the narrative somewhat confusing.I love Wagner Moura in pretty much everything he's in, although - and I appreciate this may be because I don't speak Portuguese - I didn't find him *that* impressive.While I admire the film for taking such an interesting approach, I did find myself quite disconnected from the narrative for long stretches of the film. The characters and conflicts were just not interesting enough for me and I felt legitimately confused as to what the cause of the conflict was. The film does eventually lead to emotional intensity, but it came too late for me, personally.While I enjoyed The Secret Agent, it didn't blow me away and it's honestly not the best film about this time period I've seen.
author: ryanpersaud-59415

title: The lost side of humanity
text: "The Secret Agent" is Brazil's latest nomination for the Oscar's International Feature category. Filled with Brazilian northeastern 'ginga', the somewhat weird film has been claiming important awards ever since its 2025 Cannes debut. The main plot follows Marcelo, an on-the-run former teacher and tech expert that flies back to Recife - PE, where he will soon find out he is still in danger.Wagner Moura's portrayal is amazing and deserving of the worldwide acclaim he is receiving (even earned the 2026 Golden Globe for Best Actor). Filled with many non-actors, the splendid casting is also nominated for this new Academy Award category, and I must mention some of my favorite depictions: Dona Sebastiana, deputy Euclides and the gas station attendant. The cinematography and the production design deserve reverence as well. A perfect recreation of the city of Recife circa 1977, praised as accurate and very pretty to watch.The story mixes reality with local folklore references and even Roman mythology (Janus cat) whenever possible. Most of what is seen has no explanation or motive and the audience is left with many loose ends. Nonetheless, a few themes are constantly present, some with repetition. Did you notice death, blood, shark throughout the film? And the lack of explanation, would that be like real life, where so much happens without a noticeable reason or aim?It seems the director Kleber Mendonça Filho wanted to make the audience think about what was left unsaid, while showing us the cheap side of humanity, selling itself for money, corruption and power on all levels of society. Not much has changed since 1977, or ever mankind came to exist.
author: lyoneduardo

title: excellent acting common script
text: Wagner Moura, as always, embodies his characters and deserves to win an Oscar. The film's plot highlights a very dark period in Brazilian history without providing a direct historical deep dive. It portrays scars that we still carry today, such as corruption, the malfunctioning of the public administration, and impunity. Unlike I'm Still Here, which directly delves into the subject and the government's direct persecution, this film shows that there is no way to confront those who have the machinery of the state on their side.
author: JooP-8237

title: Fever dream (pejorative)
text: I don't know what I'd need to know about Brazil's military dictatorship period in order to grasp the purpose of this movie, if that would be helpful at all; clearly the director believes a point is being made here based on the camera constantly lingering on portraits of the president like it's a student film (something like Leviathan, a paragon of subtlety in comparison, merely includes a portrait in the shot), but judging the movie as a movie, not a visual companion to history textbooks or a vibe-focused autobiography, I thought this was atrocious.Being chauffeured around on this tour of a cornucopia of colorful Brazilian characters, all set in decently atmospheric locations, is entertaining enough for a while - that while, for me, lasted about 30 minutes, thereat I started to really wonder when the plot would begin in earnest. The answer is it takes the Secret Agent until almost half of its outrageous 161 minute runtime to realize it forgot to begin the story, so Wagner Moura and some other characters, introduced for that scene, sits down on a couch to explain to the audience what's going on. While editing is utilized to make this scene have a bit more life to it, that didn't prevent it from cementing the movie in my mind as one filled with almost Netflix-like exposition, which, to be entirely fair, is appropriate here since you would expect the viewer to be asleep or on their phone by that point; if you aren't however, it's simply jarring to hear, for example, someone explain their family tree to a character who knows it (on account of being part of it) in this awards darling.And speaking of awards, of the four Oscar nominations it definitely deserves to win best casting, you're immediately impressed by the charisma of all the various guys they've got in this thing, however since there's very little connection between and rarely any point to the endless rambling vignettes they populate, they don't contribute to how enjoyable the movie is. It's surprising the cinematography didn't get a nod - I'm not saying this because it was that great, but because they used an awful lot of lenses in ways that were meant to draw attention to that fact, and Academy voters are famous for voting for whatever's easy to notice. Wagner Moura being nominated for best acting is absolutely baffling to me, he doesn't have a wide variety of expressions in this, and if there are subtleties to his performance, he must've acted so hard they ended up being imperceptible, though based on how he visibly struggles to stay awake in some moments (which - same, buddy), I doubt that's the case. My best guess would be this is a proxy nod/award for Narcos, and I would not recommend watching the Secret Agent on that basis.
author: CamaradeThia

title: Engaging Theme with Uneven Performances
text: The Secret Agent is an engaging and visually appealing film that explores corruption and how powerful individuals can damage their own countries for personal gain. Its message about moral decay and abuse of power feels relevant and thought-provoking, giving the story meaningful weight. The atmosphere is enhanced by a beautiful and well-chosen musical score that complements the film's tone and emotional moments.However, while the concept and direction are strong, some performances fall short of their potential. Not all actors deliver the same level of intensity or depth, which slightly weakens the overall impact. Despite these flaws, The Secret Agent remains a solid and worthwhile watch, driven by its themes, mood, and striking presentation.

author: renatolgtr

title: Let Brazil Cook
text: Honestly, this movie was better than I expected. Last year, we had "I'm Still Here", and now, Brazil just cooked another masterpiece.The Secret Agent is a really good movie, I just expected something else from the title, but even so, I really liked how it turned out.There were some jokes here and there that made me laugh, and the story is pretty solid for the most part, and sometimes, I found myself nervous, not knowing what would happen next.At times, the movie stops to focus on the main plot and just shows the characters bonding and having fun, like the whole horror leg plot, which was unexpected, but a good bit.The ending, on the other hand, was a bit underwhelming, it does make sense and it was foreshadowing towards it, but I did expected more.But in general, it's a really good movie, and I hope it wins "Best International Film".

title: What a masterpiece!

author: juvenalmarcelosantos

title: Between Legs and Sharks.
text: Brazilian director Kleber Mendonça Filho writes and directs this thriller that reflects the dictatorship in Brazil. A period drama that evolves into a gripping historical portrait of a dark period in Brazilian society.Starring an impeccable Wagner Moura, the film is set in 1977, during Brazil's military dictatorship. Marcelo, a teacher trying to escape a turbulent past, returns to the city of Recife hoping for a fresh start and to reunite with his son. However, he soon discovers that the city is far from the refuge he imagined, as government forces are hunting him and death threats hang over him.The accolades are more than well-deserved for *O Agente Secreto*, a powerful film that, beneath the urban legend, hides a human thriller about daily life in Brazil during the dictatorship. It's a story about a community that offers a narratively complex yet highly engaging tale. The film navigates genres with ease, resulting in an explosive and delightful mix of drama, crime, thriller, and even giallo, with well-placed doses of humor.It's a thriller that needs time to reveal its true nature. Between the darkness of its characters and the folly of their surroundings, the film conceals an intense protagonist with whom you gradually become involved, feeling like you're part of this entire survival journey. It goes far beyond what its title suggests and offers different interpretations, not only of what happened at that specific moment, but also before and after.A vigorous and genuine exercise in pure genre, inspired and captivating. Few filmmakers today manage to transport us to a time, a place, and stories that ignite fascination, where each character conceals an intriguing plot waiting to be uncovered. It overflows with a contagious, anarchic energy while portraying the rot of the dictatorship and conveying a profound love for Brazil, its history, its customs, and, of course, for cinema itself. Kleber Mendonça Filho stands out for his intelligence in every sense and surrounds himself with a cast that gives their all on screen, ensuring that we never feel the film is forced, but rather that it's a story that envelops us like the most gripping intrigue before our eyes.It's a charming film that doesn't strive for perfection as its greatest achievement, but rather finds in its humanity a way to transform its flaws into great virtues. Its uniqueness accompanies a hypnotic narrative that moves between the passage of time and the present day. It's a work that doesn't hide its message and dares to offer its own version of a part of Brazilian history, a proposition that proves more than satisfactory.
author: saolivaresm

title: A Film That Doesn't Give Closure
text: Before watching this movie, I honestly think it helps to read a bit about Brazil's history. Nothing deep, just basics. That helped me a lot. Otherwise, the movie can feel confusing. It's non-linear and slightly complicated, especially if you're not familiar with Brazil's political background.The film is slow-paced, but it's full of small details. Every scene feels loaded. It talks about corruption, colonial hangover, trauma, power, and societal decay of that period, not directly, but always in the background.This is not a movie that ends when the credits roll. Some films are easy, you watch, you understand, finished. But this is the kind of film that stays in your head. What you understand in the first watch is actually very little. When you think about it again and again, more meanings start opening up. A rewatch will definitely reveal a lot of symbolism and hidden details.The ending might feel frustrating at first because there is no proper closure. You'll even question the director. But that's the point. The real incidents this film reflects never got closure, and that unresolved trauma is exactly what the film makes us feel. Once that clicks, the ending makes sense.Yes, at times it feels laggy, but the mystery keeps you going. In the third act, once violence enters, the film becomes more intense and gripping.The movie is completely about corruption, internal, external, political, societal, everywhere. Even small dialogues and background details are filled with it. That's when you realise this is not just Brazil's story. Corruption exists everywhere.It also shows a harsh truth: justice is expensive. Justice mostly belongs to those who can afford it.Because of all these layers and detailing, the Oscar nomination makes complete sense. This is not simple cinema, it's cinema that asks you to think.Overall, a good watch. Not for everyone, but if it works for you, it really stays with you.
author: Abyjith10

title: Peak actors, terrible writing
text: I've been watching Brazilian entertainment since the 90s.
