In many cases, the acting in Brazil is superior than in Hollywood, but lately the writing is getting worse and worse.Wagner Moura is a great actor, he was the best in the Tropa de Elite movies and also in the Escobar Netflix show, for example. He's also quite good in this movie The Secret Agent, but unfortunately the movie is just not that good.The Secret Agent ends up being a 4/10 movie, longer than it needed to be, very slow pace and with an enormous amount of political and social propaganda, to the point that you'll be rolling your eyes every 15 minutes.You know the writers messed up when you end up rooting for the bad guys. That's what happened with this movie. The good guys are obnoxious and full of themselves and the bad guys are actually more genuine and, ultimately, funnier.Hopefully Brazilian entertainment can go back to the good old days of Cidade de Deus.Thank you for your attention.
author: Hazu29

title: Secretly dismal.
text: The Secret Agent is premised on a professor ( Moura) in hiding due to reasons that surface later in the film. This gives the director plenty of scope to flesh out the day to day corruption, secrecy, disregard of life, and general living conditions of the 1970s military rule of Brazil. This is done with excellent cinematography and a plethora of characters and dialogue. Where the film fails is its inability to hook the audience into being invested or caring. A lot of the scenes were mundane , lacking coherency , intensity and engrossment. It masquerades as a thriller but lacks thrills- it's more a social commentary of the time. Perhaps a better understanding of Brazilian life and subculture would have helped but the director is appealing to an international audience so no excuse here.( many foreign films have an unfamiliar culture portrayed to the audience much more successfully ). The acting is good, and it feels very realistic thanks to the cinematography. A reasonable third act with a fair resolution cannot save this disappointment. Give it a miss. It will not win best film despite its surprise nomination.
author: deleyshaun

title: Everyman in a political nightmare
text:
If you're not fond of the sight of blood, you might think twice about seeing "The Secret Agent."
Blood is the glue trying together the chapters of this Brazilian political thriller just nominated for four Academy Awards after winning the best-actor Golden Globe for Wagner Moura.Blood is both symbol and metaphor ... not to mention literal, as gallons of the stuff flows through the fast-paced action. It comes from numerous donors, including one gnarly shark. Sharks, and the movie "Jaws," are another recurrent theme for writer-director Kleber Mendonca Filho.Marcelo Alves (Moura) is not a secret agent. Instead, he is a one-time university professor
forced underground by Brazil's military dictatorship of the 1970s after defying a ruthless industrialist trying to appropriate one of his patents.Now Marcelo is living in limbo in the city of Recife at carnaval time, hiding under an assumed identity in a halfway house for leftist enemies of the state, awaiting forged documents so he and his young son can flea the country.So rather than a badge of James Bond-style prowess, "Secret Agent" is, instead, a label bestowed on him by the authoritarian regime. A label, like illegal alien. Or domestic terrorist. Or a very nasty person ...
Filmmaker Filho paints the screen with washed-out primary colors and other details of '70s culture. Marcelo (that's no longer his name) drives a rickety yellow VW bug through streets teeming with revelers in front of gaudy storefronts. The line blurs between mardi gras hedonism and the frightened vigilance and other sorts of paranoia pervading life 24/7.Bullying oppression goes hand-in-hand with wall-to-wall corruption. The hired hitmen on Marcelo's trail are in cahoots with - or no different from - the father-and-sons goons running the police force. The journalists entrusted with reporting "the news" have to resort to coded urban legends like "the hairy leg," a disembodied appendage that attacks LGBT+ lovers in public parks, rather than reporting the actual police brutality that leaves its victims in hospitals and morgues.Moura's performance spans the broadest range of emotions, from loving father and husband to an Everyman caught in a Kafkaesque political nightmare. Writer-director Filho has also received his share of awards beginning at Cannes, not just for the detail of this period piece, but for viscerally sharing the pit-of-the-stomach terror of living under an authoritarian dictatorship.Audiences elsewhere in the world, like those in his native Brazil, are familiar with the feeling.In the U. S., it's still a new sensation.
author: rickchatenever

title: Very Boring Movie
text: As usual, these "weird" movies find their way to the Oscars 😂. The Night Agent has a painfully slow pace, and for long stretches you feel like you're watching clues without a map. The atmosphere and production are solid, and there are moments that look genuinely well made, but the script feels messy and underwritten. Instead of building tension, it drifts, repeats itself, and leaves key beats unclear-so the payoff doesn't hit the way it should. With a tighter edit and a clearer narrative spine, this could've been a really strong film. As it is, it's more frustrating than intriguing.
author: josetron

title: Insightful!
text: I had a great time with this movie, it gave me a great insight about Brazil during that period that I find really interesting. Somehow it's keeps you hooked to the trama. The acting was great. I understand now why is nominated, it is really good. Waner has become one of my favorite actors, he is very versatile, carries the whole movie on his shoulders, which I find outstanding.
author: CarlosMCabrero

title: Overhyped
text: Not the film I expected especially after the Oscar nominations. Went to the theatre today buzzing, what I walked into though was rough pacing, mid soundtrack and story lacking. 5 at best. I just couldn't connect to it and I really tried. There are much better films to consume even in 2025 alone. Disappointed.
author: fryzeegunner-83624

title: Masterclass
text: This film is superb. I would recommend reading about it before watching, as being able to recognize the complex political context, brilliant nods to Brazilian folklore, etc will make the viewing more enjoyable and easier to understand scenes that may come across random or unnecessary that contribute to a deep story drenched in the complicated and impactful reality of Brazil's history and present times. The cinematography, humour, soundtrack and bright colours of costumes and set bring the same level of complexity as the politics and violence to the visual front.
author: RaidonA

title: Slow, hard to follow and too long, The Secret Agent is redeemed by a solid 3rd act
text: I headed into this really looking forward to it but was quickly met by the realisation that this was not well catered to the uninitiated, given that without any knowledge of 1970s Brazilian politics, I was missing out on key plot details and character motivations. The failure to convey necessary context is a valid criticism, considering that the film is catering to an international audience. The plot moves glacially for the first two hours, and despite being stylishly shot, I did not find it engaging and was prepared to give an more negative review. However, the final act is more tense, action-packed and intriguing thematically, so it ended on a strong point.
author: Stargrazingreviews

title: Much Ado About Nothing
text: The Brazilian film industry is living through an almost unprecedented moment of recognition, both at home and abroad. For years, the idea persisted that Brazil did not know how to make cinema, as if our production were limited to empty comedies from Rede Globo. Today, we are finally beginning to look at our own cinema with more maturity. The problem starts when recognition automatically becomes a synonym for quality. That is exactly where The Secret Agent enters the discussion.I came in with high expectations. I did not watch it in a movie theater, but the weight of Cannes, the Critics Choice Awards, and the Golden Globes had already created a promise of greatness. The shock was immediate: international praise does not guarantee personal involvement. What is acclaimed does not always connect with the viewer.I love historical productions, especially when major periods are shown through the eyes of those who are not at the center of power. That was how I saw The Secret Agent: the chance to follow an ordinary civilian being crushed by the dictatorship. Something very different from I'm Still Here (2024), which follows Rubens Paiva, a politician, a man from the social and intellectual elite. Here, the promise was different. The problem is that it is never fulfilled.The main obstacle is the screenplay: bloated, exhausting, and without a clear direction. There are too many unnecessary scenes and a constant sense of dispersion. It takes two hours and forty minutes for the film to finally try, only at the end, to say clearly what it actually wants to be.The much-talked-about "hairy leg" is the symbol of this detour. The Northeastern legend appears in a scene that is completely out of place, extremely stupid, and dramatically useless. It deepens nothing, expands no theme, and contributes nothing to the narrative. It only interrupts the rhythm and provokes the inevitable question: what am I watching?If it lost an hour of runtime, went through a new edit, and focused more on Marcelo's escape and his motivations, the film would be infinitely better. It lacks synthesis and suffers from indulgence.Still, there are clear merits. Wagner Moura delivers an impeccable performance. Tânia Maria is the great revelation, with a magnetic screen presence. The recreation of the 1970s is excellent: costumes, sets, cinematography, and aesthetic references are all carefully crafted. Technically, it is a rich and meticulous work.But cinema, above all, is screenplay. And this screenplay is fragile. When it fails, everything else becomes ornament. It can be beautiful, sophisticated, and well-produced, but it is never enough to compensate for the lack of narrative strength.I watched it on the eve of the Oscar nominations and, like any good Brazilian, I still hope it gets nominated. International recognition carries symbolic weight. It is just tiring to pretend that awards are synonymous with quality. They are, first and foremost, politics.The Secret Agent rides on the impact created by Walter Salles's film. Both deal with the dictatorship, but the similarities end there. While one is striking and revolutionary, the other is diffuse and easily forgettable. One establishes itself as a reference work. The other sounds like a footnote.It hurts to write this, because I wanted to like it. But The Secret Agent does not seem strong enough to survive the test of time in the popular imagination.
author: LuanMarques1992

title: Interesting, but weak as an experience.
text: Kleber Mendonça Filho has succeeded in maturing his filmmaking, and uses a more sophisticated language in "The Secret Agent" than in his previous films; the opening scene makes it clear that it will be a film that will work with subtext at various points. Despite having a well-developed subtext, "The Secret Agent" often feels like a film with a drawn-out script; its long duration is unnecessary, and this is evident in many moments. I don't know why many critics consider "The Secret Agent" a thrilling film because, for the most part, the film is slow, and not a meditative or poetic slowness, but a monotonous, everyday slowness... This may also be connected to the editing work, which could have made the film more dynamic. Despite these problems, "The Secret Agent" is a very interesting story about forgotten people, about crimes that are never punished, and of course, about Brazil. It's important to remember that Kleber Mendonça Filho is a person who carries a very politicized vision in his films, and this makes them more superficial than profound. There is no great philosophical work, no reflection that truly transcends pragmatism; this is one of Kleber Mendonça Filho's biggest flaws-being a filmmaker who looks at many things askance and forgets that the beauty of cinema lies in its richness philosophical, on the human side, in aesthetic refinement, etc...
author: metodico

title: Excess of Metaphor, Scarcity of Meaning
text: This review is written by a Brazilian cinephile. The fact that this is a national production does not impose on me any obligation to offer automatic praise, nor does it lead me to overlook flaws in the name of cultural patriotism. For me, cinema is, above all, precision, coherence, and narrative rigor. Having watched nearly seven thousand films over several decades - not counting the many titles revisited multiple times - I feel fully justified in offering a frank and uncompromising analysis, regardless of a film's country of origin. The Secret Agent (2025) was my first encounter with the work of Kleber Mendonça Filho, and unfortunately it resulted in deep disappointment.The film presents itself as a political thriller set in 1977, during Brazil's military dictatorship, but stumbles precisely where it should be most solid: narrative clarity, internal coherence, and dramatic discipline. From the opening moments, there is a tendency toward redundant symbolism. The decision to show full-screen photographs of artists who were prominent in Brazil in 1977 clearly aims to anchor the story in its historical period. However, only moments later, a title card explicitly states that the story takes place in 1977. The effect is unnecessary and weakens the introduction, as if the film felt compelled to overemphasize something already established.The gas station sequence exemplifies another recurring issue: the sacrifice of plausibility in favor of blunt allegory. Armando arrives at the station and there is a corpse abandoned beside it, exposed in the open air. Even after two policemen arrive, the body is ignored; one of them prefers to question Armando and search his car. Even considering the authoritarian context of the era, this situation is unacceptable from a basic standpoint of realism: a decomposing body would produce a strong odor, pose sanitary risks, and require immediate removal. Everything suggests that the corpse was deliberately left there as a symbolic representation of death made banal under the dictatorship. If that was the intention, the result is misguided and excessive, as it breaks the internal logic of the scene and undermines credibility.During the opening credits, the film briefly cuts away from Wagner Moura inside the car to return to the image of the corpse lying on the dirt. This repetition adds no new meaning, deepens no metaphor, and advances nothing narratively. It is merely another visual underline that reinforces the impression of empty insistence.The screenplay's structure weakens further with the abrupt introduction, around the 43-minute mark, of two young women sitting in front of laptops, listening to cassette tapes labeled with the year 1977 and the name Marcelo. The scene interrupts the main narrative without any explanation. We do not know who these women are, what time period they belong to, or how they relate to Armando. Only at the end of the film, when one of them speaks with a doctor - Armando's son - does it become clear that they are students researching the dictatorship era and the events surrounding Armando. This information, deliberately withheld, does not generate productive mystery; it merely causes confusion. A brief contextualization at the beginning would have sufficed. As presented, the scene feels intrusive and poorly integrated.Another example of narrative excess is the scene involving the German tailor Hans, played by Udo Kier. The character adds nothing to the plot, clarifies no relationships, and advances no conflict. It is a sterile detour that merely extends the runtime. The impression is that of a personal concession by the director to the actor, who passed away in November 2025, but cinema cannot be sustained by implicit tributes. Notably, Armando himself seems to share the viewer's boredom, leaving the scene without even acknowledging the delegate. Several similar moments appear throughout the film, scenes that exist without clear dramatic function.To be fair, the period reconstruction is impeccable. In this respect, the film deserves genuine praise. Cars, costumes, hairstyles, portraits of President Geisel on the walls, and the films playing in theaters in 1977 all demonstrate meticulous research and attention to detail. Unfortunately, this visual rigor contrasts sharply with a basic problem in the dialogue: imprecise diction. At several points, lines are difficult to understand, which further compromises an already confusing narrative.Information gaps multiply. In the secret meeting between Armando and Elza and Valdemar inside a movie theater, nothing is explained. Who are these people? What is their concrete relationship to Armando? Why are they recording the conversation? The film offers no sufficient clues for the viewer to construct meaning, treating the scene as if its importance were self-evident.The most problematic - and frankly embarrassing - moment occurs around the 1h49 mark, in the infamous leg sequence. Grandiose music accompanies the image of a severed leg lying on a lawn. This is followed by nighttime scenes in a public square where several people engage in sex as if it were an improvised outdoor brothel. The same leg appears walking among them. A cut shows a woman reading a newspaper article about the incident, oddly describing it as a "shadow" rather than a leg. Returning to the square, the leg begins attacking people. What had been, however flawed, a political drama suddenly veers into grotesque horror. The sequence is absurd, tonally incoherent, and ridiculous. Later we learn it is the same leg found inside a shark and stolen from the morgue by a corrupt policeman, who throws it into a river. If the goal was to make it disappear, throwing it into a river where it could float and be found again is an elementary contradiction. The film ends without ever clarifying the leg's narrative or symbolic relevance.The musical score and incidental music further aggravate the experience. They are intrusive, excessively dramatic, and emotionally manipulative, often at odds with the images. Rather than sustaining tension, they generate fatigue and irritation.Logical inconsistencies pile up in the final act. When Armando asks the delegate for help, he manages to identify Vilmar, the hired killer. Yet Marcelo points him out to the police while the man is shown from behind, talking on the phone, even though moments earlier Marcelo had not seen his face and had only been called by name. Soon after, Vilmar phones Augusto Borba, who answers at the beach near a public phone. How did the assassin know where to find him? After killing two policemen, Vilmar escapes and passes right by the officer guarding the entrance gate, who inexplicably lets him go.In the ensuing chase, Abdias, Borba's partner, pursues Vilmar. A seated bystander, upon noticing a bloodstain on the ground, spontaneously tells Abdias which direction the killer went. How did he know Abdias was chasing the murderer? After Abdias is killed, a newspaper reports that he was shot twice in the face, while the film clearly shows a single shot from behind. The same newspaper also gives the delegate's name as Leonardo Ferreira Monteiro, even though throughout the film he is identified as Euclides Cavalcanti. These are gross errors, difficult to excuse.Even the film's title is misleading. The Secret Agent suggests that Armando is some sort of clandestine operative, when in fact he is an ordinary citizen. One cannot expect viewers to know that during the dictatorship the term was informally used to describe unidentified agents linked to the regime. That context should be made clear within the film itself.Unanswered questions abound: Who is João Pedro, who appears to be Armando's superior? Who is the businessman Henrique Ghirotti? Why does he hire Augusto Borba and Abdias to kill Armando, when it is Armando who seems to harbor deep hatred toward Ghirotti? The logic behind the central motivations remains obscure.MarceloThe Secret AgentMarcelo ends as it begins: laden with symbolic pretensions but lacking narrative cohesion, dramatic clarity, and internal rigor. What could have been a sharp portrait of a dark chapter in Brazilian history dissolves into a collection of poorly articulated scenes, exaggerated allegories, and basic screenplay errors. The final impression is not one of productive unease, but of perplexity and exhaustion. A film that demands much from its audience, yet offers very little in return.
author: Hildebrando_Martins_Almeida

title: And then all Hell breaks loose. For 5 minutes.
text: Created with painstaking detail while divulging its narrative twists sparingly, Kleber Mendonça Filho's The Secret Agent is a slow burn delight. By the two hour mark the audience is treated to multiple absorbing digressions and a number of delicious tracking shots.Yet the exact nature of the film's plot remains elusive. By the film's conclusion multiple dangling threads remain and our natural desire for closure stymied. This goes to the film's themes of history getting lost in the fragility of memory. The modern day postscript sees the younger generation reconstructing the past as an effort to avoid historical mistakes.The ominous and dreadful opening scene sets the tone for late 70s Brazil. The Policia Militar are more interested in harassing Wagner Moura's Marcelo (Armando) than they are in investigating a dead body in the road. The dead body can't pay a bribe.Moura established his reputation in America off the back of his towering depiction of Pablo Escobar in Narcos. It is a Television leading man performance which rivals the works of Gandolfini and Cranston. The actor offers another bravura performance in The Secret Agent, one that highlights his range. Where his Columbian drug lord is an angsty raging alpha, his research professor is an often taciturn open-hearted omega.The Secret Agent brings to mind more recent South American history. Perhaps the hammer isn't mightier than the pen.Sometimes only time will tell.
author: RyanM-350

title: Neither serves the thrill nor the politics!
text: Aside from the captivating opening sequence and a somewhat engaging climax, I find that the film falls short of achieving its intended objectives, whether as a political critique or a thrilling narrative.Instead, it seems to linger in an 'in-between' space that feels distant and elusive, making it hard for me to appreciate its qualities or maintain my interest throughout its duration, if truth be told, it just didn't hold my attention.Wagner Moura's portrayal of Armando is commendable; he infuses the character with warmth and authenticity.However, considering the backdrop of the story, i.e. Brazil under a military dictatorship in '77, I anticipated a far more impactful and profound exploration of the subject matter in hand.I can still vividly remember Walter Salles' 'I'm Still Here,' which had garnered accolades at the Academy Awards previously, which In my humble opinion, was a much superior film that not only made the core characters feel close to me but also intricately wove comparable themes about dictatorship, murders, societal anarchy etc.
author: SoumikBanerjee1996

title: the jaws of the past
text:
There is no espionage story in 'O agente secreto' ('The Secret Agent') by Brazilian director
Kleber Mendonça Filho, nor is there any connection to the novel written by James Conrad more than a century ago, which inspired many other films and television series. It is one of those films that is difficult to fit into a single cinematic style, which can belong to several genres or for which a special category should be invented. The film borrows its title from a scene in which the main character passes through a cinema hall in which the credits of a film (imaginary, with Belmondo!) of that name are being projected. 'O agente secreto' is also full of references to cinema around 1977 and in particular to Steven Spielberg's 1975 blockbuster 'Jaws' - and a screening room is the setting for several key scenes. This complex and interesting movie does not belong to the genre of films about films either. It combines political realism with Latin American fantastic to depict the struggle for survival of people living in a corrupt and violent society, in the midst of a period of dictatorship that lasted many decades and dominated the history of Brazil in the second half of the 20th century.The film starts as a road movie in the semi-desert landscape of northern Brazil, an arid country strewn with corpses and dominated by fear, to evolve into an atypical political thriller with a slow movie pace. The main hero is called Marcello or maybe Armando, as he sometimes hides his real identity. He flees from the authorities and the hired killers sent on his trail, waiting for the false passports that will allow him and his little boy to leave the country. The city of Recife where he arrives is a place of refuge and perhaps of transit to freedom or, in any case, to a world where his life is not in danger. A corner of a country dominated by political terror and endemic corruption. The reasons for his behavior will be gradually discovered by the viewers who will also get to know those around him: his deceased wife's parents, his neighbors - most of whom are fugitives like him -, the gullible politicians, the corrupt policemen and the hired killers, a kind of Tarantino-style heroes transported to Brazilian soil. Flashbacks will gradually complete the story of the film's hero's life, and the events that rush towards the end will only be clarified and put into context by an epilogue that takes place in our days.I also really liked the way the screenwriter and director Kleber Mendonça Filho built the narrative and how he brought it to the screen, transforming the cinematic spectacle into a violent realistic image of Brazil in the eighth decade of the last century. Mixed in are also elements of fantasy, as present in many novels and films of Latin America, which are packaged in this case with elements of Hollywood cinematic mythology. Spielberg and (again) Tarantino seem to have met in an episode of men under a history of dictatorship, fear and repression. I appreciated the remarkable gallery of portraits created by the filmmaker and exceptionally interpreted by a collection of formidable selected and guided actors. I will mention only two names: that of Wagner Moura who has already collected well-deserved awards for the lead role in the film and the German actor Udo Kier, in his last appearance on the screens in a heartbreaking supporting role. The full list should be very long. In my opinion, in this year with serious competition, 'O agente secreto' should collect several Academy Awards, and not just the one for Best Film in a Foreign Language.
author: dromasca

title: A well-recreated period
text: A perfect 70s aesthetic, evident in the costumes, hairstyles, and sets, as well as the film grain and camera angles. The lengthy setup of the plot requires some patience, but the script's reward is well worth the effort. Wagner Moura is indeed excellent. A beautiful way to conclude the film. To be savored with patience.
author: alain_pinel

title: Boring
text: While the espionage genre usually promises high-stakes tension and pulse-pounding intrigue, The Secret Agent delivers all the excitement of watching a committee file paperwork. Instead of a sharp, cinematic thriller, audiences are served a sluggish, heavy-handed narrative that feels less like art and more like a taxpayer-funded lecture.
author: vhpcampos

title: A terrific Wagner Moura
text: If you liked him as Pablo Escobar in Narcos and in Civil War, you will love Wagner Moura in this film. Although the movie runs long (2:41) and the story can get a little confusing, the end result is more than satisfying. Directed and written by Brazilian Kleber Mendonça Filho, the film takes the craziness of 1977 Brazil under a dictatorship, a story around a man trying to change his identity, and a large and talented cast (see in particular Tânia Maria as Dona Sebastiana) to make a film that I enjoyed from beginning to end.Moura plays a high-tech expert who is trying to evade his past by changing his name from Marcelo to Armando and moving to Recife during Carnival. His wife has died and his son Fernando lives with the maternal grandparents. Armando lives in a house run by Dona Sebastiana with other political refugees. He is trying to escape his past by taking a position in a local government records office. In a subplot that is not fully explained, he spends some of his time in the records office searching for lost identity records of his mother. Unfortunately, he cannot escape his past and he is targeted for assassination by a corrupt business executive who has a grudge against Marcelo/Armando. He is urged to flee the country while violence erupts among the local police and the hit men. The film ends with an update: Fernando is now a doctor in Recife but does not remember his father despite the availability of audio recordings and newspaper accounts.The above plot does not adequately describe Filho's wonderful storytelling that kept me spellbound for the entire movie. There is Moura's acting, the great cast full of colorful people, the setting of 1970s Brazil, and humor largely based around a shark (I won't give it away). This is definitely worth seeing.
author: ProfWinerReviews

title: A World Built on Suspicion
text: This one takes its time and doesn't rush anything. It's very talky, very slow, but in a way that feels intentional. The tension isn't about big moments, it's more about the constant unease, like something bad is always just around the corner.The mood does a lot of the work here. Everything feels heavy, suspicious, and morally messy, and that's what makes it interesting. On top of that, the art direction is excellent, with a strong visual identity and carefully composed frames that give the film a distinct look and atmosphere.Quiet, smart, and surprisingly gripping once it clicks.
author: augustovlucca

title: Pamphlet
text: Pamphlet from the Brazilian left that dominates state-funded cinema. The film is sold as a neo-noir thriller with Wagner Moura on the run from a mysterious past in Recife, but in reality it's a selective ode to the "victim" of the dictatorship: the pure, always-right, heroic leftist intellectual.Wagner Moura acts well (he always does), the cinematography lovingly captures 1970s Recife, there's decent pacing in parts and cool references (like Tubarão, the hairy leg). But the script is so manichean and one-sided that it becomes cheap propaganda. It only shows the side that suits today's PT narrative: "we were the pure oppressed good guys, the coup came from eternal evil." It deliberately ignores that corruption and authoritarianism don't have just one side - and that plenty of people on the left back then had their own skeletons.
author: joaopauloxrocha

title: Expected more, but good.
text: I had not even heard of The Secret Agent. I hadn't even seen a trailer and had to go to the ONE theater that was playing this film 50 miles away! But I was intrigued by the buzz around this film. Being a Brazilian film that was directed by Kleber Mendonça Filho I thought it was going to be great. Last year's great Brazilian film I'm Still Here which was one of the best films I saw made me go into this film with very high expectations.I have to say that even though I liked the film I was disappointed because I thought I would see a much better film. It is set in Brazil in 1977 during the Brazilian military dictatorship. Armando, played by Wagner Moura is a former professor who is fleeing persecution from the regime. He arrives in Recife to gets an apartment from a woman who gives shelter to various political refugees. Armando tries to reconnect with his son which had been raised by his in laws since his wife had died. Armando is in hiding because he's in danger and has to leave Brazil. All of this would be a lot more intriguing if the film was more focused. It is technically a thriller but director Kleber Mendonça Filho makes this film more about a collection of weird and quirky characters and a more satirical tone at times that reduce the tension. But the film feels more like a love letter to films the fire tor loves. There is a focus on Jaws, for instance that in many ways is a metaphor for what Armando is going through. Also, Sr. Alexandre, Armando's father in law is a projectionust at a Recife movie theater. There are so e great scenes there. Including huge line outside a showing of Jaws, and the audience reaction to The Omen. There's a plot point about a severed leg found in a shark that gets a lot of attention. Even a very funny dream sequence with that leg. Very funny scene but it breaks what should be a more compelling and tense story. When the 3rd act comes, there is some tension but not nearly as much as if it could have been there if the film has been more focused.Also, at almost 160 minutes the film is very slow.Good film, not as good as it could have beenGrade: B-
author: flicklover

title: Corruption, fear & quiet resistance-powerful masterpiece
text: Incredible movie set in 1977 Brazil dictatorship-corruption, police brutality, moral collapse everywhere (that shark leg scene is perfect satire). Armando on the run, mystery kept me hooked until reveals. Slow first half builds tension perfectly. Themes of resistance, fear, lost humanity hit hard. Wagner Moura amazing. Tiny issue: early slow burn, but worth it. Deserves all awards-perfect 10/10.
author: DrGadimov

title: Time doesn't care. *Spoilers*
text: The Secret Agent finds its greatest brilliance in the way it closes its own narrative. By revealing Armando/Marcelo's death through a newspaper, the film does something rare and profoundly human: it shows that life simply goes on. The son, who loved his father deeply, grows up - and because he lost him so early, barely remembers him as an adult. As happens with so many real lives.There is something harsh and deeply true in this. Our lives matter immensely while we are living them, yet many of them are lost in the vast sea of time and space. And when they do survive, they are not always remembered as they truly were. We do not control our own narrative - and the film seems painfully aware of this silent tragedy.That said, the film's pacing is debatable. The plot unfolds with great artistry and restraint, supported by exceptional production design. The faded tones of the 1970s are not merely aesthetic; they feel like a state of mind. The production is impeccable in every detail.Wagner Moura's performance is outstanding. At many moments, I completely forgot the actor and truly believed I was watching Marcelo/Armando himself. That kind of disappearance of the actor into the character is rare and powerful.By the end, I was deeply impressed - but I would not watch it again. This is not a film made for entertainment. It is intense, dense, almost emotionally exhausting. There is something cathartic about it, and perhaps even nihilistic, in the sense that it confronts the loss of meaning, the erosion of memory, and the indifference of time toward individual stories.The Secret Agent does not comfort. It confronts. And perhaps that is precisely why it leaves such a lasting impact.
author: lusobrosa

title: Ordinary corruption in beautiful colours
text: Brazil in the 1970s, military regime is brutal, and ultra-liberal economy feeds, as in other dictatorships, a rampant omnipresent corruption amongst the powerful, the uniformed and, basically, anyone who has information or a neighbour to sell.Kleber Mendonça Filho, the director and writer, has a taste for the resistance and resilience of ordinary people against institutionalised violence. His central characters, each of them brilliantly brought to the screen by an inspired cast, is un-ambiguous morally but ethically confused. From corrupt policemen and their lot to fleeing idealist and his network of helpers, they belong to a manichaeans world split, but their actions tend to get away from them and the side they incarnate. That prevents the 160 minutes length of the film to become boring.That, plus the beautiful cinematography. Colour becomes a language of itself, which often emphasises by opposition the darkness of the actions. For instance the drab repulsive corruption of day to day policemen is even more blatant when shot in a dried out extra sunny landscape of fields, by the whitewashed walls and roadsigns of a gas station, where a Yellow Volkswagen driver is their target feet away from a days old decaying body, which everybody ignores but a hoard of famished dogs.Beyond being a tense thriller, the movie is also a political work. Two movies are quoted, The Omen, and Jaws, two perfect caricatures of evil lurking below the surface of the United States colonial and eco-liberal politics of the sixties and seventies, often relying on authoritarian conservative regimes installation. Mendonça Filho may well be delivering us a warning as to the return of these times. Similarly, the suggestion that carnivals and/or sensationalist tabloid like medias participate in hiding from us deeper violence and oppression is probably not a gratuitous twist of the scenario. But this is done discreetly and does not distract from the core thriller genre of the movie: Individual running away from a corrupt network of powerful enemies and trying to save himself and his family.
author: jjr4

title: Intense and unsettling
text: The film has a very interesting and beautiful representation of Brazil in the 70s, full of cool references to the culture of the time, including films from the 70s that are now classic cults. The pacing builds up to a strong ending, that i have to admit i felt it was interrupted. The final persecution scene left everyone in the theater on the edge of their sits, but for some reason the director cuts for another scene and ends the film... but overall quite good film, it impresses me how brazilian cinema is evolving fast, one film better than the other, lately. The Secret Agent is not the action thriller in the "Hollywood" sense, because it has a lot of political undertones and works with cultural peculiarities from southern american countries that western cinema has dificulty to understand and value, but it's quite a unique film from a national industry that is learning how to make films using the aesthetics and comercial language from Hollywood, and it's working quite well.
author: felipearielm

title: Political Cinema Rooted in Myth, Streets, and Silence
text: What fascinated me most about The Secret Agent is how the film operates through silence, implication, and tension rather than exposition. Its political dimension is always present, but never didactic. Power, surveillance, fear, and masculinity are explored through gestures, absences, and atmosphere instead of speeches. The film trusts the viewer to read between the lines - and that trust is rare and deeply satisfying.There is also a strong sense of Recife as both myth and territory. The city is not just a backdrop, but a living presence shaped by memory, folklore, and urban tension. The film's connection to street-level cinema - grounded, physical, and attentive to everyday spaces - reinforces its political and symbolic weight. Restrained, unsettling, and deeply intelligent, this is an 10/10.
author: rhuamaaz

title: Too much is too much
text: I really wanted to enjoy this film more than I did. The acting is good, the story is interesting enough to watch, the grindhouse/vintage style of filming was intriguing. So, what went wrong?Well for starters, the film is about 45 minutes longer than it should be. There was multiple occasions I kept asking myself "why was this scene included in the film?" and most of the time, the scene never connects to rest of the film. It's screams extra just to be extra. Somewhere in the middle the film loses it's grip and doesn't regain it until the last 20 minutes or so. With some tighter editing, this could have been a great film!Other than that, my only complaint is how hyped up the violence was and people calling it disturbing. We've seen worse done to better people on screen. It was nothing to write home about. In fact, I found the scene to be hilarious considering what was being done and to whom. An absolute riot of an ending, it's too bad what follows in the small epilogue is kind of depressing. Had the chance to end it on a higher note but, like most movies these days, it ends on a sour note.Overall, not a bad film. It's actually very good if you ignore that the film meanders quite a bit and that causes the enormous runtime to feel bloated and unnecessary in parts. I would recommend it, but it's a tough recommendation due to it's length. If you do see it, just be advised, it's a long film.3 dirty cops out of 5.
author: nicolasroop

title: Slow Burn Brazilian Thriller
text: I saw the movie "Missing" for the first time not that long ago. It was nominated for a Best Picture Oscar back in 1982 and is about a military coup in Chile and the search for a missing American that gets tangled up in the corruption of the Chilean and American governments and authorities."The Secret Agent" tells a very different story from "Missing," but because I had seen that movie recently I was thinking of it while watching this one. "The Secret Agent" is also set in a South American country, Brazil in this story, and it's also about how an innocent person gets trapped in the country because of the rampant corruption and the lack of reliable governance. Wagner Moura plays a man in witness protection trying to flee with his son, but in 1977 Brazil, witness protection was a grass roots operation not given any federal support, so it's basically a cat and mouse game to try to get out of the country before hired goons find him and kill him. All sorts of people are willing to be paid off, cops included.I'm not really sure who the secret agent of the title is supposed to be, unless it's an all-encompassing term meant to apply to any number of characters who are involved in their own private missions, most of them nefarious. The movie is being categorized as a thriller, and there are very suspenseful moments, but I'm not sure that's what it is first and foremost. It's more a slow-burn drama about people adrift in a system that seems intent on destroying them.Moura is quite good in the lead role, playing both the main character and then the grown up version of the main character's son in a final segment.Grade: A.
author: evanston_dad

title: The best Mendonça-Filho
text: The film is an authentic portrayal of the violent and corrupt Brazilian dictatorship, and sensitively shows the contrast between the barbarity and uncivilized behavior of the police and the economic elite with the friendly and kind nature of the people from the Brazilian Northeast, in this case represented by Pernambuco, Mendonça-Filho's home state. It's worth highlighting the perfect recreation of an era and the impeccable work of the main actors, especially Wagner Moura, an actor as talented as he is charismatic. Perhaps the ending is a little frustrating for a film with such a breakneck pace, but nothing is perfect.
author: otelorigato

title: AMAZING: A Lighter Lens on a Brutal Reality
text: I had read some mixed critical comments about the film, but it is a masterpiece.While some characters are slightly exaggerated for effect, the performances are striking. Certain scenes are somewhat absurd, yet the film never feels completely disconnected from reality. It portrays the harsh and violent period Brazil was going through in a somewhat "lighter" way, using subtle motifs to remind us that these were years of dictatorship.Pure Oscar material!
author: rodrigopirmez

title: A great plot gives the film a leg up
text: Catching my breath first - an incredible piece of art. Notes of Pulp Fiction, Z, Kiss Of The Spider-woman here. With the US President a big wonderful fan of imprisoned Bolsonaro, it is a gut-punch as friends and lovers are tested on commitment to freedom and the rule of law. Succeed. Fail. Try to forget.This is a beautiful gripping case study on the runaway corruption that has seeded the morally flexible authoritarian mileu we're up to today. Definitely an award worthy and morally worthy piece.
author: teharatats

title: A mess of a story...
text: ... which spoiled the wonderfully colorful characters, beautiful cinematography and expert directing. I was never bored, even though nothing much happened during the majority of the movie. When something did happen, I was never sure why.This felt like it was trying to be No Country For Old Men, but feel far short.
author: lrobiner-801-44468

title: Disparate Elements Meld Together in an Impressive Political Thriller
text: There's a lot going on in Brazilian director Kleber Mendonça Filho's atmospheric 2025 political thriller set in 1977 when Brazil was under full military dictatorship and its citizens were struggling to survive through political tyranny. While there's a natural connection to be made with last year's arthouse favorite, Walter Salles' "I'm Still Here", Filho's film is much more complex and at select moments, chaotic and shockingly violent in a vivid Tarantino-like style. The focus is on Marcelo, who despite the film title, is not a secret agent but rather a former academic researcher who desperately needs to leave the country. His department has been shut down by a corrupt minister who sees a lucrative opportunity in transferring the research department to a private company. Marcelo's absolute resistance forces him to escape to a safe house in Recife, while the minister also sets him up for a hit. The film propels into completely unpredictable directions, which includes among several disparate elements - two separate teams of hit men, a traumatized German tailor (Udo Kier effective in his last role), and a human leg found in the jaws of a killer shark (which takes a life of its own). Playing both Marcelo and later his grown son Fernando, Wagner Moura anchors the marathon film with an impressively complex performance that earns empathy as the plot progresses. A huge cast supports deftly, in particular, Tânia Maria as Dona Sebastiana, the eccentric safe house owner, and Maria Fernanda Candida as Elza, the leader of the local resistance movement. The production values are excellent, capturing the period in great depth and with visual flair.
author: EUyeshima

title: Great movie for Brazil
text: The Secret Agent delivers a solid narrative supported by strong performances and well-chosen cast. The film builds multiple layers of dramatic depth, with Wagner Moura standing out in a consistently compelling lead role. However, its excessive runtime at times slows the pace, and the final sequence feels less impactful.
author: matheusdavi-01446

title: The Secret Agent is the film of the year
text: Forget what you think a "political thriller" should be. The Secret Agent is not what you think-it's something better. Visually and dramatically, it is breathtaking.I understand some audiences may find it too long or frustrating due to its measured pacing, extended silences, unresolved ending, and complex non-linear narrative. Yet as a slow-burn thriller, it is exceptionally well-directed, edited, and acted.Director Kleber Mendonça Filho has not made a simple political thriller; he has created something completely new. He masterfully mixes suspense, dark comedy, hints of fantasy, and western, all infused with the unique spirit and specific cultural elements of Brazil's Northeast. This makes for a work that will be discussed by film lovers and historians for years to come.The result is a work of staggering originality and depth. The cast, with Wagner Moura at the forefront, delivers performances tuned perfectly to the film's shifting tones-grounded and gritty one moment, theatrically expressive the next.
author: AlissonYamakawa

title: Totally boring
text: Too long, simplistic script. Extremely boring and long. Confusing and simplistic script, 60 minutes would have been enough for a slightly better rating. Unfortunately, not even Wagner Moura's performance saves it. The film's slow pace ultimately results in a disconnect between the various stages of the film. A waste of time.
author: hugomisurelli

title: What a story ?!...
text: When i saw this movie, the first thing which came in my mind was : "the World is so tiny" !... Every people you meet each day, can be your Best Friend or your Worst Enemy. Ours lives depend on the WHEEL of FORTUNE, it was written, this is the Fate !... This picture was awarded "Best Director" & "Best Actor" to the last Cannes Film Festival... in my opinion, the best actor prize was accurate, but i question the best director award ?!... I would give it, the best screenplay instead. Wagner Moura's portrayal was fine, i really hope his name will be called on January 22, 2026 in the Best Actor Category, for the next Academy Award Season. When you watched the whole movie, you understand, this is a Family movie on several generations. I really love the film, in a certain way all movies which talk about Brazil, care for me, (Central Station, I'm Still Here, etc...) This was a very good experience watching it, i hope people will love it too.
author: cdriclria-33737

title: Incredible movie, but does all these aesthetics do the job?
text: There are many political comment in this movie like the communist granny, the couple from Angola, etc? It is so rich in political and social commentary. But i would say that it is an esay political commentary in relation to other films like "O som ao redor'', or Aquarius'' or even Bacurau that plays with stereotypes.Nowadays the popular and historical center of the city of Recife is under renovation and gentrification processes that are changing the mix of the population. How does this film inscribes in the actual political processes in Recife? It is clear that there a critical stance on the dictatorship but in relation to what is happening in Recife it does not take poisition like ''O som ao redor" and ''Aquarius". It is more of a middle class nostalgia of an epoch that ended. This is exactly the sentiment of many people that is being instrumentalized by the current city administration to gentrify the historical areas of Recife, the areas that are seen in the movie. I think that this time Kleber choose to not make an actual critic into what is happening in the city. That's why his film is easily promoted everywhere . It is easier to use it for leftist memorabilia touristification projects, than to actual make a serious political argument. There is an contradiction between ''O som ao redor'' and ''Acquarius"' during more difficult political moments, and now.
author: FOIVOSN

title: The Secret Agent (2025)
text: The Secret Agent delivers a visually rich and ambitious political thriller set in Brazil during the late 1970s under a repressive military dictatorship. The film immerses you in its atmospheric period world, with a compelling central performance and a story that blends political tension, personal stakes, and moments of surreal intensity.That said, the first and especially the second parts of the movie feel a bit loose and slow, taking their time to establish characters and context in a way that may test some viewers' patience. The pacing in these sections drifts and can feel unfocused.However, the third part is intense and gripping, with dramatic escalation and emotional weight that make the earlier buildup feel worthwhile. This structural imbalance is the main reason I rate it 7/10 - a solid and memorable film with a powerful climax, but one that could have benefited from tighter momentum in its early stretches.
author: TheBigSick

title: Everything and Nothing - total dissapointment
text: A movie lacking coherence, full of unnecessary scenes not contributing to the main story, loose ends, and flaws. A movie that is not even entertaining since nothing really happens during the first 90 minutes. It felt like a school child put the script together to attract superficial international audiences with the additional explicit violence and sex, an unnecessary "eternal victim" scene, and poorly linked "sensitive" topics (dictatorship, refugees, slavery, corruption, neoliberal foreign extraction, reconciliation). This movie feels artificial and forced, and fails to convey a heartfelt story like "I'm still here" did.
author: chanzel

title: Quirky, great performances, but disjointed
text: It's a political drama set in 1977, with flashbacks to 1974, in Racife, Brazil, during the Brazilian military dictatorship. The film's conclusion takes place 25 years later. Armando (Wagner Moura), a former university researcher, arrives back in Racife in 1977, a semi-fugitive hoping to emigrate to a university position outside the country. He was cheated by a powerful businessman, Salvatore Ghirotti (Gregorio Graziosi), with links to the military leadership. Armando goes to a "refugee" center hosted by 77-year-old Dona Sebastiana (Tânia Maria). Others, including Elza (Maria Fernanda Cândido). Meanwhile, Ghirotti arranges with two hitmen (Roney Villela and Gabriel Leone) to find and eliminate Armando. They, in turn, hire a local, Vilmar (Kaiony Venâncio), to carry out the task for a pittance. Many complications arise."O Agente Secreto" is a truly fascinating film. The storyline is creative, and the characters are all off-center just enough to remain intriguing throughout the story. In addition to those mentioned above, they include the corrupt police chief, Euclides (Robério Diógenes), the upstairs neighbor, Claudia (Hermila Guedes), and Armando's father-in-law, Alexandre (Carlos Francisco). However, "O Agente Secreto" is disjointed enough to keep it from being a great film. A quirky opening sequence and a missing leg help set the tone for this creative effort. The ending returns us to reality. Wagner Moura turns in a top-flight performance.I've since watched it a second time and changed my rating from 8 to 9. I understood many more of the plot hints distributed throughout, impressing me even more.
author: steiner-sam

title: One of the worst of 2025
text: This movie was a menagerie of nonsense and uninteresting storylines about an era of politics that was made to feel uninteresting and disconnected. It fails to feel engaging. It fails to deliver what felt like a cohesive story. And it was way too long with what felt like a constant slog of just living in this world that offers no payoff. The acting was fine, but I don't understand the hype. I really disliked this film.
author: lukemindlin

title: Well done, but mildly disappointing
text: I was very excited to see this movie a few days ago because it had just won the New York Film Critics' Circle Award for both Best Picture and Best Actor. I wondered if this would have the same impact as last year's Brazilian hit, I'M STILL HERE, did. I thought it was worth seeing, but I didn't feel very passionate about it. It was set at roughly the same time as I'M STILL HERE and touched upon the same subjects, but it took its time to build any momentum. It probably would have helped if I'd had a better background in the details of the corrupt Brazilian regime of that time, but I don't. For reasons I don't fully understand, the protagonist of the story finds out that there are two hit men who have been contracted to kill him. They've already killed his wife. He has a young son who lives with his wife's parents. He worries about their safety, too. He works in technology, which moves so fast that those scenes are primitive by today's standards. There's no Internet! (Or if there is, it's so primitive that we would deem it useless today. There appears to be no search engine!) He goes undercover and uses an alias, but they continue to close in on him. There's a lot of cold-blooded violence in this movie. Every time he thinks he has eluded them, they show up again.I basically enjoyed this movie, but ultimately, I found it rather routine, despite many fine elements. It didn't really live up to the hype for me. It pales beside the great American crime thrillers of the 1970s and '80s. As for the lead performance, Wagner Moura certainly has a strong screen presence, with Latino good-looks and a natural charm. If his English is passable, he could have a big career here. The performance in this film is strongly aided by excellent makeup and hairstyling. (I have always maintained that most people confuse acting with makeup and I think this is a pretty good example.) I probably would have liked it more if I'd been expecting less.
author: Sees All

title: Mercilessly boring
text:
Yes I am pampered by all the abundance of various movies, yes my span of attention is low and getting lower. But, man, was this a boring, boring movie.I went to see it in cinema and was not able to leave the cinema. Every minute seemed like a century...
Yes, I do not speak Portugese and I do not know the Brasilian culture and history, maybe I missed something, but the story is boring, difficult to understand, without logic... If they had cut it to 90 minutes it would have been equally shallow and uninteresting, but at least the terror would have lasted some 70 minutes less, and every minute counts with this masterpiece of movie boredom.
author: morningbugle-60910

title: Nightmarish, animistic, realistic, ultimately technological vision of dictatorial Brazil
text: I like Director Kleber Mendonça Filho, who doubles up as script writer in AGENTE SECRETO. He pulls no punches when it comes to depicting reality in his beloved Brazil.Over the course of its 160 minutes, AGENTE SECRETO portrays three men in the Solimões family of Brazil's Pernambuco State who have experienced in different ways the evils of dictatorship, with Wagner Moura delivering yet another magnificent performance as each of those men in their generation.As other reviewers point out, this is an account of events in Brazil at a difficult time when dictatorship reigned over that marvellous and multicultured land, and Mendonça Filho hides no details hard to admit about one's country.The dialogue floats from apparently normal conversation among common people to a growing realization that everyone is under threat. Photographs of then Brazilian dictatorial President Ernesto Geisel and other senior government officials hang in just about every office, corrupt policemen give cover to hitmen, welcome bribes and keep breaking the law.In a film where you also get the amiable soul of Brazilians, the contrast comes from the main villain, the wealthy company owner Ghirotti well played by Luciano Chirolli, who wants university professor/researcher Solimões I's patent, and centralization of the university funding and operation.That contrast also hits home with raw, at times exceedingly violent action, where JAWS (yes, the Spielberg-directed 1975 film) has a double with a human leg in it, and the supernatural intervenes as that leg suddenly comes alive, becomes hairy - kids laugh about comic pictures of the "hairy leg" in newspapers - and starts kicking people engaging in sex, and other compromising positions. A black Angolan woman displaying an excellent, just perfect diction of Portuguese Portuguese, keeps the residents at Solimões II's place informed about that leg. Why she and her husband appear in the film only baffled me, as they do not add to the narrative or action, other than they fled Angola and are on their way to Sweden.The animistic, supernatural element also comes in with a scarecrow-like masked man on the side of a Pernambuco country road, which revisits Solimões II in his nightmares.Add to that a cat with two faces and four eyes, at least two blind. One can interpret that in various ways. I see it as symbolizing the many angles of history and human memory.Constantly in the background of dictatorial Brazil of the 1970s, the government poses an elusive but real and constant threat to all citizens.Ultimately, nothing is sacred: two young girls are listening to tape recordings of Solimões II interviewed by a woman called Elza. How and why they have that supposedly personal and secret material, who they are - neither is a relative of the Solimões - and work for, is not disclosed but they have very modern high quality computers, so they are current generation.A positive note emerges toward the end, with Solimões III at the helm of a blood donor unit, symbolizing the heart of Brazil pumping in spite of all menaces and democratic hiccups.Superb period reconstruction - plenty of lovely VW Beetles, and other 1960s/70s vehicles on show - only heightens the enormous quality of Evgenia Alexandrova's cinematography, backed by highly effective editing by Matheus Farias and Eduardo Serrano.I sincerely believe AGENTE SECRETO and actor Wagner Moura deserve Academy Award nominations as Best Foreign Film and Best Actor, respectively. 8/10.
author: adrianovasconcelos

title: Effective Narrative beyond Storytelling
text: The charm of this nearly three-hour, sprawling film is that, despite my complete lack of understanding of the causes and consequences -- due to my unfamiliarity with both the director and Brazilian history -- I found myself watching it with great interest. The inherent fascination of the human interactions is embedded within the narrative yet beyond simply telling the story. This might be the best illustration of the difference between "narrative" and "storytelling". The dialogue is extensive but never tiresome or wordy, which is a rare treat among the selection of films at this year's festival.
author: sleeplest

title: Do not lose your time
text: I don'tunderstand the hype with this movie, it must be just a nationalistic one, or because Moura is the main character,but this is one of the worst movies I've seen in a long time. Great production and great actors, but what a terrible screenplay. After one hour (!!!) you still don't know what the film is about, what the conflict is. More than half of the it does not bring anything to the story and are just random scenes, with interesting characters and great cinematography, yes, but leading nowhere. I saw it in the cinema and it really felt like a lost of time and money.
author: prietoanamaria

title: Superb movie about memories.
text: Kleber Mendonça Filho builds The Secret Agent as a film that initially resembles a political thriller about Brazil's military dictatorship, but whose emotional and formal core lies in the realm of memory. Not just historical memory, but personal, family and collective memories that the country repeatedly allows to vanish. Armando/Marcelo is not simply a victim of the regime; he is a man trying to rescue the image of his mother from oblivion while the nation systematically erases whatever does not suit its official narrative. This intimate, almost archaeological search places the protagonist in a space where memory and identity merge. Mendonça reinforces this dimension by structuring the film as a mosaic of small recollections, fragments of popular culture, everyday gestures, urban legends and social scars that turn Recife into a living repository of memory. Every detail works as a record of something that endures despite Brazil's historical negligence. Visually striking and supported by strong performances, especially from Wagner Moura, the film understands that remembering is not a passive act. It is confrontation. It is accepting that not everything can be recovered, and that Brazilian memory is shaped as much by what survives as by what slips away. By avoiding easy resolutions and refusing the conventional closure expected from genre cinema, The Secret Agent makes clear that its goal is not to solve a mystery but to expose a country deeply uncomfortable with its own past. In the end, the film reveals itself to be far less about espionage and far more about the imperfect effort to reconstruct stories that were almost lost.
author: bruno_cpc85

title: The best of Kleber Mendonça Filho!
text: In my opinion, it is the best movie by Kleber Mendonça Filho so far (even better than Bacurau and short film Recife Frio). It has an outstanding casting, and the script, instead of an economic approach of including only scenes which are directly connected to the ending, has as a major concern showing the environment. The movie is colourful while addresses very sinister violent events, is merges absurd fantasy with political thriller, it contrasts carnival with the dark tentacles of the military authoritarian regime. Interestingly, the focus is not in institutionalized coertion. The despicable policemen, who hate communists and murder and hide the bodies without any concern, show themselves as incoherent multilayered people, able to do horrible things, for sure, but far from being pure evil. Indeed, the true villains in the film are certainly related to the dictatorship and to those cops, but they move in the shadows (although not always worried about veing fragrant), on the fringes of the slaughter-friendly regime. The university and the public interest are the ones who the greedy opportunistic villains attack in order to profit at the cost of the very country. The highest villains are spread along the different classes, and the most rich they are, the most coward too, increasing profit and reducing risks through outsourced dirty job. The good people may not succeed in a harsh realistic tale, many stories may be forgotten and erased, but the heroes may be anyone, as Sebastiana makes it clear.
author: guisreis

title: Technically Perfect But...not a 10!!!
text: The movie The Secret Agent is set during the 1977 Brazilian military dictatorship. It follows Marcelo, who returns home hoping to reunite with his son, only to find himself entangled in a web of political danger, corruption, and the oppressive atmosphere of the regime.The writer and director Kleber Mendonça Filho delivers a visually stunning film that, thanks to great production and costume design, transports us straight into the 1970s. The cinematography is another major highlight, especially with its carefully chosen color palette.One of the movie's major problems is that Mendonça focuses on presenting various characters and their stories that revolve around the central character, Marcelo, but neglects to explore Marcelo's own story. We learn very little about Marcelo's life, which is shown only briefly through a few flashbacks. Also, the ending of the movie doesn't have the same pace as the rest of the movie; it seems forced, vague, and uninspired.A former president of the Cannes Film Festival once said that Brazilian directors make movies mainly for Brazilians, not for the world, and Mendonça kind of proves that. For example, in the scene where a rich woman accidentally runs over her maid's daughter, only Brazilian viewers really understand the deeper meaning behind it, while most international audiences miss it completely.Still, the movie has some great strengths: an excellent original story, a cast made mostly of non-actors, and solid, clean direction.It's definitely worth watching!
author: li0904426

title: A deceptive title and some truly disturbing violence... yet still a film worth experiencing
text: "The Secret Agent" is a movie I recently watched at the Brazilian Film Festival. Before diving into the storyline, I must caution you-this is absolutely not suitable for children or for anyone sensitive to graphic content. The film contains harrowing murders toward the conclusion, and its tone is undeniably bleak... as intended.The narrative unfolds in Brazil during the 1970s, a time when the country was under military rule since 1964. The police are portrayed uniformly as corrupt and malicious. One of their targets is a man who, as you discover, has committed no crime whatsoever-he simply crossed paths with a powerful and ruthless figure. Much of the film follows his attempts to remain hidden while vicious pursuers hunt him down.Is this the formula for a lighthearted movie? Certainly not. But it is skillfully crafted and sheds light on a historical era many may not be familiar with-but should be. It's a well-executed, somber film that may leave you emotionally exhausted once the credits roll.
author: calcrj

title: A pretentious plot that goes nowhere
text: O Agente Secreto contains many of the ingredients of a great film - most notably its striking cinematography - but ultimately collapses into a frustrating, self-sabotaging mess.Wagner Moura delivers his customary strong performance, yet his character is a vacuum: dull, passive, and inexplicably foolish. He is being hunted, knows he needs only to remain hidden for four days before leaving the country with his son, and nonetheless repeatedly endangers himself - and others - because of... an obsessive quest to recover his dead mother's ID card?The film offers no rationale for this fixation, no psychological grounding, only a series of plot-driven acts of idiocy. There is no real urgency, only plot convenience.No other character fares better. Figures drift in, perform whatever function the plot requires, and vanish without development or consequence. The film leans heavily on familiar Latin American political clichés - the unrecognized intellectual, folkloric mysticism as metaphor, sadistic military officers, willfully "poetic" ambiguity - yet never earns their presence or explores them with any depth.By the time it reaches its cold, shrug-of-the-shoulders non-ending, the result is 160 minutes of exquisite images wrapped around nothing at all. Pretentious, empty, and smugly self-satisfied, the film is especially infuriating because of the vast gap between its potential and its long, meager delivery.
author: thomasvconti

title: A Stunning Period Piece Held Back by Its Story
text: This film tells its story out of chronological order, and while the structure isn't too hard to follow, it doesn't create the smoothest or most natural flow. The constant shifts in time can feel a little distracting, and some viewers might find themselves less absorbed because of it.The pacing also gets in the way. The movie starts off very slowly, almost to the point where it's hard to stay engaged, and it takes a long time before anything really starts happening. Even when the story finally gains some momentum, it never becomes especially emotional. It remains simple and grounded, which can work well for a period drama, especially one that aims for realism. And to be fair, the film does a great job capturing the look and feel of the time period with detailed, believable imagery.Still, as good as the visuals are, the story itself feels pretty thin. There isn't a lot of depth, and it's hard to feel truly connected to what's going on. Because of that, the movie ends up being more impressive to look at than enjoyable to watch.One creative choice that doesn't land very well is using the same actor to play both the father and, years later, the son. It's a bold idea, but it comes across as a bit cliché and doesn't really add anything meaningful to the story. If anything, it pulls you out of the experience.Overall, this period drama is well-made and visually strong, but its pacing, plot, and character choices leave plenty of room for improvement.
author: minimaster-67734

title: A bold, texturized ode to memory
text: Not so easy to categorize this movie. Even though mystery threads through the entire story, The Secret Agent expands far beyond genre. Comedy, folklore, political critique, historical reconstruction and intimate drama crash into one another with intention, giving the film a bold, high-energy identity that refuses to sit still. Kleber Mendonça Filho crafts a deeply personal narrative rooted in Recife, each chapter functioning like a self-contained regional myth filled with allegories, tension and cultural memory.Wagner Moura is outstanding as Marcelo, a tech professor who returns to his hometown while running from a violent past in São Paulo. As he tries to protect his young son Fernando, he finds refuge with Dona Sebastiana, played with luminous warmth by Tânia Maria. She becomes the film's emotional anchor, embodying the maternal, welcoming force that shelters the marginalized and turns pain into community.The film doesn't avoid Brazil's open wounds. A chapter referencing the death of a child in 2020 and another confronting blatant academic xenophobia reveal how Mendonça Filho uses these stories not as exposition, but as lived reminders of what the country still refuses to face. Marcelo's search for any document that proves his mother existed becomes a metaphor for a broader national amnesia: the memories erased, the histories neglected, the identities lost under the weight of time and authoritarianism.You'll frustrate yourself expecting a conventional thriller or a spy narrative. This is something else entirely: a film that goes full throttle in its ideas, trusting its striking cinematography and daring screenplay to shape an experience far from formula, the work of a director at his most confident.An ode to memory: the ones we chase, the ones we distort, and the ones that disappear if no one insists on remembering. When Fernando admits he no longer remembers his father, the film quietly lands its message: Marcelo is a Secret Agent because he was forgotten, and forgetting isn't just personal, it's national.
author: teufreitas

title: A Feijoada on Surviving Violence
text: Despite its many scenes of violence - served in a myriad of flavors through the characters' interactions - The Secret Agent remains a powerful and refreshing tale about Brazil. Even though the long shadow of the dictatorship may linger overhead, the film itself is not concerned with offering, nor pretending to offer, any deep political critique. It is a light, absorbing, and savory story about the nation's people, rich enough to remind Brazilian viewers of all the spices, sauces, and recipes that have shaped - and still shape - its society.For those who have never visited Brazil or befriended a Brazilian, the pace at which scenes shift and tensions dissolve may feel, at first, strange or slow to digest. But once you locate yourself within this surreal experience, you may realize you're standing before a mirror - one in which you recognize yourself in the characters who struck you the most and will soon miss as the movie goes by.Much like violence in Brazil, climaxes that are patiently built simply dissipate into the surreal haze of what living in Brazil is. The resolutions of these tensions, though carefully constructed, are neither moving nor shocking nor thrilling. Mendonça seems to do this deliberately, and that is perhaps the film's only deep message: what should move or shock you most is sacrificed, almost nullified, in favor of a smooth yet desensitized belief that life must go on - that it's just another Thursday.The final scenes, little more than a sub-subplot, showcase this once more for the viewer who might have missed the message. Without those, one must be sensitive enough to detect the kind of insensitivity that survival quietly demands.
author: DanielA-9432

title: A Beautifully Haunting Movie
text: A beautifully haunting movie. A patchwork of local culture and compelling characters, full of vibrant colors and music. It brings so much joy and pain all at once! It skillfully does what movies exist to do - remind us of life and death, of our own culture and history!At first, the ending confused me. I wanted to know more, I wanted to see what happened next! But little by little I understood it. We don't want to see what happened, not really. Society doesn't want to be reminded of it. In reality, these cases are forgotten pieces of our history. And this is why movies like this should exist."The nineteenth century dislike of realism is the rage of Caliban seeing his own face in a glass." - Oscar Wilde.
author: goddess21112

title: Other Great (Surreal) Movie by Kleber Mendonça Fiho
text: Kleber Mendonça's fifth film is sensational! With this work, he establishes himself as one of the great directors of world cinema. In this work, he addresses the tragedy of the military dictatorship in Brazil in an allegorical, acidic, raw, and often comical way. The performances are the film's strong point, with Wagner Moura giving a magnetic performance. The editing and cinematography are also strong points. It is very reminiscent of films by Tarantino and the Coen brothers. In short, it is a work to be remembered.
author: binhosan

title: So good
text: Kleber making history again, a film rich in analogies, based on the past to portray the present. A film full of irony, playing with the very seriousness of the unjust situation that Armando is experiencing. I fell in love with the supporting actors, especially Dona Sebastiana, a character so real that I find myself wanting to know every stage of her life. I have nothing more to say about the film, it explains itself. The ending is literally reality, without adding or taking away anything. I am proud to witness this spectacle, the same feeling I had with "I'm Still Here," it's not a film made to reach first place in awards, but made for us Brazilians and necessary so that we don't forget this period of our history.
author: wanessasantana

title: Brazil, 1970s
text: The title is as misleading as it is abstract. Or rather, 'Secret Agent' is not in itself an abstract phrase. It could refer, for example, to James Bond. But the relationship between this title and the content of this film is indeed abstract. "Passive Agent" would be a better title, meaning (in grammar) "complement of efficient cause." Lacking a plot, why so many subplots? Why a redundant and expletive epilogue? On the plus side, "The Secret Agent" is an interesting allegory about official archives, silencing, and contested memory. The film highlights the important role of digging, connecting the dots, reading "against the grain," and questioning official discourses in order to uncover suppressed narratives.
author: jgcorrea

title: A great film with an underwhelming third act...
text: "The Secret Agent" is yet another proof of how great Brazilian cinema is. It's very comparable to I'm Still Here in terms of themes and filmmaking, but it's much more violent. The film tells an intense, heavy story set during a period of Brazilian history rich enough to spawn countless narratives. And despite its long runtime, it's always magnetic and gripping thanks to its incredible direction, cinematography, and-above everything else-the performances.The cast, led by the legendary Wagner Moura (with a special shout-out to Tânia Maria), deliver Oscar-worthy, unforgettable work. There are even a few funny or lighthearted scenes sprinkled in to lift the mood, which I really appreciated.On the other hand, the third act felt a bit underwhelming. The choice to use the same actor to play a different character (something I usually hate) felt unnecessary, and several loose ends were left hanging. You can fill them in with your own imagination, but with such a long runtime, those threads and the entire story deserved to be completed on screen.
